We visited the mountains on the rainy day, not much to see, good hike though.
Ticket for the elevator is 55k per person, and parking for motorbike at the nearby shop for 10k
Attraction touristique rien d'extraordinaire au bord de route encombrée.
宗教觀光著名地點，樓梯斗峭對老年人可能很大負擔，有電梯上下但是要買門票，一般風景無奇特，就走走看看，景區大量特產店，編織包非常多，建議跟團的可以在這裡買，種類數量多，價格要大砍很坑爹，小包大約抓80-120台，會安古鎮很屌不太給砍的，喜歡就在這站砍，量多件會更好議。

Overall it js okay. There is a separate entrance ticket charging 20k vnd for a smaller cave and then elevator and to the top another 55k vnd per person. And a separate 15k vnd for going down the elevator which was absolutely weird.
There are multiple levels and you can decide what you want to explore.
The heavens gate is very high and the stairs can be slippery and its rocky at the top so wear comfy shoes. Rest is all okay and very commercial
We reached on a weekday by 9am so we got less rush but in an hour it was full

The mountain and it’s many caves and pagodas are simply breathtaking. I don’t think anyone in their right mind could not be impressed. This is without a doubt a must see attraction if you’re in the local area. The whole experience was slightly tainted however by the encounter we had with a select number of pushy, borderline obsessive shopkeepers outside the main entrance. Myself and partner travelled to the mountain on a hired scooter and as we were travelling on the main highway just a few turnings off from the mountains main entrance we were hounded by an individual who decided to ride next to us and shout for us to follow her for somewhere we could park for free. We explained on multiple occasions that we were not interested and wanted to find our own place to park and she refused to stop following us and stop shouting at us. It was that bad we continued on the highway in order to lose her as we were uncertain of her intentions. As we circled back on ourselves, low and behold she manages to find us again and repeat the process. She was clearly doing loops trying to lure people in. After taking a shortcut and managing to shake her off, we were then greeted at the entrance by a number of shopkeepers shouting at us trying to persuade us to park outside their shops for free. By this point myself and partner felt a bit intimated and succumbed to parking outside a shop for ‘free’. It turns out that the woman who had hounded us worked at the shop in question and we were expected to buy something from the shop in return. We bought the cheapest item we could just to pacify her. This isn’t so much an issue as we were happy to pay to park regardless, and the parking was extremely close but we just didn’t appreciate the way the whole thing was approached. The only other drawback was just how busy the place was. It was absolutely rammed. We arrived around 3pm so I’d advise going early or as late as possible. That being said, we had a cracking experience overall, dragged down only by the experience we had with the pushy shopkeepers. Had it not been for this, it would have easily been a 5 star experience.

평지 한가운데 솟아오른 바위산 위에 있는 불교 사원.
약 4개의 크고작은 동굴에 불상들이 있고, 큰 사원이 있어 독특한 불교사원이 인상적이다.
다만 바위산에 둘러싸여 있는 동굴은 한국의 동굴과는 다르게 무지 습하고 더워 더위를 싫어하시는 분은 힘들수도 있으니 참고하시길.
입장료는 2000천원, 올라갈때 엘베 1000원
내려올땐 걸어내려가면 금방입니다

あくまでも、自身の主観であるが、感銘を受けるほどのものは無かった…美化され過ぎの感がある。

１．多数の観光客でかなり混雑している
２．弱風が引き抜けるため、市街地より若干は涼しい
３．洞窟内は、冷気を感ずるほどのものは無く湿度が高い
４．名刹伽藍の様な荘厳さは感じられない
５．洞穴に石像（佛像）が安置されている
６．一部の洞穴に淡い太陽光が差し込んでいる
７．平地にある小山（筑波山のスケールを小さくした様な）
８．岩肌に大理石を想わせる様なものは少ない
９．階段（１段のステップがやや高い）の数や規模は、それほどでもないが、何しろ歩き続けると暑苦しい（ダナンの何処も同じであるが）

かつては信仰の地であったであろうが、商業化され、その面影も感じられない。一部の敬虔な仏教徒が手を合わせる姿が見られた。
個人的印象であるが、ダナン（及び周辺）と言う地は、ビーチ以外の観光資源の資質が高いとは言い難い…そのため、当所がクローズアップされるのであろう。
個人的には、仮に訪れなかったとしても、後悔するほどのものではなかった。
ダナンに『10泊』していたが、再訪する気にはならなかった。

Don't take the lift I think it's more worth for older folks who can't climb. Use the stairs there are two entrance fees one for elevator and the other is entrance 🤷 it's good place to visit but don't go by the hype. If you go to this place by bike there is no parking lot and you have to pay and park before shops.

Be aware of the price you’re paying as map is included (55k) when you can just take a picture of it from the sign :) the site itself is great, lots to see. Some terrains inside the cave are slippery without any handrails or steps. Use good walking shoes and be careful. Some vendors selling coconut, pop, and water.

Un po' complicato da fare a causa della mancanza di indicazioni chiare perché non sai mai se andare a destra, a sinistra, su o giù. Ho pure acquistato la mappa ma se non sai il vietnamita quello che vedi scritto nelle varie grotte non corrisponde a quello scritto sulla mappa.   La grotta vicino alla biglietteria del gate A, quella a sinistra dell'ascensore per capirci, non è l'entrata principale come ci ha detto la cassiera, ma è la Am phu Cave. Non ha uscite perciò non cercate inutilmente un passaggio per andare avanti. Una volta vista la Am phu Cave uscite fuori all' aperto, girate a sinistra e proseguite fino a dopo l'ascensore e li troverete la biglietteria per l'ascensore ed il vero percorso. La grotta più grande, la Huyen Khong Cave ci ha un po' deluso, forse perché si era creata un aspettativa troppo grande. È comunque una visita che rifarei anche grazie al costo irrisorio.

A tranquil place to visit despite it being touristy although it is a little disconcerting to see how commercialized the site is especially for a functioning Buddhist temple. Vendors outside are way overpriced and they lure you by being friendly, so get souvenirs elsewhere. Even Hoi An is much cheaper than them.

(En are followers)
굉장히 험하거나 하진 않지만 그래도 운동화 신고 가시면 훨씬 안전할거 같아요 소요시간은 대략 2시간 정도 였으며 7월달이라 매우 습하고 모기가 많으니 긴팔 긴바지나 기피제를 뿌리면 도움이 될 듯! 물은 다행히 어디서든 판매해서 쉽게 구할 수 있으니 무겁게 들고다니지 마세요!

Its was nice but can’t say incredible if you stay here you can go but there is not must place.. it was nice to see this area on the peak and the caves are kind of cool. We visited here on July / arrived 09:30 but after one hour start too sweat and sticky and there are lots mosquito please check that we took 2 hours chose kind of big cave and peak
The cave is nice. It's a bit of a hike but doable. Don't buy souvenirs from the vendors there, it's a ripped off.

I found the experience somewhat underwhelming. While the caves were undoubtedly beautiful, I didn't ascend to the top for panoramic views.

Keep in mind that the area experiences constant dripping, so it's best prepared for damp conditions. Additionally, I should caution that the staff encountered were generally unhelpful or rude in response to questions.
정상부 동굴안으로 비치는 빛은 성령이 임하는 듯한 오묘한 분위기가 있지만 더운날씨에 둘러보기엔 만만치 않음. 동굴이라 해도 내부까지 너무 덥고 대리석 계단도 미끄러워 조심히 다녀와야함. 엘리베이터가 생각많큼 긴 것은 아님.

出發去峴港最期待的景點，可是全部連陰府洞都走過覺得這裡台灣人應該不會喜歡。地獄洞很空虛，認真解釋地獄的程度輸給高雄蓮池潭⋯最期待的玄空洞可能因下午光線未能從上方洞口射進來導致洞穴內昏暗不明少了莊嚴的氛圍。路線指標不確實，跟著遊客走才發現大家都走錯路。下山的階梯非常陡梯階又大，不是那種好爬的階梯，年長者不建議前往，即便搭電梯洞穴內也有很多階梯且非常昏暗。
唯一印象深刻的地方是relaxation area，靜坐於此真能感受到前所未有的放鬆⋯磁場很好，有到訪的人可以試試在此休息片刻。

베트남의 습하고 더운 날씨에 더불어 등산코스가 있기에 엑티비티를 좋아하는 분들이면 추천합니다.
대신 불쾌지수가 금방 높아질 수 있다는 점 명심해야할 듯 합니다.
Highest place? 는 극강의 오르막길이나 올라오면 꽤나 괜찮은 풍경을 볼 수 있습니다!
Depending on if you take the elevator the wait time is up to 20 min. Crowded. Not that great. Some things okay but otherwise boring and time wasting.
I feel like this place is nice. Lots of walking if you're up for it. There is also a lift but they charge you extra.

There are 3 ticketing booths. Thus, three options for entrance. Either you take stairs going the mountain, or you enter a big cave first as its entrance and lastly, you can enter thru an elevator so you dont need to climb up the mountain.

* Dangerous
( slippery, rugged, cactuses, etc… )

Beatiful though..
I think it is exactly 3.5 stars but no option for half so i will give 3 stars for this sight.

Make sure if you are okay for hiking, walking in the heat and sweating.

ㄹㅇ 위험함... 노약자ㄴ 땀흘리는거싫어하면ㄴ 모자필수 바위 미끄럽고 선인장가시 찔릴뻔하고 험난했어요.
산 나무 돌이나 불교틱한거 좋아하면 가보세요. 동굴이 정말 멋집니다. 날이 더워서 갈지말지 고민된다면 가지마십쇼.

Billet d'entrée de la montagne de Marbre: 40k

Ne suivez pas les locaux qui crient en vous voyant et essaient de vous embarquer pour un parking payant (scooter) devant chez eux, vous pouvez vous garer gratuitement dans la rue en continuant un peu.

Sympa mais pas transcendant. Beaucoup de monde, et points de vue franchement pas incroyables. Mais ça passe le temps.
나름 둘러보기 괜찮지만 한번 정도는 가볼만한 곳. 그냥 간단히 구경하기 좋고 나름 약간 등산아닌 등산을 하는터라 운동도 되고 괜찮은 코스임. 동굴이 우리나라처럼 막 시원하고 그러진않음. 중간중간에 음료수 파는 곳도 있고 그냥 소소함
Very limited accessibility. There’s only one elevator. The steps are uneven and slippery. The temples are interesting but if I had to choose once again, I’d skip Marble Mountains.

Not amazing but okay. The biggest let down are all the tourists who push and have really bad etiquette.  Felt sorry for the monks everyone coming and ruining thier tranquillity taking over staged selfies

Orientace v areálu příšerná, mapu kterou dostanete/ si koupíte, můžete hned zahodit. Dotěrní prodejci v areálu, nesystémové rozdělení/ směr prohlídky.
Although they placed a lift to go up but you have to climb lot of stairs to reach the caves and spots even after the lift.
It’s really physical demanding. A lot of steps, a lot of staircase climbing, but of course a lot of Buddha too. Better be prepared with super comfy shoes ya.

有點無聊……..

There were numerous coaches packed with local and foreign tourists visiting marble mountain which is a popular sightseeing destination.

There is a lift for visitors to go up to the limestones hilltop at a nominal fee to get a glimpse of the shrines and religious artifacts but the panoramic view of Danang coastal lines was worth it..

Going down by the cobbled and uneven stone steps was a challenge for the elderly who chose not to pay for the ride down.

Beside the trappings of a mechanical lift going up the hilltop which is a mini version of the Sydney's Blue Mountains elevator, the attraction is overrated.

They were many visitors idling or checking out stalls peddling souvenirs, clothes, food and drinks at the base of the hill. We came here through the purchase of a 5 day 4 night Danang holiday package from our Malaysian travel agency who made all the accommodations, sightseeings, travel and food arrangements for us.
There is not really much marble to see at all. There are a lot of statues and caves. You can get some really great photos but come prepared for very humid conditions.
去可以買票搭電梯，下來走樓梯，景點主要就是山洞內的各種佛像以及景觀
Marble Mountains is beautiful but has many stairs and steep paths. Best to wear sport attire and comfortable shoes so you can climb easily. Bring water and a hat if visiting during the day.

When we were there it's quite hot. But good for a short hiking up to top of those mountains & discover some caves. The surrounding communities also nice with traditional job as Rock carving. Nice statutes with different colors.

Pretty disappointing for me. Price was very low so that was good. Caves were ok but not spectacular. We went as we had time and it was convenient, on this basis worth a visit but not recommended on FOMO only.

We came here 12 years ago and then again recently. Not much had changed at all. It's a nice ride from Hoi An on the scooter. The climb up to the summit is relatively easy if you do a bit of hiking. The place has some nice pagodas and caves to view. The view from the top is also worth seeing. We went to a couple of the larger marble shops at the bottom on the highway. The staff there were unfortunately very unfriendly so we left quickly. There was a small entry fee for the mountain and we also had to pay a local shop owner to leave our scooters. They do not let you park the scooters in the bus carpark at the mountain.

A local tourist attraction at the southern end of Da Nang on the way to Hoi An.  If fit you can walk to the top or if unfit like me, you can catch the lift.  Lovely pergodas and temples and many caves converted into shrines.  You will see local monks doing their day to day business and there are little stores selling drinks, ice creams and curios.  There are still many steep steps to traverse both up and down so you need to be fit and agile.  The steps are also uneven and there are no handrails.  Will take about 3 hours to see the whole mountain

It is worth to come by here, but in my eyes not a must see.

Some cool caves and nice views, but the area is filled with overly aggressive sellers for fldeinks and souvenigers. Each vendor is aggressively fighting for your attention which takes away from what could be a very serene and contemplative area.
If you have been to other asian countries you will not find this fascinating, it’s beautiful but not wow. The steps are steep and slippery, needs a certain level of fitness and to wear proper shoes.

A good place to visit. Entree fee is cheap, 40k VND. The hike up isn't bad. The view at "Cổng trời" is nice. You should spend around 2-3 hour at this place. There are 2 places for you to choose. Up to "Heaven" or down to "Hell" 🤣. Also, many shops for souvenir, like gém and stones. A quite nice experience.

Could be a nice place to visit if it wasn't for all the garbage on the sides. Going down from the highest peak, where some people were smoking and throwing cigarettes on the floor, we actually saw someone dumping a full bag of smelly plastic bottles in a hole. We could not believe it and I was ready to tell him off but he seemed local so did not feel it was my place to do so... To local authorities, you should really take better care of this place. Fines should probably be imposed on the ones who do not care about your - otherwise pretty - marble mountain.

Sehr schöner Kalksteinberg mit 1-2 tollen Höhlen und einem halbwegsguten Viewpoint. Kurzer aber steiler Aufstieg!

Leider wird man konsequent mit Ticketkauf und Souvenirs belästigt, die Service Mitarbeiter waren extrem schlecht gelaunt.

Là một trong những nơi nên ghé thăm ở Đà Nẵng. Mình là lần thứ hai ghé nơi đây. Giá vé cũng rẻ nên đi sớm nếu bạn muốn đi thang máy, vì xếp hàng đi thang máy rất lâu. Leo lên trên bạn có thể ngắm cảnh từ trên cao. Bạn có thể thư thái thăm quan núi ngũ hành. Ngoài ra có thể thăm Động địa ngục ngay cạnh quầy bán vé. Động rất mát mẻ, từ động bạn có thể leo lên cổng trời.

Ngũ Hành Sơn là quần thể gồm năm ngọn núi đá vôi nằm ven biển Đà Nẵng, nổi tiếng với vẻ đẹp hùng vĩ và huyền bí. Mỗi ngọn núi mang tên theo ngũ hành: Kim, Mộc, Thủy, Hỏa, Thổ – tượng trưng cho sự hài hòa của đất trời. Bên trong có nhiều hang động, chùa chiền cổ kính như chùa Linh Ứng, động Huyền Không mang đậm dấu ấn tâm linh. Từ đỉnh núi, du khách có thể ngắm toàn cảnh thành phố và bãi biển Non Nước tuyệt đẹp. Không khí trong lành, mát mẻ quanh năm, rất thích hợp cho hành trình khám phá và chiêm bái. Đây là điểm đến không thể bỏ lỡ khi ghé Đà Nẵng.
Trời dịu mát thì đi thích lắm. Mát vô cùng. Khoan khoái hít bầu không khí sạch.

Đẹp và kì vĩ, hang động rất lớn, nhưng leo rất mệt nhen. Ng lớn tuổi và gđ đưa theo trẻ nên cân nhắc khi leo. Đc cái chi phí rẻ, Vé động âm phủ chỉ 20k. Vé lên chùa linh ứng ngũ hành sơn là 40k/ng, thang máy khứ hồi 30k/ng.
Mình đi trời mưa, khổ cực, trơn trượt. Nhưng sẽ hợp ai thích trecking, nó hoang sơ mà nó mệt.

SỨC KHOẺ KÉM LÀM ƠN ĐI THANG MÁY vì Chùa khá cao và dốc, nhiều khu nên việc đi lại khá mất sức. Đông khách du lịch. Khu địa ngục phải mua vé riêng, nên đi sớm lúc đông người chứ vào muộn cảm giác rất âm u!!
Nắng nóng nên đi sáng sớm ạ, lên Cổng Trời có ngắm trọn vẹn cảnh phía dưới và hơi khó đi, động Huyền Không là động đẹp và rộng mát nên đến ạ

Giá vé tham quan hiện tại là 40.000/khách.
Chưa bao gồm vé thang máy.

Một danh thắng tại Đà Nẵng mà du khách thường đến tham quan.

Có nhiều giá trị tại Ngũ Hành Sơn như:

Dấu tích chiến đấu chống Pháp và Mỹ của quân dân Quảng Nam - Đà Nẵng.

Tên gọi Ngũ Hành Sơn hình dung đơn giản địa danh được đặt tên theo phương vị của Ngũ Hành (Kim Mộc Thủy Hỏa Thổ).

Ngũ Hành Sơn được các triều vua viếng thăm, tu bổ, tôn tạo từ giữa thế kỷ XVII, XIX, XX. Tương truyền vua Lê Thánh Tông là người khám phá ra NHS. Đến thời nhà Nguyễn, công chúa Ngọc Lan con gái của vua Gia Long đến ẩn tu tại “Phổ Đà Sơn”.

Đặc biệt, vua Minh Mạng đặt lại danh xưng cho Ngũ Hành Sơn, khắc tên trên thềm và sườn núi. Năm 1825 vua còn ban lệnh tu sửa chùa Tam Thai.

Về dấu tích con người ở từ đầu thế kỷ XIV. Vị trí của Ngũ Hành Sơn vốn là lãnh địa Amaravati của Chiêm Thành, trải qua quá trình lịch sử của dân tộc, người Việt đã dần tiến về phương Nam, vì vậy đầu thế kỷ XIV, dấu chân người Việt đến NHS.

NHS hiện có nhiều chùa, am, tháp, vọng, miếu thờ, cổ vật, bút tích quý, … đặc biệt nhiều ngôi chùa được phong quốc tự (Tam Thai, Linh Ứng) và từng có quốc sư trụ trì.

Nếu bạn đang tìm một nơi để khám phá thiên nhiên ngay giữa lòng thành phố Đà Nẵng, Ngũ Hành Sơn sẽ là một lựa chọn đáng cân nhắc. Tuy nhiên, hãy chuẩn bị tâm lý vì nơi đây khá đông khách du lịch, đặc biệt là khách nước ngoài.

Giá vé vào cổng là 40.000 VNĐ, và nếu bạn muốn tiết kiệm sức lực thì có thể sử dụng thang máy với giá 30.000 VNĐ cho cả lượt lên và xuống. Đi thang máy sẽ tiện lợi hơn rất nhiều, vì để leo lên núi đá khá cao cũng mất kha khá sức.

Về cảnh quan, nơi đây có một vài hang động tự nhiên nằm rải rác trong núi, tạo cảm giác mát mẻ và kỳ bí. Một số hang khá rộng và có ánh sáng tự nhiên lọt vào rất đẹp. Bên cạnh đó cũng có vài ngôi chùa cổ, nhưng xét về quy mô và kiến trúc thì không quá đặc sắc như những nơi tâm linh nổi tiếng khác – chủ yếu vẫn giữ nét nguyên sơ và mộc mạc.

Điều đặc biệt nhất của Ngũ Hành Sơn có lẽ là việc tồn tại một "ốc đảo thiên nhiên" giữa lòng thành phố hiện đại, mang lại cảm giác vừa bình yên vừa lạ lẫm cho du khách.

Địa điểm khá đông khách du lịch. Không khí mát mẻ.
Có thể mua vé vào tham quan bên trong động huyền không hoặc leo núi, đi thang máy lên trên đỉnh.
Còn không thì có thể đi dạo xung quanh, ngắm nhìn và mua sắm một số mặt hàng lưu niệm về làm quà tặng. Với giá cả khá ổn áp.

Ngũ Hành Sơn là một danh thắng gồm 6 ngọn núi đá vôi nhô lên trên một bãi cát ven biển, trên một diện tích khoảng 2 km² nằm cách trung tâm thành phố Đà Nẵng, Việt Nam khoảng 8 km về phía Đông Nam, ngay trên tuyến đường Đà Nẵng - Hội An; nay thuộc phường Hòa Hải, quận Ngũ Hành Sơn, thành phố Đà Nẵng. Nơi đây khá đẹp nhưng bị tác động của con người quá nhiều hàng quán khá bề bộn làm giảm nhãn quan. Vé bán vô động riêng, lên đỉnh riêng thang máy riêng rất bất tiện, hang được tác động cắt gọn mở rộng nên mất hết nét tự nhiên
Điểm đáng đến trong đời , khá đẹp ! Đi hết 3 hang động là sức khoẻ rất tốt ! Tuy nhiên thu vé đi thang máy và hướng dẫn không đầy đủ là điểm đáng trách nhất !

Quá đẹp và hoành tráng. Tới đây mua vé bạn có thể lựa chọn đi thang bộ khám phá hoặc đi thang máy. Mình chọn đi bộ để ngắm cảnh. Nhiều view để chụp hình lắm luôn, góc nào cũng xinh, đi mỏi cả chân và 1 đống hình để up. Rất xứng đáng.
Hình như thang máy chỉ có 1 khu vực, đi hết phải vòng lại, đồ bán khá mắc, vé thang máy chỉ có 1 chiều + vé tham quan, cảnh quan thì bình thường, chủ yếu là động và chùa
Một địa điểm du lịch ngay trung tâm tp Đà Nẵng. 90% các bạn sẽ leo bật thang, nên các cô bác lớn tuổi nên cân nhắc. Trong hang thờ nhiều Phật. Sau khi chinh phục tất cả thì cảm giác rất tuyệt.
Đây là điểm du lịch tâm linh với rất nhiều hang động đẹp

Phong cảnh trong động đẹp và mát mẻ. Tuy nhiên đường lên sẽ hơi nắng và đông đúc vào giờ cao điểm.
Có thang máy lên xuống đỉnh núi, giá vé tham quan 15k và vé tháng máy khứ hồi 40k

Quần thể động Thủy rất là hoành tráng, có chỗ còn nguyên vẹn từ thế kỉ 3 khá là tuyệt. Tuy nhiên view biển bị khách sạn nào đó chắn khá nhiều. Sư cô sư thầy staff ở đó rất dễ thương nhiệt tình. Chỗ đi dốc có thể mua vé cho thang máy.

- Khu du lịch gần thành phố (cách trung tâm khoảng ~8KM).
- Vé đi thang máy là 40K /người, (vé riêng vào động huyền không là 20k/người).
- Nếu sức khoẻ tốt thì có thể đi thang bộ ở bên phải gần các shop bán đồ sẽ không tốn tiền.
- Khu vực có khá nhiều hang động và một ngôi chùa ở trung tâm.
- Đường lên đỉnh (trong đây gọi là cổng trời) không có bậc thang mà là đá nên mọi người cẩn thận, cũng chỉ là đoạn ngắn không quá khó.
- Về cơ bản cũng bình thường không gì đặc biệt, nên đi trải nghiệm một lần cho biết.

Mình đi vào thứ 4 nên khách không đông lắm. Phí tham quan là 40k/người, phí đi thang máy là 15k/ lượt. Bạn chỉ cần mua 1 lượt thang máy lên. Lúc xuống thì đi qua cổng số 1 để xuống. Bạn nên mua nước uống ở Winmart đối diện xéo với lối vào cổng số 1. Đừng lên trên núi mua, 1 nước suối bán ở đó 20k.
Cảnh quan còn khá là tự nhiên nhưng thấy nhiều chỗ để hòm công đức quá, điều này không hay cho lắm. 17 năm rồi mình quay lại có nhiều thay đổi nhưng điều này không làm thay đổi cảnh sắc nơi đây

Ngũ Hành Sơn nằm cách trung tâm Đà Nẵng khoảng 8 km về phía Đông Nam, trên một bãi cát mênh mông gần bờ biển, thuộc làng Hoà Khuê, ấp Sơn Thủy, huyện Hòa Vang nay thuộc phường Hòa Hải, quận Ngũ Hành Sơn.

Nằm trên trục đường đi từ Đà Nẵng đến Hội An, Ngũ Hành Sơn là 1 thắng cảnh đẹp, tiện cho du khách muốn tham quan kết hợp chuyến đi Hội An.

Phong cảnh trên núi thực sự đẹp và đáng ghé thăm. Tuy nhiên, cần phải có sức khoẻ tốt để đi được hết các hang động trên núi. Lưu ý, khi tham quan mang đồ gọn nhẹ, đi giày thể thao để dễ leo vào các hang động, đem theo nước uống (trên núi đồ uống khá đắt).

Ngũ hành sơn hay còn gọi là Núi Non Nước. Ngũ là năm, năm ngọn núi đặt tên theo ngũ hành: Mộc, Thủy, Thổ, Kim, Hỏa(Dương và Âm). Hầu như tất cả những du khách đều ghé thăm ngọn Thủy Sơn vì lớn cao đẹp nhất.
Thủy sơn có chùa Tam Thai, chùa Linh Ứng,...với nhiều hang động, Vọng cảnh,..
Bạn có thể chọn leo bộ hoặc đi thang máy. Đã quyết tâm leo bộ thì mang ít đồ đạc nhất có thể nha, đường lên cao khá dốc, có chỗ dốc dựng đứng nên sẽ  tốn nhiều sức lực hơn bạn nghĩ. Tuy nhiên nếu bạn đi theo đường mòn thì cây cối khá nhiều, nên việc chinh phục núi để thưởng thức vẻ đẹp hữu tình sẽ giảm áp lực phần nào.

Ngũ hành Sơn là nơi di tích thắng cảnh có nhiều hang động, huyền bí,du lịch ngũ hành Sơn có khung cảnh rừng cây thiên nhiên, và vách đá dựng đứng, cùng những phien đá màu sắc, cùng với hoa cỏ ngũ sắc khoe hương, với dòng sông mát nên thơ,trong lành, và đài vọng cảnh,ngon thủy sơn ngắm nhìn cả vùng biển trời lồng lộng trên cao, cùng với những ngôi chùa nằm trên núi,trang nghiêm ,thanh tịnh, mát mẻ, là nơi các bạn nên đến tham quan, trải nghiệm thú vị

Ngọn Thủy là cao nhất, có 2 cổng vào, 1 trong 2 cổng có thang máy lên chùa (được khoảng nửa đường lên đỉnh núi), có chỗ giữ xe ôtô, nếu đi xe máy phải gửi nhà dân. Giá vé vào cổng là 40k (thanng máy thêm 15k) để tham quan các ngôi chùa trên núi (3 hay 4 gì đó) và 2 hang động, 2 đỉnh núi trong đó cao nhất là cửa trời.
Đi hơi mệt nhưng rất đẹp. Một trải nghiệm tuyệt vời
Hợp cho ai thích hang núi thiên nhiên.
Điểm mình ghé 6/2019 giá vé tham quan là 40 vé thang máy 15 một chiều lên đó thì cũng khá rộng chịu leo chịu đi cũng mỏi chân. Có bảng giá rỏ ràng, là điểm du lịch duy nhất giữ xe của mình 10k :))

Tuyệt vời ông mặt trời! Đi vào núi rồi thì mới thấy thiên nhiên hùng vĩ thế nào và bàn tay tạo tác của con người kì diệu ra sao. Trong núi có rất nhiều chùa, trong các hang động cũng điêu khắc rất nhiều tượng phật, các vị thánh, đặc biệt là Động Âm Phủ mô phỏng khá chân thực 18 tầng địa ngục. Cảnh quan rất đẹp, những kiến trúc của con người ở đây cũng phản ánh rất rõ nét tín ngưỡng Phật giáo. Tuy nhiên, khuyến cáo mọi người nên đi giày thể thao vì leo núi khá mệt và nguy hiểm, người sức khỏe không tốt nên dùng thang máy đi lên rồi đi bộ xuống để tham quan, và tốt nhất, nên đem theo đồ ăn nhẹ và nước uống của mình vì các gian hàng ở đây bán rất rất đắt!
Lâu lâu test thể lực cũng tạm ổn . Địa điểm nên ghé khi bạn đặt chân đến Đà Nẵng
Cũng ok

Hình ảnh thì mình không có chụp, nhưng mà mình xin review cảm nhận của mình nè. Các bạn có sức khỏe tốt thì leo lên ngọn núi luôn nha, ngắm thành phố oách xà lách luôn. Còn không thì đi thang máy giá 15k/ lượt nè. Vé vào cổng đối với sinh viên chỉ 10k hoy :))) nên các bạn nào là sinh viên chú ý mang theo để nhận ưu đãi nhé. Leo hơi mệt 1 tí, mà đã, cảm giác sợ té lắm á, mấy b sợ độ cao thì bò lên cho chắc nha :))) mình toàn bò không à :))) cảnh vật ở đây tạm ổn, mà nước uống quà lưu niệm giá hơi chát nha các bạn hihi
View chưa đẹp lắm, so với giá vé thì hợp lí, ae nên leo bộ trải nghiệm, leo tầm 10p là đến chứ ko mệt như mấy ông bán vé kêu :))
Đẹp,rất nên đến.
Yên bình
Cảnh không có gì đặc sắc lắm. Nếu có cơ hội vẫn nên đi 1 lần để trải nghiệm. Leo dốc nhiều nên đi giày thể thao hoặc sandal đế bằng. Cây cối nhiều, mát mẻ ...
Ngôi chùa và một làng nghề...

Nơi dành cho những người yêu thiên nhiên, thích vận động, khám phá những địa điểm nổi tiếng tại Đà Nẵng. Giá vé 40k/người, 55k cho lượt đi thang máy. Tại đây cũng có bán một số quà lưu niệm cho du khách. Ai không quen đi bộ, leo dốc sẽ rất mệt

Vị trí đẹp, động huyền không là đẹp nhất. Hang âm phủ nhân tạo nhiều. Mua đá quý ở đây là hợp lý, nên hỏi người hướng dẫn bản địa để dc giá tốt. Khách du lịch họ quát giá nhiều lắm. Người Đà nẵng rất tốt tính...
Rất đẹp. Phù hợp cho mọi lứa tuổi :3

Ngọn Thủy sơn - Núi Ngũ Hành Sơn. Đây là nơi có các danh thắng như chùa Linh Ứng, Động Vân Thông, Động Huyền Không, Vọng Hải Đài.
Giá tham quan là 40000đ/người. Vé thang máy là 15000đ/người/lượt.
Quá đẹp
Những người thợ lành nghề sẽ làm khuất phục những vị khách khó tính nhất
Hang động, chùa chiền, bi ký... cùng với những huyền tích mang màu sắc Phật giáo làm cho nơi này có một sắc thái rất riêng, không lẫn vào đâu được so với các danh lam thắng cảnh trên cả nước.
Địa điểm du lịch nổi tiếng của Đà Nẵng, nơi tập trung làng nghề đá Non Nước, có chùa Linh Ứng, nhiều hang động đẹp nếu bạn muốn khám phá và mạo hiểm.
Lần đầu tiên mình leo núi và đi vào động. Động cực kì đẹp và mát luôn íii. Nhưng mà trời nóng với lại mình không đủ sức khỏe để đi bộ hết động âm phủ và lên cổng trời nên hơi tiếc.

Đây là một địa điểm khá nổi tiếng ở Đà Nẵng.
Đứng trên đỉnh núi có thể nhìn thấy bãi cát trải dài, nước biển xanh ngắt, xa xa là khu vực trung tâm thành phố nhộn nhịp.
Từ bãi biển mỹ khê đến ngũ hành sơn khoảng 10km. Đến đà nẵng tốt nhất là nên thuê cho mình một chiếc xe máy để vi vu vì các địa điểm không quá xa nhau và có thể hít hà hơi biển một cách trọn vẹn
Khá cao, nhiều miếu thờ trang nghiêm
Quang cảnh thiên nhiên hùng vĩ. Tuy nhiên khai thác dịch vụ du lịch còn khá xô bồ từ khâu giữ xe đến vệ sinh công cộng xung quanh rất lộn xộn
Ngọn núi có phong cảnh đẹp với các ngôi chùa và hang động. Có thang máy đi lên và đi xuống cho người ngại leo thang bộ.
Ngọn núi cao khoảng 108 m, ngọn núi kỳ vĩ và linh thiêng với những ngôi chùa và hang động, đây cũng là nơi để luyện tập sức khỏe dẻo dai
Địa điểm đẹp cho bạn.
Núi non đẹp, bậc thang đi leo rất mệt. Những người ko leo nổi có thể đi thang máy. Mua hàng lưu niệm nhớ hỏi kỹ đá hay thủy tinh, có thể nhận biết bằng mắt.
Thiên nhiên là tuyệt vời

The Marble Mountains are a must-see when you are in Da Nang. It is a group of five hills with beautiful caves, old pagodas, and great viewpoints. There is so much to explore, and every cave feels like a hidden surprise.
You can take the elevator to go up for an extra charge, but the real fun is taking the stairs because you can see more of the beauty. It is not actually a long walk, and you can easily reach the top in about 20 minutes.
The caves inside are huge with amazing statues. This is one of the best places for photography and beautiful scenes. I really enjoyed climbing to the top to see the view of the beach and the city. Definitely worth a visit

Visiting the Marble Mountains is definitely worth it. The place is full of history, spirituality, and stunning scenery. The views from the top are beautiful, especially overlooking the coast and the city, and the temples and pagodas hidden among the mountains create a very peaceful and unique atmosphere.

If you plan to use the elevators, be prepared for some waiting lines, especially during busy hours, so it’s a good idea to allow extra time. Wearing comfortable clothes and proper shoes is highly recommended, as some caves are humid and the stairs can be slippery.

One thing to keep in mind is that there are many shops around selling jade products, statues, and souvenirs. While they look nice, most of them are not authentic, so be cautious and don’t buy them assuming they are real jade.

On the positive side, the walk is very diverse, combining nature, culture, and architecture. The caves are impressive, with natural light coming through the openings, creating a magical atmosphere. Overall, it’s a very enriching experience and quite different from other tourist attractions in the area.

Once you have got past the persistent sellers and parking lots this is an interesting place to visit.  We visited independently and found it difficult to find info in English,  our Vietnamese wasn't good enough to figure out exactly what was on site.   In the end we only visited the caves, this was a reasonable sized site and took us longer than others suggest.  It was a bit oddwithout context,  lots of strange figures and carvings but that aside the cave was incredible to see.  The whole area was busy so we decided against the rest of the mountain.   The main entrance for those walking is hidden away at the end of the main area where the coaches are.  That seemed the nicest approach away from all the crowds who seem to use the elevator entrance.

The caves might be tricky for those with limited mobility,  steep steps with hand holds to reach the view.

My advice, get there early or late.   Have a strategy for parking before you get hassled. Give yourself plenty of time,  it is more than a couple of hours visit to do it justice and getting away from the main throng of people.   This is tourist site, busy in the day.  Interesting if you're in the area,  we cycled from DaNang easily enough, but wouldn't call it a highlight.  Lady Budda site was much nicer.

The Marble Mountains are a really fascinating place to visit in Da Nang and definitely one of the city’s most unique attractions. I visited as part of a tour, which made everything easy and gave helpful context about the history, temples, and caves in the area.

The mountains are filled with beautiful pagodas, stone steps, viewpoints, and natural caves, and the views from the top are absolutely worth the climb. There’s a strong spiritual and historical atmosphere throughout the site, and it feels very different from anything else in the city.

That said, some of the caves and pathways can get quite tight and congested, especially during busy times. If you’re claustrophobic, it’s something to keep in mind — a few areas are narrow and can feel crowded. Taking your time and following the open paths helps a lot.

Overall, it’s still a memorable and rewarding experience, and I’d highly recommend it to anyone visiting Da Nang — just be prepared for stairs, crowds, and a bit of adventure along the way.

Our original tour was cancelled due to heavy rains and we went to the marble mountain look-out and the pagoda The elevator was out of service, due to rain, and so the only way up was a steep set of stairs that were of irregular height and depth. Rain caused a stream of water to flow down the stairs ensuring everyone’s feet were thoroughly soaked. Our group then went to one of the marble carving suppliers. Like the shopkeepers at the base of the marble mountain, they were insistent on trying to interest one in products that most of us were uninterested in. Read that as a heavy sales pitch. It was thoroughly annoying.

As I imagined nothing big.... A couple of promontories about 8 km from the center of da nang .I did on foot.but there were many people who proposed a transfer with motorcycles... I like walking much more... it allowed me to pass by houses where (New Year's Eve period) there were people praying to ancestors (their dead) next to laid tables ... interesting!
Far too many tourists, absolutely crowded and clear. It was super hot in the cave. We left after 20 minutes.

On this trip, we totally gave up on believing reviews. This is a total nothing. Climb tons of stone steps to see some little temples. Keep climbing and you almost miss the cave. It's one cavern with lots of decoration. Another cave you "can" go through are some very treacherous little wet steps to an area which looks like a dead end, so we had to manage to slide on our butts partway back down to exit. We saw some young girls outside who said you can in fact go all the way through that cave, but I cannot imagine how they did it. Total waste of time. And money, it is not free as some people say.

This place has a ticket office at the entrance and you pay VND 40,000, which is the entrance fee to the limestone cave. The inside is smoky with smoke from incense sticks, and you have to go up and down steep stairs like never before in Japan. If you are concerned about your lower back, you should avoid it. Then you buy tickets to go into the mountains again at the ticket office to get on the elevator and to get on the elevator. Even after getting off the elevator, you still need to be physically strong.

Careful where you park.. local shop store will tell you to park close to thier store but you need to check their products before getting your motorcycle back. Very expensive souvenirs and they won't let you go without buying or they will get mad and good luck getting your motorcycle back.. otherwise the mountain is nice

A place like Marble Mountain would be an amazing tourist asset to any region. Except Da Nang and Hoi An (and Viet Nam) have allowed it to become a tourist trap, a place where travellers are harassed, and heavy handedly encouraged to get ripped off at their marble stores.After a long day of sight seeing, on a incredibly hot and humid day, my easy rider motorcyclist left me in the hands of a local seller, who I was introduced to as someone who would show me to one of two entrances to the mountain. I of course worked out later that the seller was just trying to funnel me towards the exit I would descend to later on, where she would wait, and attempt to exploit as the tired, sweaty, and exhausted traveller I would be after exploring the mountain. I did get exploited that day, to my shame, as I paid for an item over the odds in a moment of weakness.Marble Mountain is a blight on the Vietmanese tourism landscape, and if local and national tourism authorities were interested in returned travel to the region and Viet Nam they should intervene with what goes on.
Waste of time, merchants are rude & scammers.  Do not bother, not much to see anyway.  Parking scam, pushy merchants & way overpriced in the shops.

Went there with my family to enjoy the hill and you are immediately grabbed by people selling crafts. There is a charge to use the elevator both up and a separate charge to use it to come down. There is also an entrance charge and they won’t even allow you to take a photo unless you have paid for it all. Once you are up the mountain (about 5 stories) you are once again accosted by guides forcing themselves on you and then demanding money. I’d take a hard pass on this one.

We set off by moped from Hoi An expecting to see beautiful unspoiled mountains. Instead we got hassled as soon as we arrived by a shop owner saying you park your bike for free here and then saying you buy from my shop for my family! There are loads of tourists, pagodas all over the mountain with sellers everywhere calling out to you. It is not at all peaceful. Not much of the natural rock to see as covered with Buddha images etc - we didn’t bother to go and see the caves as the queues were very long. We didn’t bother getting a ticket for the lift as well as entry pass as the lift only takes you half way up anyway and again the queue for that was long. We couldn’t wait to leave and gave the shop keeper some money but didn’t want to buy from the shop another woman came out demanding we buy from the shop and we had to explain we’d already paid. The rest of Vietnam you are not hassled even at the night market in Hoi An so this left a sour taste in the mouth.

Was hassled so much by the locals it was unpleasant.  Coupled with the fact the people in front of the ticket queue warned us the ticket office tried ripping them off, we turned around and decided not to view.

Avoid this place. It is overpriced even by the standards of other shops selling marble in the same street. Unfortunately I discovered this afterwards when I went back the next day and compared prices more thoroughly.  If you have the misfortune of being cornered by a sales shark called Ly.She is a liar about what is handcarved and what is genuine marble. I discovered after I had bought 2 dragon figures that they were not marble but composite marble (ie made of marble powder resin and moulded, not hand carved) Easily identified by scrating the bottom with a key. It scratches and leaves a white mark with white powder. I was shown this by an honest vendor I another shop. This woman spins a convincing story about her father carving the 2 pieces I bought and showed me an identical piece in white marble, claiming her brother made it, but with less detail as her brother was not as skilled.She also claimed all the "crystal balls were marble or crystal, and many were just that. But others were Chinese made glass. This I also learned from the above helpful, honest lady who showed me how to check for composite. I was thoroughly fleeced. What I learned..... Always get prices in dong as they always give prices in usd (which is illegal in Vietnam) At least then you know how much you are going to really pay if usd is not your currency. And do the math.This place is very close to the entry/exit from marble Mountain lift. Ly claimed the shops either side of phuoc Nhap were also owned by family members of hers. So avaiod them too. The further from the entrance (it where the lift is) the more honest the shops become and prices are more reasonable with negotiation.

Do it only if you have time to kill. The sculptured look like they are made of cement rather than marble. Also, they don't sell elevator tickets for up and down together. To go up you queue downstairs to buy tickets and then to use the elevator, same thing when you want to go downstairs. Steps aren't very safe, and old people should avoid. Even though they sell elevator tickets, to see the site you will need to climb up and down stone steps, so people with joint problems should avoid.

We visited the marble mountain as it is part of all the tours between hue and hoi an after the hai can pass but it is really not really worth to pay 40k to see this mountain recently constructed. It feel as a complete rip off

My husband and I can't believe this Da Nang site has received so many rave reviews!  I visit Buddhist sites as one of my greatest pleasures in my travels and this is a commercialized touristy trap!  The "caves" are little carved-out artifices with mostly restored Buddha images (showing no subtlety in iconography) and treacherous stairs with uneven stones.  Admittedly, I would face the challenges of the steep steps for an unbelievable image at the top, but none were to be found.  And with merchants following us all the way to sell some unattractive trinkets, it was an endurance test.  Save your energy for truly beautiful, ancient, and historically significant sites in Hue and/or Hoi An instead.

Not recommended if you are not a active hiker and have lots of time. Nothing much to see on the top of mountain. No English service or information. Just pay for elevator to the middle of mountain and you still have to hike quite a bit. It took time and I sweated a lot because Danang was very hot and humid. It is far from many attractions.

I would’ve given 4 stars for the experience of exploring the Marble Mountains alone, but it had been ruined from the start by the ticket lady in the main ticket entrance near the elevator.This middle-aged woman worked on the 9th June shift around 10a.m. when she expertly shifted my attention from being aware of how much change I would receive from her. She bombarded me with information, how I would have to pay again later when taking the elevator down & gave me different tickets I didn’t even have time to know their differences. Still, purposefully not returning my money yet.It was a VND95,000 price she quoted for what I later learned actually only costed 80,000 altogether. Worse thing was, I didn’t have a VND100,000 bill at the time so I gave her a 200,000 bill. So she was supposed to give me back a VND105,000 change, already with an extra VND15,000 to keep in her own pocket.As she flooded me with info, my daughter kept on nagging about the heat. It was a freaking hot morning & with a long queue for the elevator to bring us up the mountains, I just wanted to take my daughter a.s.a.p into the elevator.I think she immediately learned of my situation and slyly took advantage. By the time I realised that I only got VND5,000 as a change, it was already too late & difficult to go back to the ticket booth and try to argue our case. No one in the ticket booth would’ve had good enough English to be able to understand my chronological explanation. Or even if they had sufficieny English, they would’ve pretended they didn’t understand. It was a deliberate corruption act by an officer, how would I expect I would get help from her colleague?!Attached are pictures of our VND80,000-worth tickets which have costed us VND195,000! Their serial numbers are shown and could actually be used to trace the crooked officer, if only there were an effective grievance mechanism to deal with such corrupt behaviour of an official thief!!

We saw much better caves in SaPa and the step weren’t so slippery. Pay careful attention when buying entry tickets because they automatically add the price of a small souvenir handbook you really won’t want. Loaded with bus tours.

Packed with tourists. Our backpack got cut open on the bottom, luckily it’s made with two layers of thick material so we didn’t lose anything. If you look into other reviews you will see it happens all the time.  My suggestion: Don’t even waste your time to come here.

There's two entrances and different fees plus the fee doesn't include the elevator ride which my mom and an injured companion rode. Not much to see. It also difficult if you come with kids as there are steep climbs.

What a waste of money and time! You will find yourself with loud, annoying crowds of hundreds of Korean and Chinese your busses climbing down (in case you took the elevator) the steep, uneven steps back to the scammer ladies of the marble shops who "invite" you to park for free. On top the reward will be a kitschy series of newer temples, also overrun by the masses. What an unpleasant experience.
Full of tourist... Not so interesting.. It's business with too many bus who re coming. There is light like a nightclub.. What the hell

We arrived by taxi.woman approches hi whefe u from welcome the usual chat .she walked with us not leaving our side to the entrance fee 40.000 dong to climb steps 55.ooo dong to go in escalater tiny mountain not worth it.go to lady budda its free in and so much nicer.anyway we went up gate 2 came down gate 1 and the woman was waiting for us u come my shop mow yes u say u come my shop.had to go to shop and hsd to buy something small so she would leave us alone.you help my family she kept saying.as we walkex out there are loads of them praying on people who arrive alone.if your with a tour they leave you alone as the guide shuns them off.Dont Go.

To anyone planning on visiting Marble Mountains - please be aware that we got robbed in broad daylight today (Dec 28), with my girlfriend's bag slashed and a phone taken. We reported it to security and were taken to the police station, where we found out they'd taken 3 other phones that day and this is a common problem.She was surrounded by 3 women on one of the stair ways, all wearing hats, pretending to take selfies - her bag was then unknowingly slashed four times and her phone taken (and still not recovered).Two of the girls were captured by security and taken to the police station - staff were helpful and took us to the police station so this is just a warning to take precautions.Be alert and keep an eye on your belongings visiting here - we've been safely travelling for 15 years with no problems and class ourselves as 'sensible travellers' so this is a shame to have happened whilst visiting Vietnam.

Marble mountain was nice to see, but when they scheating you immidiately at the register where you buy the tickets for the ellevator, it is a false start.the both lady´s at the register asking double the price that they should do.ohoh cheating, cheating....... soo bad!

We booked a tour and it was the biggest waste of time and money ever,we saw nothing amazing ,we had to climb 1 mi stairs at 31 degrees.There are no descriptions in English up there so you will see an old temple ,a cave and an observation tower that seems completely unsafe

My wife and I were very excited to go here but wish we hadn’t bothered. You pay a fee to go up the elevator. On the way back they shout at you to pay to go back down. It’s cheap so it’s not about the cost - it just felt like a scam. And you need to watch so they give you the correct change back. It’s way overcrowded - I doubt they ever know how many people are walking about here. Tourists everywhere. People will push past you (not unusual in asia, but still annoying). Queue to get in or out a cave, up steep slippery steps with people pushing and you will get pretty annoyed pretty quickly. Been to some great places in Vietnam. This is not one of them. Avoid.

I do not understand all the pooha about this place. There is a small cave to visit, the price for the elevator too high and the marble being sold by the numerous shops seem to be imported, as the mountain is very small and no real excavations seem to be taken place. The total offer of statues on sale probable when piled up with reach midway the mountain.I assume it is just advertisement for the marble working companies along the road, which have great artisans, I have to admit.

My sister and I went here and I have to say, I enjoyed this more than other pagodas. Different colours than usual and it was very pretty. The day we went it wasn't so busy.Unfortunately, immediately when you arrive you get harassed by people telling you to park at their place. This is why we decided to park it somewhere where no one was shouting at people. When we got back our bike was build in and we couldn't leave. She told us to pay 10.000 dong or buy a large water for 20.000. At first we thought it was too much but after we agreed if she would just get us out first. She wouldn't do this, but we wouldn't pay before. They can charge more just to get you out after you already paid that 10.000, so we started moving bikes ourselves. (We were on a time schedule and this was taking too long & we explained ourselves but she just wouldn't listen).She got really mad and started pulling, pushing and pinching my sister and I. She stole our key and only gave it back after we finally got them to agree to a mutual exhange. We were shaking from anger and because it was scary afterwards. We get that she wanted her money and that moving the bikes wasn't nice but there was absolutely no reason to start hurting us!
I personally would not recommend.  Not transparent with costs.  Staff were rude when we asked questions.
Over crowded and not a worth it. Too many people and u have to buy the map for 15k which is ridiculous. Only tourist making pictures. Not a nice place to visit. Just skip.

Very common karstic moutains where the locals from Hoi An decided, in the 90’s to develop another buddha « theme park » to attract the tourists. Don’t be fooled and avoid this. View from the top on a industrial area, great...

I wish I could give them 0*! Decent view of all the construction in Da Nang/ surrounds (if you like the look at construction sites). Supposed to be a religious scared place - millions of loud people, no crowd control. Terrible vendors who harass you constantly. Filthy dirty- the toilets were the worst I’ve seen in Asia yet(and make you take your shoes off) Seemed expensive - they should at least sweep and clean the toilets. 40k entrance + 15k elevator each way and they charged us twice and then denied it.

This could be a wonderful experience with the caves and stone carvings. Unfortunately though the whole complex has been turned into a dangerous tourist cash cow for those that operate it.There are some tricky steps and restricted cave passages to negotiate and there is no crowd control whatsoever.Beware if you are claustrophobic, on the large side or have a disability as you may find yourself in a queue of masses of noisy tourists in a restricted space, climb or descent and unable to easily turn back.It was the worst part of our holiday and it is totally appalling how the crowds are not managed after entry. Avoid at all costs unless you can be sure of avoiding the crowds. But I don’t know how since there are literally dozens of large coaches. There have no doubt been accidents at this location, as there were many very concerned and slightly panicky people who were not warned about the narrow passageways. This leads to people shouting in the caves making things worse for others.The local maps provided on entry are useless as are the signs around the complex. There are no warnings at all about climbs, restrictions or the fact that one steep climb leads to a dead end and you then have to descend via the same route! a major problem when there is a queue of people behind you and they all have to stop on very narrow cliff edge steps to let you pass.Find something else to do that is not the current main attraction on TripAdvisor or Lonely Planet etc - at least until they resolve the crowd control issues at this site. Unfortunately I suspect this will only happen after several accidents happen at this site. (We are a fit couple, 31 and 45 years)

Irish couple on tour, well worth the visit but as we got near the entrance a lady waved us to park our bike “free of charge”.. as we parked our bike and got off she said it’s free to park your bike, once you buy something in her shop of overpriced marble!
You must be paid for entrance to the caves.And you must be paid for the elevator going up.And you must be paid for entrance to the mountains.And you must be paid for the elevator going down.
overcommercialised and poorly maintained site - wasting your time to visit this Asian Disneyland . Do not bother

Nothing is special about this place.  Wasting our precious time visit this place.  I do not understand why this place rate 4*.  I strongly skip this place when visit Da Nang, unless who wants to waste his/her time.

Wish I would have listened to the review below that states the problem isn't with the mountain itself but with the rude staff and everyone trying to sell you something. From the minute you drive up you have people chasing you down to sell you something. When you buy your ticket you can pay for the elevator up. It's less than a dollar and totally worth it when it's scorching. BUT BEWARE... you have to pay to go back down again which is ridiculous. It's not about the money bc it is cheap but just another Vietnam scam. On top of that the staff was so rude and the ticket lady tried to short us change. When we realized she shorted us 100,000 VND we turned around and she immediately pulled it out and gave it to us bc she knew exactly what she was up to. Not the first we've heard about this happening there. Then after we paid 3,000 to park the motorbike out front, she tried to say we needed to pay again when we left. We just ignored her and left. The whole place wasn't very impressive at all - decent views at best. But it's completely ruined by the ongoing scams.

This was by far the worst place I have ever visited in Vietnam or South East Asia. The problem is not the mountain itself but the behaviour of the staff there. As soon as you get out of the taxi you get harassed to come and buy things in the souvenir shops. Don't make the mistake of going in one. You're not leaving unless you buy something. You will get short changed at the ticket office and you will be screamed at if you point it out to them. At the sites people follow you to sell you stuff. The worst was when we asked for directions to path to another cave, they deliberately pointed us to the exit and then wanted to charge us to get back again. Avoud

Super crowded noisy and extremely humid. They didn't bother to limit people so everywhere was crowded. Cannot even take a single photo without having at least 10 people inside. View was nothing to shout about. Pls skip this place as there was REALLY no value added and there was other location u can go to for the views and better photo opportunity. Waste of time!

I usually plan my trips pretty perfectly to avoid backtracking. I only had a day in Da Nang and the Marble Mountain ticket office clearly states they sell tickets until 5:30pm so after an awesome beach day I showed up at 4:45 to discover the ladies in the ticket office decided to pack up early for the day. I politely asked if I could still buy a ticket and they rudely yelled "no!" as they continued to count their stack of cash. So, I guess this review is mostly based on the ticketing and not the mountain itself, since I didn't even get to see it. Tip: show up before 4pm or else you might also enjoy being yelled at for free.
Hard getting up and down the steps ,make sure you go when it's cool a lot of walking required .I wouldn't be rushing back
Mostly a tourist trap. Take 30 mins and bail out. Not much to see there. There are better pagodas in Vietnam. Cave is average

We had an interesting tour here and were persuaded to buy some statues that would be shipped home to Liverpool without any problem. They arrived in Felixstowe ( a 700 mile round trip for us). It cost us almost as much as the statues did to get them home (VAT , port fees, transport costs) Two of the statues were broken due to inadequate packaging. The suppliers immediately said they were broken in the warehouse but no answer to my question of why they were shipped here then. They said the insurance had told them to make more, but I didn't want to pay to get them here again and no guarantee they wouldn't be broken again. They said they would compensate us but 6 weeks later, despite them saying they have done, we have nothing from them. Clearly they are playing a game to keep their warehouse stocked having taken the tourists money.

Just too many people and tour groups. Everyone moving so slowly through the mountain. The natural attraction is ok however there is rubbish everywhere off the designated route. Sad as some caves are cool but covered in litter. The general amount of people is really unnerving.

Lovely view of Danang and very interesting with lots of status and a large Buddha. Starts are very step and irregular in size would not suit people who do not have average fitness. There USA.igt which takes you part way up and would advise you use this as you have more steps still to climb. Would advise you visit in the morning as it's hard work visiting all the sites. Had a great time.
Total dissatisfaction there was a lack of organization and no tourist management. The elevators did not work and vendors were aggressive.

Highly over rated site. Visitors  are usually "invited" to visit one of the stone carving shops where the staff is very pushy to sell. The temple complex and caves on top of the mountain are ok, but certainly not as spectacular as many guides describe it.

Its gives perfect views and scenic backdrops for the perfect selfie. It was really hot but its worth the visit & worth the climb. However, I am really dissapointed with the staff. We bought a ticket to take the elevator up. It was cheap (less than USD$ 1), but we had to buy another ticket to come down. At the ticket counter, my friends and I paid seperately and 2 of us were short changed by the staff by 100,000 Dong (USD$ 5). They gave us change in several small notes ( 5,000 & 10,000) We didn't check the change cos there was a queue. Who knows how many others had the same fate.
Nothing special!Stop only If you are driving past!Lots of sellers at the bottom of the mountain!!!
We found this place to be horrendous actually! We really hated it. Its a massive tourist trap and not a whole lot of history, its a maze of Thai pagodas and Chinese temples...

This place is a complete tourist trap. It's a small mountain with a lift up the side to take you halfway up. Essentially it is one large souvenir shop, being surrounded by shops selling the same thing of marble sculptures. We rented our own scooter and were approached before we'd even gotten to the turn off for the mountain on a Vietnamese lady on a scooter who was initially very nice. She continually asked us to come and park at her family's shop at the base of marble mountain (every shop owner has started a scam of charging you to park, threatening that your bike will be gone if you don't pay to park it at one of their shops). We continually politely declined until she wouldn't stop and we decided to drive away. At which point she yelled out to her friend across the street who also came and blocked our path to leave. When we started to leave she began to abuse as I'm Vietnamese. It was a really horrible experience and put a sour taste in our mouths. We decided to leave and drove up to the Lady Buddha in Da Nang instead which was much nicer. On our return the exact same thing happened on the highway again, but from the other direction. Clearly the women sit on their scooters waiting for westerners to hustle you into parking at their shop. Just another example of an attraction completely exploited to the point of losing any and all draw power.

Not good for family with young children and our tour guide was terrible. Climbing the stairs is really difficult, going downstairs is just dangerous. Also there are a lot of people asking you to buy the same stuff. It gets old fast.

We wanted just to buy tickets for the elevator as we were not interested in the cave and had little time. We wanted to have a look from the top but the staff was yelling "No,no!" and wanted to sell a set of cave & elevator ticket only and didn't let us buy only the tickets for the elevator.We turned back and left without see anything.If the ticket is a " set of 2", why they sell them separated??It looked like a tourist trap.
Just a hollowed out mountain. Tourist trap. The pagoda is nice but if you're sick of pagodas then there is not much left to see

I don't understand how TA can be so off sometimes. I cannot believe some reviewers said this attraction is amazing. There are some steps to climb, a few small caves (if you've ever seen any other caves, these are not worth your time), a pagoda and a viewpoint that really wasn't that impressive. You could vaguely see the surrounding buildings and beach but it's not exactly a stunning view. As mentioned in previous reviews, the ladies selling marble will hassle but also the stalls selling drinks. The place isn't well maintained and there's lots of rubbish along the pathways. Not trying to sound negative, this place is a cheap attraction if you need a break between travelling but it's certainly not worth going out of your way.

We never planned to head here but had some time to kill and went out on our scooter. Firstly, if you're thinking of doing the same don't. Secondly, the reason for the awful score is because this is a first rate tourist trap. Sadly this lives up to the unfair reputation Vietnam sometimes gets for treating it's visitors like ATM's. From the minute you head down the road, vendors urge you to park in their shop which is clearly a scam. Ignoring this, we parked in the allotted area for the mountain itself and went to pay. The lady in he office gave us change for 100vnd when we gave her 200vnd. When challenged, she didn't even put up a fight and just handed over the 100vnd. A classic scam but sours things before you even step in.It's littered with overpriced tat and then your charged to enter the park and the same again if you want to use the lift. If you want to come down the lift, you pay again. It only drops you halfway up as well. That aside, wondering around is surreal. It all feels new and incredibly concocted. There's litter everywhere, beautiful caves adorned with tacky neon and when you want to use the toilet, you're greeted by a woman who's first words are 'money'. It's terribly sad to witness and shouldn't be visited as it will tarnish Hoi An which is quite lovely. At this point, we decided to call it a day and head back to Hoi An. We only wish we'd done this before we paid. We're seasoned travellers with a fair and subjective view on things but you can file his under 'tourist trap' in the dictionary. Vietnam is much better than is.

This was a stopping point for the mini bus from Hue to Hoi An. The cafe was a rip off and it was the usual tourist trap with no time. We led a revolt...there is another cafe round the back of the main highway where you can get cheaper food and a much warmer welcome. Don't believe them when they say there isn't anywhere else.
Local guy followed us around asking if we wanted our picture taken which was a bit annoying, the site wasn't very interesting and there are much better views for free elsewhere.

When I went the lift wasn't working (although I think I may well of still chosen to climb all the stairs) !It was a wet day and the steps were very slippery (so take care)Even though it was a wet day it was still very hot and I found it hard going climbing the main steps and then all of the other ones once you start entering the caves. For me once I had seen one I quickly lost interest as they all looked pretty much the same !It might well be a very interesting place for some people but it just didn't quite do it for me....

My Vietnamese friend who paid for the tickets was scammed. The ticketing agent told her she needed two tickets per person to enter the cave. So the two of us ended up with four tickets, each paying 30,000 vnd. Not a lot of money but the experience was embittering, especially because the staff were sniggering.

Very hot and busy. Lots of the same sights you see all over . Steps very dangerous . Tried to overcharge us by double at the ticket desk . Even a street restaurant tried to up the bill for 3 sandwiches despite the prices clearly written on the menu !

We went at 7.00 Am to avoid the heat. It didn't work. There are two ways to climb the mountain hard and ridiculously harder and a modern lift that was damaged on the Typhoon and is out of action.Take the first entrance it's the better. Met a bus load of people trying to walk up second set of stairs when we were coming down and they were very stressed at the half way point. Watch out for the little old lady with no teeth at the first cave she will seem very helpful and then demand money for her services. Some nice Buddha's and shrines and a reasonable view from the top. A few English guides might help. It could be so much better. Don't do this if your stamina is not good.

I visited this area during my 5th trip to Vietnam and fell for a scam by Xuat Anh handicrafts and Phuoc Nhap handicrafts.  The vendors will follow you about all week to sell you items they claim are marble.  I bought from these vendors and was promised if I paid $100. dollars shipping they would arrive in Jacksonville Florida and I would need to pay any charges determined by the United States authorities.  I travel a lot and didn't have a problem with paying charges determined by the US since that is a set and not arbitrary charge. We all pay duties.  Instead, some guy on the West Coast requested that I give him several hundred dollars by wire which included a truck cleaning charge or I would not get my purchases.  Needless to say I did not wire this unknown funds and never got my items.  Don't buy from these people unless you can carry it with you.  I had read a poor review about Marble Mountain vendors and bought anyway so its my fault, but don't fall for it.

Sorry to say, but this place was a complete waste of time.  The caves are nothing special, with tacky lighting and decorations (try Phong Nha-Ke Bang National Park, the place for caves in Vietnam).  This place is crawling with tourists and groups with megaphones.  Zero information is provided - an obvious ploy to get you to hire a tour guide.  There is a view from the top, but it's not very impressive, as the surrounding area is mostly industrial sprawl, ruined from the mining of the marble.  You can't walk more than two steps in the village below without someone trying to sell you something, and we had a particularly bad experience with the shop where we parked our motorbikes.  Specifically, the shop owner promised safe parking, then hid our helmets and refused to return them until we bought something for her shop.  Like I said, the place is one giant tourist trap.  If you want to see the area's natural beauty, head up to the Hai Van Pass - rent your own scooter, or hire a driver to take you.  The views are incredible.

Avoid the Marble Shops surrounding the Mountain. The girls selling the bracelets etc are charming but forget to tell you that the items are vastly overpriced by 4-5 times and up. I made a mistake of buying from one of these shops for two items which I later found out was worth 6 times less than what it was worth. I went back to the shop but the girl who sold to me hid when she saw me, I asked for a total refund but was refused. I will never be back to these shops so I would like to advise others to be careful. Totally dishonest people.David.

I was very disappointed and would have been more so if I had made a special trip here luckily it was walking distance from my hotel. 3 caves nothing special. Good views from a few points and several temples/pagoda's, well over rated!!!

We ended up here on the obligatory stop en route to Hoi An.  We were set upon before we were even out of the car!  Buy this, good price, cheap, good quality.  Now tell me, who can carry a 10kg marble carving around with them for the rest of their trip?  When we finally made it clear we were not interested in buying anything, the antics of the upstart young lady were something to behold!   She left us in no doubt whatsoever what she thought of us..

if you are traveling a lot of south east asia you will probably see better caves better scenery, I was very disappointed with this site. I ended up paying an expensive taxi fare so I could get back to my hotel in hoi an because I didnt want to wait another 2.5 hours for the rest of the group. not a lot to see, and too many local tourists and a big school group. also sooo many shops trying to sell you marble and the usual souvenirs. bit by the tourist scam again! when you go to a city or town and you see a lot of travel agents offering the same tours, they are usually rubbish!  so plan carefully

My family and I recently visited Marble Mountain and we were impressed with the marble statues and urns for sale at reasonable prices.   At "Ut Lan Art and Stone Sculpture" we bought two large marble urns on stands and paid for packing, insurance and shipping to Australia.   We photographed the urns in-situ.    Two urns duly arrived two months later only for us to find that they had substituted the urns that we had purchased with badly broken ones that had been glued back together before packing.    Insurance only covers damage in transit - not damage before shipping!!   Of course, no replies to my EMails to Ut Lan.   "Ut Lan Art and Stone" is a rip off - so beware.Phil BrandAdelaideAustralia
The most boring place I've ever visited. Nothings well kept, everyone's out to get ur money.Left me feeling very down and fed up!

There were a lot of tourist groups when we went at 10am. It costs 20K VD to go inside the caves and there is an additional cost to go up an elevator that takes you up to a view of Da Nang. I preferred seeing the Lady Buddha to this. I wanted to know more about the history of the caves and area. There are several displays inside the cave which were odd. For example, they had temples, then some figures with masks on that appeared to be killing people, and then some random plastic arms at the entry in the water. I had to Google to understand what the significance of the caves are but only a bit of information came up. I did hear a tour guide say to his group “welcome to Hell” so I am not sure if this is relevant to the odd figures and displays that I saw. Based on some Google searching there is some relevance to that place being used as a hospital by the Vietnamese people during the time that the Americans invaded Vietnam. It wasn’t worth it. There are some shops there and marble and jade seems to be a big thing in that area. You will see a lot of stores selling small statues and if you walk further out you will see stone shops with even larger carvings.

Niente di che. Tanti turisti per vedere un tempio di Budda e qualche statua. Una grotta umida. Per fortuna si sale e si scende con l’ascensore. Dall’alto panorama piatto sul lungomare. Parcheggio stracolmo di bus turistici e venditori di souvenir.
Unmengen Touristen die Tausende Bilder von sich selber machen. Vorsicht: das Ticket für den Lift gilt nur für eine Richtung. Wenn man wieder runter will zahlt man nochmal. Nicht empfehlenswert

The scenic outside the mountain is beautiful. The inside of the cave is wicked and in my opinion, not suitable for children... There are literally art drawings of demons killing children. I felt so disgusted in the cave, I wanted to get out as fast as possible.

Aan de hand van de recensies op TripAdvisor toch besloten te gaan. Marble Mountain ligt op de weg van Hue naar Hoi A via Da Nang. Omdat wij een goede gids mee hadden hebben we ook het mooie oude altaar uit 1400 gezien en se hyrogliefen, maar wat is dit verpest met de overdaad aan toeristische troep en marmeren beelden. Bovendien is het er nu - in mei - al veel te druk. Er zijn echt moeite dingen om te zien. Zeker met die hitte.

Over rated tourist trap. The caves are small there nothing special.There are a lot of uneven stairs.The end of the caves will lead you out to the shops. Where you will get harrases by taxi drivers and shop owners.Skip this is you have been to any other caves in Vietnam.

De omgeving van de marbleMountains is enorm vies, veel puin op de grond en veel zooi. Zeer zonde. Het zou een prachtige locatie kunnen zijn maar rondom elke tempel is enorm veel puin. Niet de moeite waard. Zou dit zeker overslaan

Overall a mediocre experience. The climb can be very steep and once you get to the top and the opening within the caves, it’s very underwhelming. It’s also really warm so if you do go, I’d strongly recommend wearing proper footwear and bringing a small electric fan along with wearing something you’d be ok to sweat through completely. It’s not expensive but it is not a must-see. Not an experience / site I’d recommend for visitors, especially if you have children. It’s uncomfortable for them.
Didn’t really enjoy as i’m not particularly into religious sites. Could be a very different story for someone who is.  Went only because there isn’t much things in the city itself.

Marble Mountain trip was a bit of a disappointment. Be aware you do have to be quite mobile to do this as there are a lot of steps and they are uneven. Our guide explained the history and facts making it quite interesting. However there was a lot of similarity between the caves. Our trip included lunch at a local house this was a part we should have missed. BEAWARE of the thieves . One of our group had a backpack opened and it had been disturbed. Backpacks on front if you go.

We took the elevator up to the top and walked through several small caves with a few old religious statues and other items.  Soon we found no other place in that area that looked attractive so we walked back down to the bottom of the mountain, which only took about 10 minutes.  The stone steps going down the mountain were also very dangerous and difficult to walk in comfort.

Sorry,  I'm sure it was just me, but I was not impressed.  I found the site very dangerous and if you have mobility problems I would avoid it.  Perhaps if you can keep walking up higher and higher, then maybe it would be better, but I would prefer normal "building temples" or sites  with safe pathways.
Very busy and unremarkable. Inside was okay but if I had my time again we wouldn’t have gone. Decent view and good location for some photos but that’s about it.

A strange place atop a karst. Didnt actually see any marble. At the top of about 100 steps is about 5 pagodas and three small caves. Some annoyingly loud singing from some monks using microphones which they really didnt need. Cost an quite a lot too, 40 dong per adult, then 15 dong for the elevator up (and they dont tell you it's another 15 dong to get back down - scammers). There are some food shops at bottom and top, didnt eat or drink anything though. Viewpoints are ruined by constructions of new hotels. Wouldn't recommend visiting.

You need to pay 2 times to visit some of the caves, and other caves they got on the map and in the pictures, is not there at all. Then you need to pay for another cave so you need to pay 3 times. It is like average but not worth it.
Not much to view or cherish. I wouldn't recommend if u have to visit with kids. Not much to see around and very rude staff

If you have been to the caves in Guilin, this isn’t spectacular. There is an elevator that will bring you to the top. You have to pay 15000 dong in addition to the admission ticket. Expect some steep climb. It’s manageable though. If you have time to spare, you can consider this day trip.

Well, is nice but I believe is overrated. I don’t believe is a must and no one should feel that missed anything if not here. Large, LARGE groups of Asian(Chinese) tourist that are getting them here with big busses and it gets very,very crowded without particularly no reason. The worst of all is the “sharks” around the shops that are very annoying. Trust me, at one point they get very annoying.

An example of could be great - however it wasnt the cleanest, you have to go out your way to get to it, and the hassle if you turn up on a moped finding parking with people outside chasing you down the street for their 'free' parking (if you buy marble) which you'd do well to get on a plane isnt worth it! After 1 cave their all just cavesGo up monkey mountain or make the most out of Hoi an!
There were a few things to see here but there are many steps and the whole placed looked tired and unkempt. Very disappointingafter a very hot ride in the lift.
Most disappointing thing I did so far in Vietnam. In my opinion you can easily skip that from your schedule.

Having gone to use the ‘free’ public bathroom I was already wondering why it seemed a lady with a little stall outside was haggling with two ladies who had just come out of the bathroom. As I left the bathroom I was then shouted at in Vietnamese, turns out I was supposed to borrow a pair of flipflops she had conveniently stashed next to her stall. Having held my hands up and apologised claiming I didn’t know she used her sweetest English voice and told me to buy something from her stall. Not agreeing to pay for anything I grabbed her mop and cleaned the bathroom floor myself. I am more than happy to tip cleaners in bathrooms as such is the norm in many countries, but I do not agree with being tricked into buying off a stall.Next my boyfriend and I observed one of the men (who we assumed who worked there) empty a bucket for donations to ‘poor people’ into his cap, he then went onto the next stall to buy himself some drinks and pocketed the rest. If we were to donate, we would hope our money would go to those it was promised to or at least to the conservation of such a site but not for someone’s own personal use. It is disappointing to see such a spiritual and meaningful place being taken advantage of by a couple of people who disrespect its visitors.

We were first time in Vietnam to visit this marble mountain.It has been an uncomfortable experience when we trying to purchase the elavator and entrance tickets. The seller at the booth showing an unfriendly face and just throw the ticket to us after we paying for it. When enter the marble mountain, we are shocked that we actually needed to wear proper attire in order to climb out the mountain in order to prevent from injuries. there cactus and spiky plants up on the mountain, the only thing you can see is the view from the top. and there's nothing else.If you are active hiker, you may go for this choice. if you are there for fun, please choose another option.
Very interesting we went on a rainy day and still enjoyed the visit. Be prepared as their is a lots of steps take a lot of water.

Probably causes a better impression to westerners that haven’t been to Asia before, but the site is really not impressive. China has half a million places more applesling than it.Good for a quick stop, but don’t keep your hopes very high.Total price for the site was around 70.000 each person.

Went to marble MoU Tau s. While impressive, lots of very steep steps, some too steep to safely descend. Literally you think if I fall here I'm gone. My wife had her hand bag picked by some scum thief while there. Seems it is a common occurrence there, yet nothing is done about the scum thieves. When we reported it the marble mountain could not care less.Given the cost, the amount t of  steep, no real hand rail steps you need to traverse this is not a friendly attraction.Save you money go elsewhere If you ho be wary of scummbag pick pickett thieves. Very disappointed.

Hard to skip but disappointing - over crowded lots of rubbish and views built out by high rise hotels. If you can get to more remote locations avoid. Da Nang itself is a great place to visit with terrific beach.

Nothing really to see. There is one cave that’s quite big and has what looks like a stone Buddha inside. But other than that it’s just plastic deities. The best bit is the high point I think it’s called heavens gate, it’s a bit of a climb but the views are worth it. Wouldn’t go back. Don’t feel that if you miss it you’ll have missed out.

Fortunately we didn’t have to go out of our way to visit as we were on our way from Da nang to Hoi An. As we have been to a number of places on this trip we have been spoiled for choice and found this to be dirty surroundings to get to the entrance.  Very hot and really for us not worth the effort.  However not all doom and gloom, some nice Buddha statues but the caves had weird piped music which cheapened the whole thing.

Marble Mountain was part of our transfer fromHue to Hoi An. I would totally recommend to skip this place and spend more time on Hai Van Pass or even Lăng Co beach.There are two entrances 1) to some cave 2) An elevator to the top. For each entrance there is a charge. Not griping about the charges but mainly am unable to relate to the statues etc Some of the signages in the cave are written in mandarin. It would be really helpful if there is an English translation for non mandarin speaking tourists.

I thought this was highly overrated. You are bombarded with people selling marble goods in the marble village upon approach  which immediately turned me off. You have to pay more money (only 10,000 vnd) to use the elevator which is a shame for people who cannot use stairs.
