I did not see signs posted in English so I could not add any historical or religious context. I think you should skip this and aim for Ba Na or Bach Ma National Park.

This is the perfect place to observe hundreds, maybe thousands of Chinese tourists in on small enclosed space. There is an amazing variety of them, and most of them move really slowly, so you have time to study them in great detail.
Honestly it’s too overrated. It’s just a set of staircase. No real hike. Totally skippable. Go to monkey mountains instead.

Visited en route from Hue to our hotel in De Nang.I would not recommend bothering to come here. Lots and lots of shops selling marble trying to grab your business using imported marble.The caves and pagoda on top on the mountain aren’t very impressive and are very touristy.The best bit was the very badly signposted hike to the peak which we found by exploring lots of footpaths from the main seating area.

Wasted about an hour here and 40,000 dingo dollars entry plus an extra 15,000 on the lift up to walk around a dangerously slippery crappy cave and pagoda complex, that was as interesting as a flip flop washed up on a beach!!! Although the natural aspect and views are good, the mosquitoes aren’t, neither are the fake tour guides and vendors outside. Trust me you don’t need a guide, just buy a ticket from the abrupt women at the booth near the elevator, wear decent footwear and expect people to try and sell you stuff when you use the loo.
High pressure to buy.Not a friendly day trip.If you not in market for stone skip the village and go to mountain

it of course depends on what people likeHowever not for meYou can walk up to the top and down or take the liftWe went up by lift and walked downNot for anyone with disabilities as steps very steepVery hot humid and unkeptFirst and only time we were hassled by sellers which is unusual as Vietnamese people we otherwise met fantastic
Nothing to rave about.  Just caves and nothing charming about them.  Some buddha carvings inside with strings of flashing lights, neon liked, making them look quite cheesy...  Not worth it at all....

Honestly this is not that great to visit. Only did this as I was on my way to Hoi An. Regret wasting my money on this. If you must then fine but i would go straight to Hoi AnSo you pay to use the lift. If you are able to walk please use the stairs as there isn’t much difference and you burn a bit of calories. The only good thing about this is the great pictures you can take.
If you like a bit hiking you should come here for me is pretty hot and have nothing much up there. hard to find a drink and food and a map for walk around still complicated about me

If this was your first time seeing caves, you might think that Marble Mountain is good. Unfortunately I was very disappointed. Having been to Halong Bay Caves there is no comparison. The lighting is tacky with cheap neon flashing lights and people drop their rubbish all through the caves & area. I felt like grabbing a bag & picking it all up. One positive,  nice view up to the top.
Want to be hassled by touts and see quite average “mountains” ?  Lots of little shops selling trinkets and there’s a walk or lift to the top.  Nothing special - spend the time going to other places.
This place is crowded. Though the cave is nice, the walk up and down the steps inside were dangerous. Too much incense burning inside the cave.

This could have been a wonderful experience. Beautiful views, hidden paths, caves and pagodas. But all this was spoilt by all the tourist tat at the bottom and vendors hassling you. I'd give it a miss! I would rather ramble through all the back streets of Danang seeing vietnamese life.

I don't know how much time you have to visit Vietnam but if you are in a hurry skip it. We stayed for three weeks in Vietnam and this is the only part I would have skipped. Rent motorbikes and go to Monkey Mountain or to hue. The road is amazing.

We went from Da Nang by the yellow bus (20.000 VND one way). The mountain itself and the caves are nothing spectacular. The caves are very small and INCREDIBLY crowded. There are queues at the entrances to the caves and the tourists don't respect the nature. They are too loud as well. It is beautiful if you can filter the tourists from the sight, but if you have something better to do - skip the Marble Mountains

We rented a bike a went to marble mountain since we where staying in Hoi An , lots of people there lots of climbing, if you have a bad knee or hip skip this out , it’s not worth the effort , there is a elevator but it does not take you the temples ,

We drove our motorbike here. On arriving to marble mountains we were hounded by a lady on a motorbike (and followed around the block by her) - trying to scam us into parking at her place, which we had to very firmly refuse after many attempts at doing so politely. Many other shop vendors try to get you to park at their place as well - just ignore them and go straight to where the buses are pulling in and there's a legitimate parking area where you only need to pay 3000 dong to park and they will look after your bike. Entry to the mountain is 40,000 dong per person and an extra 15,000 if you want to use the lift to get up the first section of stairs. We just used the stairs as we don't mind the exercise, and it's not very difficult. But if you can't be bothered use the lift (remember though that there's lots of stairs and difficult areas to access when you get up there). Also they try to charge you an extra 15,000 dong for a map - seriously don't bother, there are maps everywhere once you get up. There are basically temples in caves and statues of Buddha up there, which we don't really have interest in, and we felt it was all just a big money trap. Some okay views from the highest points which is why I give 2 stars, and the small marble statues are only $1 US (or less) if you would like a souvenir. If you choose to go bring a big water and snacks (you will get severely overcharged if you try to buy there), and steer clear of the many scammers around.
Very disappointed not as grand as I was lead to believe no hand rails and damp steps poor lighting and hazardous

People who are a regular travelers would not find this out of world . A missable !! As for people who would want to know more about the history and archeological significance it's worth . Rest Danang as a city has other things to offer as well !

Not sure if we just didn’t understand the point of some of it but seemed to just be an average cave they had filled with statues like the picture and of demons sawing a woman in half etc. View was distinctly average.

We could walk to this from our Hotel on Non Nuoc beach, and the lift certainly saves you a walk.However you do have to 'run the gauntlet' of all the Marble shop sellers harassing you to come into their shops.  Then there is the trinket and refreshment sellers.Once you get up to the pagodas, there is more 'touristy tack' and other refreshments.  Which in a lot of cases spoiled the view of the ancient monuments we were there to see.I would happily wonder in and out of shops make a few purchases as I go, but I hate being pestered into viewing and buying.

If you enjoy lots and lots of steep uneven steps that lead to caves with no stalactites or stalactites, this could be the best day out ever while visiting Danang.   There is an elevator that takes you up, and down if you want to pay again, but there is still a lot of climbing to do.The cave and various pagodas and buildings are nothing special.  There are plenty of better pagodas to be found.  Yes, it is all very pretty (although the amount of rubbish along the trails was disturbing.  I even saw a shoe!)However, if you are looking for some marble statuary of saints, gods, dragons, lions or communist dictators from the 1930s, it actually really good.  Seriously, the marble sculptures and trinkets shops were the best part for me.

I've read reviews about getting shortchange here but getting it first-hand is quite an experience. My friends and I wanted to get both the entrance and elevator ticket and we've calculated the amount before proceed to the ticketing counter. However, when I received the change, it's all in small notes of 5000 and 1000 VND so I spent some time to check it, standing beside the ticketing counter while she continued to sell tickets to the other tourists. It turned out that she shortchanged me by 50,000VND. When I asked her about it, she immediately return the 50,000 VND without even counting the amount she gave to me previously. As we're walking towards the elevator, we overheard a couple got shortchanged as well and had to claim it back. The mountain view is decent, however the dishonest people made the trip to marble mountain not as enjoyable.

This is a place to visit if you have never seen a cave or been up on a hill that has a good view, or if you want to be around a lot of other tourists. Yes, it is an interestingly shaped actual marble "mountain" but it is so touristy that it is not appealing. Too many vendors selling the same things, too many people just trying to get a good picture, and for some strange reason there was Spanish sounding music playing on speakers as you walked through the area. Although this was not an expensive place to stop, I still wouldn't recommend it.

We went on a typical rainy day. Be aware you have to pay entry, and elect for an elevator ticket (which is one way and you pay again to go down). Typical in Vietnam, they make money out of tourists at every opportunity. The hikes are steep, and in the wet very unsafe. The views and sights are no big deal if you are well travelled. Outside, rude stall owners try to sell you souvenirs and mutter about you if you say no. Drive past and don’t bother.

its a nice site with some good views, but by adding the lift to the top, the place is easily accessible to everyone and for large parts of the year you will have to share the small temple complex with thousands of people.  We ended up being there about 20 minutes before we wanted to escape....took 1-2 photos then left as fast as possible.The pity of it is that if the site were not so busy or just properly managed it would still probably be quite nice. If you do go, I would suggest going at dawn or dusk when the place is a bit quieter

Unfortunately all the tourist go there. One has to get tickets to go to the top with the elevator and one to get down. The ticket persons are very unfriendly, probably fed up with all the tourists... Annoying the vendors at the entrance who are pretty persistent getting you to buy a souvenir!

We had the spontaneous decision to visit the marble mountains as we took the bus from Na Nang to Hoi An.In hindsight, it was a bad decision.The entry fee of 40'000 dong is somewhat ok and the 15'000 dong for using the elevator as well. However, the views aren't that great and it's a tourist trap. Besides the great caves there is nothing else we enjoyed. There are fake stones all over this place, the place is covered with trash and advertisement and even the benches are branded by coca cola. Also, there are speakers all over this place playing loud disney music making the stay less than unenjoyable.If you want to enjoy a calm and nice view of Vietnam, don't go up this mountain.

Views are mediocre in comparison to most other mountains on offer in Vietnam. It is set up as a tourist trap and is heavily crowded. The views are mostly of cleared land and construction with sub par ocean views. Only good thing about it was the amount of stairs which was good to work of some of the delicious cuisine on offer in Vietnam

Lots of steps even though we used and pay for the lift there are still hundreds of steps to walk up when you're at the top,Most of the other monuments and caves when you reach them that there are limited and quite boring,so honestly it was not what we were expecting and I would consider not visiting!

The mountain and buildings were absolutely amazing to look at BUT too many tourists were let in at the same time and there were too many tour guides with LOUD MICROPHONES AND SPEAKERS for their tour groups. Although there were signs asking all visitors to be quiet and respectful these tour guides were definitely not which completely ruined the ambiance and ruined our visit. Very disappointed.

Mixed reviews on this one,  it is very beautiful and scenic.  Please make sure to wear great shoes, the stairs get extremely slippery in the rain having many marble stairs.Downsides were the fact you start getting harassed by local merchants from the second you get out of the taxi.Likewise the mountain had garbage all over as tourists really abuse this magnificent natural wonder.

Took the lift up the mountain so I have nothing to say about climbing up.Once up the mountain top, you get a great view of the city and sea, but nothing grand or breathtaking.Quite a few Buddha at the top and you can purchase joss sticks here.Do not come if you have difficulty walking across rough terrain.

I guess the mountain is nice...but the queues and the heat and the crappy touristy atmosphere killed it for me and i would have to say it was the least enjoyable part of our visit to Vietnam. Unless you enjoy being squeezed in with masses of people and being harrassed by hawkers give it a miss.
The visit was spoilt for us by being short changed twice at the official ticket booths, and by being pestered for money in the caves. It was an amazing place but

We stopped here on our way from Hue to Hoi An. Our driver dropped us off for 90-120 minutes - whatever it was, it felt like too long. Some of the caves, statues and views are pretty, but bad music emanating from speakers hidden in bushes and obnoxious tour groups exacerbated my growing temple fatigue. Perhaps having a guide would make the experience more enjoyable - we didn't really know what we were looking at half of the time, but we didn't really care. If you do go here, wear sturdy shoes. And bring water if you don't want to deal with aggressive vendors.

Its another tourist place. As said,  theres no choice for "good price" places to go..... if you want a bit if exercise to burn the breakfast fat. Then, walk up the stairs, dont take the "lift".  Entrance fees per pax 80,000 dong.  You can climb up the path to "heaven's gate" and proceed to "higest peak",  relatively good views around. Have a coconut drink 35,000 when u come down.
We have travelled for 1 month and was just very boring I found. I don't know what it is about it but we really didn't enjoy it
A large hill with Buddhas and lot of steep steps. A lift takes you up and down for  a small fee. Disappointing.

The caves are largely temples dedicated to different gods/ goddesses and the area is scattered with pagodas and different marble statues. Amazing view at the peak of the marble mountain for sunset, but other than that, not that much to see. I spent about an hour.

Many steps to climb and would not recommend for those who do not have good knees. Not much to see up in the top so it makes you think not worthwhile to climb the stairs. So when I went to Da Nang again in 2016_12, I did not go there again. A much better alternative is to go to Hai Van Pass while you are on the way to Hue.

This is clearly a place to avoid. You get the impression that an old marble mine has been transformed to make it look like a Buddhist temple travestied with some Hindu motives... Outside you can read that this was a place used by the Vietcong to launch attacks against the american imperialism... all too ubiquitous to be real

We stopped off on the way to Hui to see the marble mountains. We had a wonder and went in the caves but it was nothing to write home about. Make sure you wear appropriate footwear as the marble steps are steep and slippy when wet plus if you want to go into the caves then it involves climbing.
It's the most boring part of our trip.  I really don't see the point of visiting a recently built concrete tower and an average Chinese temple.

On the way to HanOi, we dropped by DaNang. I was going there with high hope to see marbles in the mountain caves as the name suggests.  However the one we went to was filled with sculptures of the after life portraying the journey to hell of Buddhism belief. Didn't like the feeling of it all, dark and scary. I felt commercialisation has destroyed the natural beauty of the place. So sad! Had I known earlier this was the 'scenery' I was getting , I wouldn't even bother to pay the entrance fee to get in, though wasn't much.

Steps very slippery. Quite muddy in areas which made the steps even more dangerous. One walk through the back of a cave you needed to be a rock climber. But once you started you were committed to keep going. Hand rings to help the climb were broken off in places so I found it quite distressing but had to keep going. Once you had climbed there was good views but you had to be very careful of the walking. Rubbish a lover the area. Steps down were extremely slippery. Didn't go and look at the rest of the caves after the first experience and I am a fit and daring person. Wouldn't bother going ☹️

The nature of the marble mountains was nice. once upon a time. now it's a stretch of industrial areas. the only part worth while is the area where the telescope is (that you need to pay for). I truly believe this place is built by the local government (slammed some statues against a mountain, lack of soul and relevance) to have something else to offer in Da Nang then beach. Don't recommend to go.

Marble M is worth a visit but be prepared - though interesting it is crass. Worth the climb and exploring some caves and a walk around the area. That's all in my view. Locals are pushy. Sure it their livelihood but they not aware their heavy antics are an anti sell. It has the opposite affect - it drives tourists away. I have heard many scam stories - of innocents being robbed blind. It is a trap most tourists fall for - buying stuff and putting it on the mantelpiece upon arriving home - and being appalled at how tacky it looks - and wondering why they bought this kitch statue that weighed a tonne.

I have to say that I was really disappointed to find out how fake this place is. The Buddhas and pagodas are beautiful and right up my street plus the views are amazing, but there's no getting away from the fact that most of it has been recently added or the original has been knocked down to make way for something prettier.I was looking forward to the caves especially, but again the Buddhas inside them are concrete and recent additions. There is music coming from speakers made to look like rocks and lots of fake marble.There is a lift up, but be careful with the steps coming back down...steep, uneven and slippery.Naff. Not sure what else to say.

If you have an interest in the innumerable designs and possibilities that 'sculpting' marble can produce and/or wish to buy any of this stuff for your garden/home it may be worth a visit otherwise it is just an unexceptional pleasant temple up a steep climb.

Unfortunately you have to pay to enter, pay to go up the elevator,  pay for a map  even if you don't ask for one then pay to come down if you are unable to walk down. You need to allocate at leadt 1 to 2 hours. Overalll not a big deal if you don't see it.
Very hot with people hawking their marble knick knacks.  Lots of trash everywhere, not really worth the stop.

We've been travelling for 6 months now so I don't know if we've been spoilt by other caves and view points in other countries, but this was a total disappointment. It's basically something of nothing. Don't waste your time or money.
You need to pay to go up and the view was not that great. Unfriendly staff because there is not much foreigners in Danang.
I did not enjoy this attraction at all. It was not particularly safe either we ended up at the top of a rock where I don't think we should of been because there was no staff on hand.

Went with our 1.5 year old and a stroller. Took the lift up (which is additional charge). A lot of very steep stairs that required to leave the stroller aside. The view is not extremely special. The caves themselves are nice, but wouldn't recommend with younger kids
An excuse for people to sell you marble sculptures. We were led in and quickly left. Even out guide said climbing the marble mountain was not that interesting so we left.

Don't spend time and cost to visit this mountain where us nothing great and special for exploring. Just a normal mountain display all the statue made by marble and has a Chinese temple for praying. Just need spend 15 minutes to finished tour.

A mountain included several temple. You should use good shoes to visit everywhere. There are so many stone seller.If you want to buy something on stone check the material with lighter.(stone never burned with fire)

In our opinion there is better things you can do with your time in Hoian and Danang. The caves and the mountain doesn't offer much as a tourist attraction. The Buddhist sites are dirty and rundown. The is trash everywhere and the scenery is much better at The Lady Buddha in Danang.

This has been highly recommended by all local tour agents in DA NANG. They claimed this to be an "MUST SEE" sight in DA NANG !!! The MARBLE MOUNTAINS is situated between DA NANG and HOI AN. In fact, if one travels by coach to HOI AN from DA NANG, the tour agent will throw in the Marble Mountains sight for free! There are some marble carvings on top of a small hill with a temple. Entrance fee is required and additional fee for using the elevator to go to the top; else one has to walk up. Uneven MARBLES are used on the steps; and this becomes very slippery and "DANGEROUS" when wet !!! No hand rails !!! There are some caves up the hill; but unable to visit, as the tour agents will allow very short time for "HAVE BEEN" tour! Some shops around and sell marble carvings, but the workmanship ispoor as compared to CHINESE carvings ! Visit it for the sake of "HAVE BEEN" !!!

The bus journey there was the most exciting thing.Get the local bus from just outside Hoi An only £1 each much cheaper than bikes, taxi or tour. It'll drop you off at the top of tourist trap road and from there you can get into the complex.Every shop on tourist trap road sells the same rubbish - if you want to pay £800 to ship home a massive Buddha statue this is the place for you.The steps up to the Mountains aren't that bad if you're young and fit. You'll be crowded out by Chinese tourists taking selfies and panting halfway up.The most authentic bit about the mountains is the smaller Buddha which is faded and worn other than that this place is set up to generate money. Fair enough if you like that sort of thing but you can go round it in half an hour.

Ugh, this place was a disaster! I would definitely not recommend going there. The stairs aren't a hastle; there aren't many of them. But I swear, the groups I were stuck behind had never used stairs in their life. So so slow. But okay, I got past that. The views weren't anything spectacular, and the shrines were 'so so'. You see many while in Asia; I need something extraordinary now. The caves were a let down too. The main cave at the top was CHAOTIC; it took us over 20 minutes to make our way down because no one was stopping to let us back down (one way in, one way out). If you are like us, and have somewhere to be ... Do not even attempt it. It's not worth it.Maybe I came here during a busy time ... But that was enough to put me off for a long time.

We took a car here on the recommendation of Vietnamese locals.The drive was the best part of the experience passing interesting paddies and views of buffalo. On arrival buying tickets is very confusing and the staff were the most unhelpful we have come across in Vietnam .You need different tickets for various visits.There is not a lot to see .Lots of ordinary caves that were so full of tourists that any spiritual feelings soon left you.We have seen far better temples in Hoi An or Saigon.The geology is interesting but that is about it.The area is full of cheap tacky stalls and it is very untidy Very sad also to see the developments along the Da Nang coast.Stay in Hoi An so much more to offer.
Was dissapointed with the place, nothing much to discover and all the caves are pretty much the same. Not coming back for sure.
Yes it's five hills of Karst uplift hills which have some caves in them. If you have never seen caves before then this might be for you.

There are not too many tourist things to do in Danang, so spending 5 days there I think we felt obliged to do Marble Mountain. We were able to walk there from our Hotel.We took the elevator up and then walked around for maybe 90 minutes. We felt the place was a bit run down and poorly maintained considering its a main tourist attraction. Like many of the other reviewers comments, there was nothing too special here.The heat and humidity was intense so I suggest you go early in the morning. Even you take the elevator up you will still need to climb and descend many many steps.

This was the least interesting place we've visited. If you've ever seen a cave before or climbed up a mountain then you will disappointed. The bottom is just a road full of marble shops and show rooms so not even a nice area to visit and look down on. Lots of yours include a stop here but if you can avoid it I wouldn't bother visiting.

Not sure why anyone would stop off here unless they were a Man U footballer or Kanye West fitting out one of their mansions. The carvings are largely more status symbols than Michel Angelo's David and the mountain is a huge slab of marble,  it may have resembled a mountain 100 years ago.  If you need  a toilet break on the way from Hue to Hoi An then stop by.

It's 15000 to VND to enter and then 15000 VND to use the lift (recommended!) lots of steps around the entire site. Some parts weren't very spectacular but the last cave was interesting. Outside of the site people will try and get you to go to their shop, one lady even rode up next to me on my scooter encouraging me to go into hers. The car park is 3000 VND for bikes but if your late back they ask for more! Wear good walking shoes and take a drink, the site is large with lots of steps up and down!

Just a word of  caution if you intent to purchase items to be shipped back to Australia , there are a considerable fees that not explained at time of purchase, a recent example for $1,000 purchase incurred fees of equal amount to receive the goods at home. The seller also states they will put a lower price so you don't pay as much, this is misleading and you will pay full fees & taxes on proof of your purchase at time of pick up from customer bond store.

Build a elevator in the middle of nature? Are you kidding me? They destroyed the own beauty of the mountain already.If you go by bike/ motor, be careful with the staff at the parking. They are always mark up the parking fee although they have a tariff.This place is the most disappointing place I have ever been in Da Nang.

All drivers on the road between Danang and Hoi An will try to talk you into visiting this place because they receive commissions from shop owners where they will drop you off. This should already warn you about this place: it s all about extracting as much money out of tourists as possible. The view from the top is interesting (but any highrise hotel in Danang is as good). The pagodas and caves are also interesting but the place becomes crowded to tourists and is anything by temple like. Just tell your driver that you have already been and keep going. Avoid.
Moderately interesting climbing up inside for a a look, but the coloured light features inside are so unnecessary, very hokey, and belong (maybe are from) the 1970's.  Almost comical.

We made the mistake of buying an entry ticket to the entrance where people climb up the mountain.  We thought we were going to catch the lift but apparently we were sold the wrong tickets!  So up we went ... climbing up the mountain via the steepest stairs I'd ever climbed - with little security and safety measures!  Once we were at the top, we soon realised that the only way down was the same way we came up!  It was not a pleasant climb up or down to be honest.  You are given a map of the different areas to visit in the mountain, but it's nearly impossible to work out where everything really is.  To make matters worse, no taxi would take us back to our hotel as they considered it too close to make it worth their while.  So we had to walk back in the searing heat for around 30 minutes, kids in tow.  Not our best experience haha.

We were on route to Hoi An and stopped over at the Marble Mountain. It's a very touristic place and not very noteworthy. If you are in the neighborhood go see it, otherwise, just skip it. Nothing gained, nothing lost.

Rubbish, hawkers, tacky lights, extension cords etc were a real let down for what probably used to be a gorgeous place! Lots of shrines, statues, temples, butterflies & beautiful flowers to see but you just have to overlook the commercial crap, rubbish & inauthentic objects along the way!?!
Nothing interesting to see. It was also a very very hot day, and that made us really looking forward to go back to the hotel.

Total waste of time if you're staying less than a week in the area.It's kitch, tacky, and full of rubbish objects you remember seeing at your grand parent's house.Better to stay 3 more hours at the beach, in Hoi

In spite of the beauty of nature and of this attraction, there seems to be ZERO organisation here; you have to buy a ticket to enter the cave, which is terribly dark and the steps are horrendously slippery; then ANOTHER ticket to take the elevator to visit the mountains; then ANOTHER ticket to enter the mountains. I mean, the tickets are not expensive at all, but it still feels like a rip off, especially when you have bought the entrance ticket and the sellers still make you buy another/other entrance ticket(s)...
There is really nothing to see here. Basically you'll just see different vases, statues that are made from marbles etc and sell it to you.

We stopped here on route from Hue to Hoi An and I have to say it was a strange experience. You go up an elevator on the side of the mountain, and are then able to walk through a badly mapped route of strange plastic statues and small empty pagodas. There were a few points of interest like the huge marble Buddha and one of the caves was interesting but there are odd shrines everywhere with plastic dolls again. Also we found out from our guides that none of the marble being sold around the mountain is actually from Vietnam, it is mostly shipped in from Pakistan so a souvenir was pretty pointless. Personally I think there are better places to see, it's like people have just built random stuff on the mountain to make it a tourist attraction.
That's it a view of the sea and the terrible development of what should be a beautiful coastline.  One sign said a wholly owned US company so we know who'll be spoiling such a striking coast.
Don't waste your time here. Most likely your driver will convince you to stop here. He will receive a commission from the business.nothing worthwhile to buy or shop
Trash everywhere, lots of tourist crap at the bottom for purchase and a few Buddhas/a pagoda at the top. We took the steps rather than the elevator. Not worth the 15,000 entry fee.

Our guide took us here when we got off the plane in Danang. We were quite impressed until he explained that 95% is new -it has been made for the tourists! We listened to other tourists impressed with the historic buildings fully unaware of the modernity. All the statues are concrete. We were then taken to a marble factory that hassled us to buy. It was their job but I became very unsure of the authenticity of the products.Unfortunately Vietnam is full of fakes and scams -very sad because most people are wonderful. Even our guides confessed they were never sure what was genuine.

A bit lack lustre in my opinion. We were over-charged by the ticket sellers. It is 15,000 for the elevator and 15,000 for the cave plus they snuck in the map booklet which costs an additional 15,000. Had we of known they were including this in the price at the time we would have opted out. When leaving, we approached the elevator and were informed that we had to purchase an additional ticket to get to the bottom (15,000vdn). I asked the elevator assistant if there was another way down and he said no. Having now read other reviews there areClearly steps you can take but I guess his advice was another money grabbing idea.There is a decent view from the top but not quite worth going out of your way for and definitely not worth adding Da Nang to your itinerary if this is the main place you are interested in visiting.

After fighting our way through the vendors,we discovered that the lift was out of action.Being  a wrinkly with walking difficulties I took one look at the steep uneven steps leading up the mountain we decided that it was not for us.A further visit produced the same outcome,they really need to get their act together if they want to attract visitors to this,one of few attractions in the area

It´s ok to go there and see the mountains but do not by anything - it´s very expensive and not the real thing. Trust me, I bought two lions and one of them lost a paw very quickly.. Poor quality in my opinion. In fact, it´s better to just drive by and see the mountains from a distance. They have women "hijack" you on the road, and says things like "Oh, you are a good driver" and then they ask you if you are going to the mountains - and if you say yes you are trapped. They "help" you and very soon after you had seen what you are there to see you are in a shop they led you to and if you are like me, you by overpriced things. A Vietnamese girl we spoke to later on said to us "Oh, don´t by anything there.. Well, that was to late for me.. =)

I have been there several times and have to admit, that now I do not go there any more, as it is a nightmare with all the sellers running around offering the best price for you and free transport where ever you want the stuff.
It would be peaceful sans the crowds. Very touristy but nice pogodas. Be prepared for walking and steep stairs.
Maybe we just have too many of this in Malaysia. The view to us is just nothing special. Not as good as what the people rating here. Lot of steps to climb to get to the cave temple.

It is awful, I only give it 2 stars as one of the caves is reasonably good and the views are nice. Firstly when we arrived by motorbike everyone tries flagging you down to make you park in their shop for "free" but of course you are then told u need to buy at least a drink on your return. When you come to pay the ticket office try and scam you in every way possible, firstly they tried charging 110,000 dong per person until we pointed to sign which said 60,000. They then tried giving us back our change 100,000 dong short of what we were owed. No doubt they'll do this all day to the hundreds of tourists and earn themselves a nice bundle of extra cash. The mountain itself has a bunch of average caves, and a number of shops selling very over priced drinks which you desperately need due to the steep steps and hot humid weather. Even if you say no to them 5 times they'll still ask you repeatedly until you out of their sight. Don't waste you time going here, it's a tourist trap and a waste of money.

The place itself was interesting. But the staff spoiled it - maybe they think the tourists spoil it! To be fair, it was siesta time so maybe they were just tired. You have to walk along souvenir-lined streets to get there so be ready. It cost 100,000 dong ($5US / AUD) for entry to marble mountain elevator and pagoda.

Perhaps it was our guide who was more interested in getting us to spend money in one of the many shops selling marble items that many us lose interest in going up the lift to the pagoda at the top. It seemed to be a terrible tourist trap - and especially as very little, if any, of the marble comes from these mountains now.

Right from arriving and getting dropped off at a marble factory this trip was only going to be about the locals trying to sell marble. Some fantastic pieces and cheap compared to Australia!Save your time and enjoy the beach

Like other reviewers have described, we were taken to Marble Mountain by our driver en-route to Hoi An, and like other reviewers have described, the driver drove into a pre-designated spot outside a shop where we were immediately given the hard sell as soon as we got out of the car.  Unphased by this, we took a walk towards the entrance to path to the pagoda, to see what the strange structure was on the side of the hill.  Realisation then dawned that this beautiful landscape had been blighted by a huge concrete elevator to take people to the top of this (quite small) incline.  Towering over the top of the hill in all it's concrete glory, this monstrosity assists the less able or willing to enjoy the views previously rewarded to those that had made the climb.  You will gather from this that I was not in favour of the elevator or whoever's decision it was to destroy the landscape with it's presence.  Such a shame!  Otherwise, some nice shops if you want to buy marble, a lot of hassle is you don't.

We expected mountains but they are more like short rock hills which you could drive by and not realise...the giveaway are all the large statue shops on the main rd. the lift to the pagoda is ugly and spoils the look of the pagoda in a landscape shot. Very hard sell by everyone and really nothing to write home about. I would not choose to stop again and spend any time there. I would not spend an hour there. Sorry totally underwhelmed by this site.

The Marble Mountains site is the classic "local place of interest" that has been overblown by tour guides and commercialized to bring in as many tourists as possible. The street leading to the site and the entrance is lined with vendors selling everything you can imagined carved into marble. The problem is that the prices are ridiculous and to add insult to injury, much of the stone they are sell is not even real rock but a painted hard plastic. We saw many examples of crudely carved chuncks of stone priced at $70 to $90 US that you see in the airport for $5 US. On top of this many of the vendors are very aggressive. There is also lots of garbage laying around, especially along the pathways near the top of the hills where the caverns are.
From Dang you have to choices .1)take moto bike about 15 minutes between Danang and Hoi An. 2 0 you can get taxi there .In my opinion : no thing specialy

This is billed as a significant tourist spot. We took the lift to the top and walked down through the temple area. Unfortunately the grounds here are very heavily littered with rubbish and this really detracts from what could be a pleasant space. The approach road is cluttered with shops selling stone carving and the general air is run-down. Could be okay if they used rubbish bins, collected the litter and tidied up the approach area.
So on your way to Hoi An (a must) look to  your right as you drive by...then check this off your list. You have visited the Marble Mountains!

Don't get yourselves into one of the Marble shops where they try to sell you anything they want. The trip up the mountain and in the caves is ok but only if you pass by on the road from Danang to Hoi An. Would not recommend going there on a half day trip.

Only go here if you have extra time to kill. Really nothing to see just a so so pagoda but the depiction of heaven and hell was interesting. But be sure to understand if you take a tour or taxi driver they will try to stop at many overpriced marble shops which look legitimate. Rather purchase such items at the exit of the mountain and realize 50% of asking price is realistic of selling price to local Vietnamese.
She turns on you if she thinks you haven't tipped her enough when you are trying to leave...scary. Then the marble souvenir people are determined to sell something,anything to you.
after arriving there we decided not to go up. below they sell a lot of marble  that is supposedly from china (which at least helps to conserve the mountain).

I don't realy feel like there was anything worth seeing here. The pagoda on top is not anything in particular and the caves are not either. It can be seen in other places in Vietnam, where it is much nices.

This is my 5th year to Vietnam - in particular, Hoi An. I've never had an urge to go to Marble Mountain but as I am here with my partner and it is his first time we decided to go there.Firstly, it is far out from most things, our taxi trip was roughly 250,000VND each way. When you get there, the first thing you see is hundreds of marble shops all selling the same thing and wanting you to purchase CRAPPY souveiners. Most of the marble in these shops is imported from China anyway, so we didn't waste our money.We got the lift up to the top of the mountain as we had little interest in walking up. This is fairly cheap - 45,000VNDpp up and down. Once up there we quickly noticed how dirty it is. There is rubbish everywhere, particularly water bottles and food wrappers, it takes away from much of the mountains beauty. Also there are very few signs in English which makes it hard to navigate your way around - you can see signs for the next upcoming attraction where you are, but there are no signs of how to get back down etc. We found a cave and started climbing it, it's very slippery, so DO NOT WEAR THONGS! We then had a Vietnamese lady come and show us around - she came out of no where and by reading previous reviews before going I was well aware she wanted money. She didn't do much for us, just bossed us around and took a few photos, regardless, we appreciated the help so we paid her 100,000VND ($5AUD). We thought this was plenty considering she didn't do much and we didn't ask for her to come along. She was VERY RUDE however and demanded another 100,000 for "2 people" and we refused, saying that we didn't ask for her help and that it was rude of her to ask for more. She got very angry and yelled something at us we couldn't understand so we walked off. After spending about 2 hours looking at temples and what not we decided to leave as there are hundreds of stairs and everything looks the same. We couldn't find any signs back to the lift so we asked and no one understood - we therefore ended up walking back down.It's also hard to get a taxi when down the bottom as there are very few there.All up I wouldn't waste my time or money every going back and would suggest to give it a miss, particularly if you are tight for time and are not fit or don't want to walk up and down lots of stairs.
Boring and nothing but a 'hard sell' of inferior marble.
only taken to visit shops selling marble products.
Only the view at the top is OK walk to the right of the lift to find the stairs to go up and down .

We were bombarded by people pushing their wares or asking for money. We also purchased a double ticket and when we finished looking around we went to go back down we were told we needed to purchase another ticket. Not a pleasant experince.

the way we were treated  by our driver and his "friends" who operated a marble sales operation was just disgusting. We hired a car via our hotel  and once at the area we were driven straight into a garage of the marble goods seller. We were all but held prisoner and given the hard sell on their wares. When we demanded to be allowed to go to lift we were marched past all other stales by these people. A younger member of the selling team even stayed with us for the whole walk around the wonderful area and kept answering many calls to report our where a bouts . When we found the steps back down the girl all but refused to allow us using the stairs and was demanded we use the lift. Once down the stairs by magic here was our car. We were then driven past all the stalls and straight back to the first garage and the awaiting intimidating sellers eager to see us. When we refused to leave the car and demanded to be returned to our hotel all turned on us with verbal abuse. Beware!!!

Don't be fooled by the name. The "Marble Mountains" is not a mountain range, but a rather small mountain surrounded by shops selling marble. If you like tacky statues of lions and buddhas that are made of something that closely resembles (but probably isn't) pure marble, then this is the place for you. If not, you'd better drive on to Hoi An without slowing down.

My partner and I hired a car to take us from Hue to Hoian. We stopped at Marble Mountains and ended up at a marble factory. The mountains are easily visible from the road as is the pagoda. I rec admiring from afar and not spending too much time at the Marble Mountains.

Prinzipiell kann es da bestimmt ganz cool sein, aber hier ist auf engem Platz leider völlig überlaufen. Auf keinen Fall den Aufzug nehmen, den nehmen fast alle, dann hast du a.) eine lange Warteschlange und b.) ist auf dieser Seite noch deutlich mehr los… nimm die Treppen,  nach maximal 5min bei geringer Anstrengung ist man oben. Man hat oben auch Toiletten und Getränkeverkauf, schöne Aussichten und mehrere Höhlen. Keinesfalls über das Heaven-Gate gehen, 1-2 Idioten die nicht gehen können und du steckst bei den Menschenmassen fest

Short scooter ride from Danang or Hoi An.As soon as you enter the main street leading to the entrance you are harassed by Hawkers trying to get you to park at their stalls. Its made a bit harder by not knowing where the entrances are to go up to the Mountain.So we paid for parking 5K VND (60c) lol and were harassed to buy crap from vendors at the bottom. An older Vietnamese lady was helpful with pointing us to the entrance. (Remember this for later)Ticket price to go up and explore mountain reasonable - 40K VND This was easy enough, bring cash.Site lacks any sort of explanatory signs to give visitors a better understanding of the area - the main signage are boards which show where you are and where you can walk.Again once up top on the mountain you'll be harassed to buy useless trinkets from vendors and cold drinks (even had my own water in my hands and wanted to sell me more? ) lol.Exploring the various caves is very cool and is interesting but again zero information or sign boards to explain anything there to give you an understanding of the history of the area.Nice views from the top! If you've got kids don't let them roam free some deathly drop offs at some of the lookouts.On the way out via Gate 2 you are forced to walk through all street vendors selling crap again and guess what !Same lady that showed us the entrance was there expecting us to buy stuff from here shop.My wife thinks I'm being overly negative but I disagree, have to post this stuff to teach people who may not be as well versed travelling in south east Asian countries.For all the above reasons only a 2☆ for this one! Go but just be prepared to be harassed constantly here too! Enjoy! 😀

A frustrating visit to the marble mountains ruined by the pushy vendors at the entrance and tour groups milking around at the top all trying to sort out their return elevator tickets . The way to purchase tickets is not entirely clear and you are not permitted to read or ascertain how to do this without being interrupted. You have the choice of two sets of stairs (40,000 VnD) or an elevator (additional 20,000Vnd each way) to get to the top. Because we knew no better we walked up the stairs to the right of the elevator / not a difficult climb - took less than 10 minutes). However this ends up putting you in the thick of the tour groups who have used the elevator. Much better would be to use the stairs to the left of the elevator - about 100 meters walk away. These lead you to the better caves and views anyway. Once up on top it’s all the usual Vietnamese mish mash of temples, caves and water sellers but it’s absolutely fine. Once finished have a walk around the local streets to see the carved statues etc .

It is adventure to go up to the mountain. You take the lift or walk up. Just be careful as it is like 105 steps and every step is u even.Remember to buy ticket and map too. You will get few pieces of postcard.Scenery is really nice and you will see a lot of Buddha statues crafted in marble.

First a clear warning: you have to be fit and in good condition to make it. And it is probably going to be extremely moist, muddy and dirty and/or extremely hot and crowded. So dont pick the wrong shoes - not the white ones or with heels. There will be endless stairs and lots of climbing, water dripping from the ceiling and so on. And the stairs are a bit dangerous, in bad condition, have no railing, vary a lot in different sizes etc. So this is no trip for wimps.Caves are beautiful and some of the statues are - hmm, interesting. The place is pretending to be hell, so what can you expect. It also has served as Vietkongs hospital during the Vietnam war, so it definately has been the hell for many people in the history. Their arent any signs of this old purpose any more, though, which is a pitty.Lots of different kind buddha statues etc. which can be a bit overwhelming and frankly a bit boring after a while. Very, very touristry attraction, so not for nature seekers.
Nice experience. Must take the elevator because climbing the steps is difficult because it is long and steady.........

There are two places where you can get the tickets. In the first one, the entrance fee is 40,000 vnd (the mountains and Huyen Khong Cave) + 15,000 one way elevator. In the second one, they sell you the tickets to one of the caves separately, and it is 20,000 vnd. When you are up there, you can get one more ticket for the elevator down to the bottom, again 15,000 vnd. I would advise to get the elevator once (up) and then walk down on foot. If you go to the wrong queue to buy the tickets, the staff in both ticket booths gets unhappy and doesnt have patience to explain where you have to go to buy the tickets. It’s hot up there and the prices for water and snacks are higher, my advice is to take at least 2 bottles of water and not to buy it there. In Huyen Khong Cave there were the carvings and small shrines that show vivid scenes of Buddhist “hell.” They depict souls being punished for earthly sins: tongues pulled out, bodies boiled in cauldrons, and demons meting out gruesome justice. It’s both fascinating and chilling, a stark reminder of karmic consequences. Think twice if you go with children - they might be scared and you might need to explain this history to them. I saw much better caves, so I would not say this one is a must-see, but it’s quite nice to visit though.

Dunque, i 3 pallini sono perché di per se sarebbe anche interessante, ma girare per le marble mountains non è per niente intuitivo, nemmeno con mappa alla mano. Ciò che non aiuta affatto sono le varie mappe disolcate sul cammino senza la caratteristica puntina "You are here". Sostanzialmente sono inutili.  Inoltre si pensa di pagare l'ingresso al sito e invece paghi solo un pezzo, poi paghi l'ascensore. Poi paghi altri biglietti per visitare un'altra grotta, veramente confusionario. Poi ti rechi all'ascensore per scendere, e scopri che i 55.000 vnd a testa pagati precedentemente valevano solo per salire, se vuoi scendere devi pagare altri 55.000 vnd. Ho visto altri turisti un po' persi a girare in tondo. Mi sembra di aver buttato un sacco di tempo. Non mi sento di raccomandare un tour organizzato, in quanto i 2 punti salienti che menzionano tutti i tour siamo comunque riusciti a vederli (almeno quello)
Not for the elderly or those who cannot climb stirs ar walk long distances. Has historical importance and stunning caves.

Bon. Ce n'est pas que ça n'a pas d'intérêt. C'est une visite agréable. Toutefois en ce qui nous concerne, nos attentes étaient un peu plus élevées. Peut-être trop d'attentes de notre part. Ce n'est pas un site incontournable dans la région de Danang-Hoi An-Hue. Le site est très bien mais manque un peu de wow.

It is adventure to go up to the mountain. You take the lift or walk up. Just be careful as it is like 105 steps and every step is u even.Remember to buy ticket and map too. You will get few pieces of postcard.Scenery is really nice and you will see a lot of Buddha statues crafted in marble.

So dont pick the wrong shoes - not the white ones or with heels. There will be endless stairs and lots of climbing, water dripping from the ceiling and so on. And the stairs are a bit dangerous, in bad condition, have no railing, vary a lot in different sizes etc. So this is no trip for wimps.Caves are beautiful and some of the statues are - hmm, interesting. The place is pretending to be hell, so what can you expect. It also has served as Vietkongs hospital during the Vietnam war, so it definately has been the hell for many people in the history. Their arent any signs of this old purpose any more, though, which is a pitty.Lots of different kind buddha statues etc. which can be a bit overwhelming and frankly a bit boring after a while. Very, very touristry attraction, so not for nature seekers.

Bon. Ce n'est pas que ça n'a pas d'intérêt. C'est une visite agréable. Toutefois en ce qui nous concerne, nos attentes étaient un peu plus élevées. Peut-être trop d'attentes de notre part. Ce n'est pas un site incontournable dans la région de Danang-Hoi An-Hue. Le site est très bien mais manque un peu de wow.

Das Hotel ist etwas weiter vom Flughafen entfernt als es den Anschein macht. Es lohnt sich mit schwerem Gepäck einen Trolley zu nehmen. Sind schon 400 Meter zu gehen. Das Hotel ist sehr anonym, gross und unpersönlich. Die Zimmer ruhig trotz der Nähe zum Flughafen, leider lassen sich die Fenster nicht öffnen.

Ich bin mit einem Grab (Motorrad - 96.000 Dong) zu den marble mountains gefahren. Am Eingang sind unwahrscheinlich viele Händler. Der Eintritt kostet 55.000 Dong (Elevator extra 15.000 Dong). Ich bin hoch gefahren und runter gelaufen. Die Wegweiser and ich manchmal irritierend. Auf dem Gelände gibt es einige Tempel, Pagoden und Höhlen zu entdecken. In einer Höhle muss man ein klein wenig klettern. Regenbedingt war ich nicht lange bei den marble mountains - vielleicht 1,5 Stunde. Auch wenn es viel zu entdecken gab, würde ich die mountains nicht als Highlight bezeichnen. Nach den unzähligen Top-Bewertungen hatte ich mir die mountains imposanter vorgestellt.

If you have done a few temples, buddha's, pagoda's, etc, then you can probably miss this one.  There are much better ones to expereince in Vietnam/Asia.  I walked the complete thing in 1 hr, and took only 3 photo's (2 of the ocean) as there are good views from there.  Marble lady is much better to see.

The Marble Mountains included some beautiful scenery. Beware of the shop keepers at the base of mountain who draw you into their shops to buy marble statues. Lots of products are fake and they will try to overcharge you.

We went there in the evening so by the time we went through the caves it was dark and we couldn't enjoy anything. The steps are high and uneven at certain places. Also the lighting was poor. So it was a little challenging with the kids. But our tour guide was very helpful and kept her flashlight on during the climb. At regular intervals they take breaks and show you around which gives your body enough time to recover. So even my 6 year old could climb it with a little encouragement. I would request the management to improve the lighting around the path. But you can definitely do this once!

This is an OK way of spending a couple of hours.If you have a motorbike, the vendors can be a touch of a hassle,  but let them park your bike and buy a đ15000 bottle of water when you retrieve it.If you're fit (or mean), avoid the đ55,000 entrance and lift charge by using the path about 50m to the left of the lift.There're a couple of caves, a pagoda, and quite a few steps at the top.The cave at base is đ20,000 is probably worth ten minutes of your life.....

I visited Marble Mountain after hearing intriguing things about it, but I found it to be less impressive than expected. While it’s worth a visit if you have extra time, I wouldn’t consider it a must-see attraction.

The mountain consists of the 5 elements of the universe in Asia culture, consisting of the Kim (Iron), Mouc (Wood), Thuy (Water), Hoa (Fire) and Tho (Earth). Visitors here will discover the mysterious beauty of caves combing the sacred atmosphere of Buddhism and visit the Tam Thai Pagoda and the Linh Ung Pagoda.You can choose to climb up via the steep rock stairs or take the elevator up (one way is 15,000 dong) which we chose the latter. The view here is nice and you can feel the peace and tranquil. If you are into Buddhism, you will surely indulge in this place where you can get up and closed with the many statues of Buddha. And there's a cave behind the temple and is nice and serene.As the passage and stairways are made of uneven rock and bricks, do be very careful in walking around.Do take note that the elevator service closed at 5.00pm.There's also a small scale touristry street market near the main where you can grab souvenirs and gifts here.

The mountain is quite nice and a local landmark at Da Nang. There is nothing, you need to see for certain, but if you are fit and a good climber, it might be worth going here. Otherwise, do not risk anything as the mounting and dismounting of the location is very demanding, especially for seasoned people.

20,000 VND for admission. First few minutes are interesting as you walk in and see the giant cave. But as you go deeper into the mountain, there are statues of naked women being sacrificed and creatures killing people/children. No explanation for  the story is available. Daughter was a little freaked out so we left.

Here’s our honest review from our experience (family of 3 and our daughter is 8yo).I did some research based on the day tours and gathered their itineraries and decided it was easy for us to go on our own.We took a grab from Hoi An to the ticket booking office in front of Am Phu cave (between gate 1 and 2). We bought tickets to get into Am Phu cave and I didn’t know what to expect until we went in and noticed all these dark and disturbing sculptures. I overheard a tourist guide saying the ground areas and lower areas are “hell” and the stairs going up is “heaven”! No wonder it was called the “Hell Cave”. My 8yo daughter found the sculptures disturbing and didn’t continue exploring the ground/lower areas and we just did the tight and upright steps to the top.Next up, we went to see a pagoda. There were too many pagodas and my daughter would get bored seeing more than one pagoda! So based on my research, we did the famous and oldest Pagoda in Da Nang, which is Tam Thai pagoda. For this, we had to go to the elevator area and buy a separate tickets for a one-way elevator ride and entrance to the upper area (level 5). We followed the path and looked at the Tam Thai pagoda and a few quick snaps.Lastly, we went to Huyen Khong cave which is where you see the popular poster card photo of The Marble Mountains. We were very lucky as there was the sun shining through the gap at the top of the cave and the beautiful sun ray directing down to the ground was a full display. This was the highlight of this short trip!We finished up by walking down the stairs, back to the bottom to have a drink and catch a grab back to Hoi An.

Marble Mountains (Nui Ngoc Son) offer some interesting views and unique geological formations, but it's important to be prepared for a physically demanding hike.We visited on our way from Da Nang to Hoi An with our driver (PM if you want his contact info) because we had time to waste before checking in to our Hoi An hotel. Key Points:Fitness Level: The hike involves numerous steps and uneven terrain, so it's not suitable for those with mobility limitations or small children.Elevator Access: While you can take an elevator to the top, there's still significant walking and climbing required to explore the caves and temples.Touristy Atmosphere: Marble Mountains is a popular tourist destination, so expect crowds, especially during peak seasons. Overall: A fine way to fill some time but not really a destination itself.

I liked the marble mountains. The view from the top is amazing. The entire crafting is done with marble within the mountains. I had taken the lift, as it saved time and is more convenient. Better to make the trip early morning, as it is relatively cooler. We had planned marble mountains and bana hills on the same day and had just 1 hour for it. Bana hills is way better, so it makes more sense to reserve bana hills for a separate day.

It’s an ok attraction but nothing special.  It’s not a mountain at all, just a localised rock outcrop with a few small caves.  I was expecting it to be much better than it was and so I found it rather disappointing.
If you want an informative tour, ensure you go with a guide. Entry $ does NOT include your exit ticket. Lots of stairs!!!!

The marble mountain contains an anicent buddist temple with many statues built in caves. It is modified with new elements for tourism. To visit the caves and statues, visitors have to prepare to walk upstairs and go through narrow passage, which can be a bit demanding for elderly.

I started my Vietnam trip from the south and making my way up north. I was not disappointed with this attall and really enjoyed my day. Yes I did get harassed by the shop owner at front but I kindly declined, ignored her as I walked to the other side to walk up the mountain to view the caves. Note for you all if you want to try to avoid getting harassed to buy something then please do not get dropped off at gate A. Gate B was less harassment I thought so it’s better to be dropped off there. The reception lady tried to charge me for a map that was not needed but as I did again slightly decline as you do in Vietnam. Just stick to your guns and ignore their money snatching techniques. Other than that and overall the attraction was pleasant and spent 2 hours here.

The marble mountains are a decent attraction, but lots of locals trying to sell things to you. Lots of temples/shrines in the caves so if that’s what you like, great. Probably seen too many myself!!!!

We made it! It's a hike up and down uneven steps, not recommended for older folks like us. The 15,000 Dong elevator only goes halfway up the trek, so you'll still need to walk farther if you want to explore the place. Be forewarned that the elevator shuts down at 5 PM, so plan accordingly.

We joined a day tour during Tet to this Marble Mountain and a few other places from Da Nang. Everything was included in our tour fees including a hefty Tet surcharge (but excluding extra tips for the tour guide and bus driver which is expected).  I think it is worthwhile to join a  tour as you will not be harrassed by parking or touting if you go by yourself.  Everything was smooth with a reputable tour. I give it a 3 star though because the steps are huge, slippery and dangerous and it is not just a few steps but hundreds of steps up and down like around 600 or more altogether. For those with short or weak legs (small kids and elderly) it is not advisable to climb but there is a lift service with an additional fee which is reasonable at 10,000 VND. If you like temples you may appreciate this place but if you don't there is nothing much to look at except for its history and how it survived the war.  There is a scenic view from the lookout point on top of the mountain.  If you want to buy genuine marble crafts you should buy them from the factory stores nearby the mountain.  Our guide brought us to one of the factories nearby for a 5-minute toilet break but didn't give us time to buy anything from there although I saw some skilled marble crafts that are hand made that are worth buying for that price. I still regretted not having it.  If you are interested about real marble crafts do go to the factories around that area but not the shops in Marble Mountain for they sell cheap souvenirs.

Nice place..but its a real shame about all the garbage left lying around. Why do people think it's ok to just leave your trash wherever you want? Too bad the administration does not see fit to provide a few more garbage cans. Supposed to be a National Treasure but not treated as such. Kinda ruined it for me.

Fomos conhecer as Marble Mountains, fomos de Crab. Bastante interessante essa montanha calcarea que contém diversos pequenos Templos. Agora as escadas sejam subindo ou descendo são muito altas cansa bastante.

Everything is quite spread out and so many different caves and pagodas to visit - it takes hours to get through all of them so we picked a few we had on our must see list based on online images and took our time at each of them. I would really recommend climbing the very steep very narrow stairs inside the cave to the top - a bit scary and quite slippery, especially when coming back down, so wear sturdy shoes with good grip and hold on as you go. Quite impressive with great views from the top of Marble Mountain.There were quite a few people in the main cave and in a few of the more popular ones (a bit touristy) but it is quite a big area and we had times where there were no or very few people around. Lots of stairs - we had to pace ourselves.Worth the visit but you're basically paying 3 times; 1st for the cave, 2nd to go up the mountain and the 3rd time to use the lift that goes up the mountain (no choice) and they're not upfront about it.  They just sell you the ticket and when you get to the next spot, you're told you need another ticket and when you get to the lift you need another ticket, (having to queue again each time) instead of being upfront with signage or by simply letting us know how it works so we did not have to queue and pay 3 times.  Leaves a bad taste in the mouth. Leaving the mountain we were absolutely harrrassed by some of the taxi drivers following us around, being in our faces, interrupting our conversation and physically dragging us towards their taxis.  I realise they all just need to make a living, but they would not take no for an answer even when we told them that we already had a driver organised. Quite unpleasant and annoying. We had some wow moments and we had some less than impressed moments. It was nice enough all up but the ticketing and harrassing taxi drivers took away from the overall experience.

Je suis venu par taxi, hélas pas au bon comptoir de tickets. Le numéro 1 n'en vend pas et le personnel ne parle pas anglais. Je trouve l'autre bureau ou on vous remets des tickets et un plan sans explications . Il faut faire la queue pour l'ascenseur. A 11.00 du matin compter 40 minutes. En haut ce trouve quelques temples et grottes sans aucuns intérêts.  Seule la grotte principale en fin de circuit vaut la visite. Ici aussi prevoir une visite a l'aube ou en fin de journée.

Плюс- расположение в городе, я дошла, прогуливаясь от пляжа в южном направлении ( гору с лифтом очень хорошо видно). Затем с пляжа - по пустырю, тропинке, а там выйти через ворота, завязанные на веревочку, на дорогу. Есть 2 входа: ближайший, с лифтом, и подальше, пешком, второй дешевле на 15 тыс и предпочтительнее. Чего- то примечательного не увидела- вид на море( пустырь, по которому я шла), город; но с колёса обозрения вид лучше. Скалы смешные, если вы их ещё где- либо видели, прогулочные зоны скромные; единственный для меня интересный момент- пролезть по лазу на смотровую площадку ( с младенцами и пожилые поднимаются туда же по лестнице)

It was nice but not very special. A lot of stairs, sometimes a little bit slippery. There are several caves. Nice to walk in, have a quick look but nothing more than that. We have 2 kids in the age of 2 and 4. We took the elevator to go up and went with the stairs down.
Average, i would not go out of my way to make a special journey here thats for sure, caves ok. Steps slippery in the wet.
It was a horrible day,  it rained all day the mountain was flooded. I am sure it would be lovely if it wasn't raining and it wasn't slippery
The place is very steep and the only thing to see is the cave.I recommend comfortable shoes and ensure that just take the steps.Don’t buy tickets for elevator, they are just a scam.

Is lots of stairs and steps (slippery when rain) caution when climbing up, some of the statues are poorly done. Would recommend to take the lift up to explore more , including the highest point where there is a 360 view of Danang, you need to buy ticket to go up and also buy the ticket at the too to come down.

エレベーターがあるので楽勝かと思いきや、着いてからも結構階段の上り下りやきついです。夏場だとかなりバテます‥。雨降った後とかは滑りやすいので（大理石の急な階段など）、靴は滑りにくいものを。最初に地上のチケット売り場でチケットとエレベーター券（地上では往路分の片道のみ販売）、地図と絵葉書のセットも必要であれば、購入できます。地図はグループに１冊あれば十分です。メインの洞窟は、結構奥にあるので、そこへの往復だけでも普段運動しない人には疲れます。他の洞窟は、帰り道余裕があったら見るといいと思います。エレベーターは1ヶ所しかないので、どうせ往復することになるので。帰りのエレベーター券は、乗る前にやる気のないスタッフから買うことができます。休憩所みたいのもありましたが、屋外でもちろん冷房とかはないので暑いです。洞窟の中が、若干涼しいくらい。年配の方や、体力に自信のない方は、夏場、雨の後などお気を付けください！
午後からのホイアン行きツアーの途中で寄りました。ゆっくり観たい場合は、ダナンからタクシーで行くのが良いでしょう。ツアーだと余計な大理石販売店によることになります。トイレ休憩という名目で。夏は必ず飲み物持参で。途中で売っていますが、値段高いかもしれません。

The caverns were interesting, with high open shafts that brought in light.  We were put off by the crowds.  Tourists walking up the narrow and steep stairs with their toddlers, constantly yelling.  It was pretty unsafe- I can see the appeal of the elevator.  One parent even had a child in one hand and a folded stroller in the other, climbing the steps.  Crazy!  The outdoor areas were more peaceful and had a nice view of Da Nang.

We seem to be in the minority from reading all the reviews but we all found marble mountain Very disappointing.There’s not actually much to see and the caves are disappointing it’s all quite dirty, In comparison, going to see monkey mountain and Lady Buddha was very enjoyable and I would recommend that instead.

We had a day in Da Nang and we decided to spend time visiting this attraction. We got a taxi here, and the moment we got out of the taxi, we were approached by lots of local women who circled us and who were hounding us to buy their goods. Despite saying no and firmly indicating that we weren’t interested, they proceeded to follow us to the entrance, invading our personal space in what can only be described as intimidatory and frankly aggressive behaviour. We felt like getting back in to the taxi and leaving so not a great start! The caves and pagoda here are very nice but this is nothing special and there were too many people. We nearly broke our ankles walking up to the heavenly gates for the most underwhelming views of the city.  The rocks were ankle breakers with people scambling up and down - very dangerous and not worth the effort! We visited at 09:30 and it was so hot. The caves were nice to see and a respite from the intense heat. The main cave was really nice but with hindsight we wouldn’t have bothered - an average tourist attraction.
The mountains are magnificent and the caves are amazing. However, I'm bothered by the litterings, vandalism, tourist-focused atmosphere. I wouldn't visit this place if I knew about it...

Overrated if you ask me, the ground level cave was great.  Taking the elevator (for which you must pay, both ways) to the apex led you to an array of typical statues and pagoda.  Go to the Lady Buddha on SonTra mountain instead.

This was our second trip here and we didn't enjoy it as much as the first time. The caves are cool to walk into and a nice respite from the heat but I wasn't a big fan of the windy small stairs to climb up and down especially as they are two way.It does feel like a little bit of a rip off getting charged for every bit separately, the cave, the Pagoda, the lift both ways, would prefer to pay one ticket price which covers you for everything. There are a few vendors to get drinks and ice cream along the way which was welcome in the heat.There was building work going on so it was quie noisy on the lower levels and you did get dust over you when you walked past to the high levels, feel like they could have put up on a dust sheet

Not for everyone as some steps and caves are rocky steps of marble polished over time. At least one cave, most people chose not to go deeper because the  steps are a little challenging.There was  a lot of trash and junk,..even in a country known for it  one would think more pride would be taken at an attraction like this, making it less beautiful. Overall, it was Ok for money, but note the large hell cave is separate admission.  The area at the entrance has a tourist trap vibe and vendors will charge 5x more...they work it well and we're  fun, to talk to so yes, we knowingly overpaid.,. For a souvenir with a story.

I’m not sure I would recommend this place. Yes, the temples and caves are very cool. But it is crazy crowded, the signage is not great (the map we were given was sort of helpful but we still got lost more than once), and all the neon lights and souvenir stands make it feel more like a theme park than a cultural attraction. If you are staying in Da Nang or Hoi An and don’t have much time to sightsee and don’t mind the theme park atmosphere then you might have a good time, otherwise I’d recommend going to Hue or going to temples that are less commercialized.

The Marble Mountains consist of 4 or 5 mountains but there is just one famous one with many temples and caves. It involves climbing about 150 steps up to the first temple but be careful as the steps can be slippery in the rainy season. There is an elevator for the elderly or disabled. When you reach the first temple and cave it is possible to climb much higher to visit other caves and temples further up the mountain. Unfortunately with my health condition i was not able to do this but i was satisfied at the first temple etc.

We visited Marble Mountains on the way to Hoi An. We paid a bit more to use the lift both ways but you can take the lift up and climb down if you had more time. There was a lot more to see than I had expected.

This is a great place with a climb that may be hard for people with mobility issues.  There are caves and religious statues that are worth seeing but beware of the merchants and their wares.  The prices are exorbitant and there is a person who will track you in the main store until you leave, probably to assist but is there to sell.

With a few large shops with marble stature manufacturing shops and 50 small tourist plastic shops this is the perfect trap for tourists. When you first get up on the ‘mountain’ tranquility kicks in and it is quite nice but on average only worth 3 stars and I am being generous.

We were only able to see the first cave as very uneven steps and lots of them to go higher up.We asked and were told it was easy but definitely wasn’t .A lift takes one part way up for an extra charge although not expensive.Notfor the elderly.

Marbel mountain we visited by bike which was 30 mins from our hotel in Da Nang and very exhausting especially when you are not a sporty person like me.Better take a scooter. 5-10 mins before we arrived a woman guided us to the entrance and showed us a spot where we can put or bikes and local can guide them, of course they wanted us to buy some drinks or anything for that favor.The entrance to Marbel Mountains where 40.000 VND per person and extra 15.000vnd if you want to take the elevator which I highly recommend if it's hot.Up there you can see nice statues, sights and enjoy nature.Some small cave where you can enter, some need a little climbing so beware of slippery stones.One small cave you can climb little higher thru a narrow hole which is doable but seems dangerous first.On the other side above you can walk normally down normal paths so no need to climb back down.It's a nice little adrenaline kick, up you habe also a nice sight.If it's really hot though, don't forget to take water!!You can buy done drinks/bottles up there too.Going down you have severely paths to choose from.If you come down they will want you to be something from their shops but beware of overpricing.In total its a nice trip and beautiful scenery

We visit mourble mountains because there should be caves and some pagodas with monks in it. Everything were there but it so turistic place, there is souvenir stand on top of the mountain, everywhere are speakers with music for better atmosphere which should look like a stones (its weird). And in the end you don't even have to climb up any stairs, because there is elevator.

Interesting spot however very much over rated as it has become very tourist orientated. Tours will take you to some of the "Marble Manufacturing" place at the foot of the mountain but you really do not get to see much they are looking for business

Went to the Marble Mountains as a part of our tour from Hue to Hoi An. Tickets are 40,000d and tickets for the lift are 15,000d. At the top of the mountain, there isn’t much to see and there are a lot of people crowding your view and path. Many shops try to sell you the same “handmade” statues that are quite fake. Don’t let their anchoring fool you, You should never pay more than $5-$10 for a statue depending on the size. Definitely recommend negotiating and pretending to walk away and go to other shops since they all sell the same things. If you’re pressed for time, you should easily skip this.
If you have a lack of time, recommend you skip this place. The scenary is unspectacularTo get to the viewpoint, one has to climb over huge bolders . Not recommended for elderly or young children

It is an easy way to go there as it is very close to Hyatt Resort, about 5 mins by taxi.  The best way is to buy the ticket for entrance and one way to take the lift up as it is good to walk down the mountain. It takes about one hour to finish the tour.
Half day tour of ancient cave. Cooling and relaxing slow walk up the mountain.with ancient religious statue

I have been wondering what to write about this place for quite some time. Finally, I would go with many of the TA reviews which says it is a waste of time. I agree with that. It has the potential to be a great place, but actually when you go and experience it, there is nothing much. It is just a mountain which has a fancy lift to go up, but once you go up there is nothing. Also, you end up doing lot of hard work climbing steps and rocks to various view points. The only problem is that the view from there is so ordinary!! Haha really funny this place is. On top of that it is crowded and you will see people are just going to different points aimlessly, and you kind of also want to do the same thinking there is some treasure up there. But actually there is nothing:) One view point is extremely funny. You go up a narrow rocky path (rocky as in really rocky) and you reach the viewpoint which is also rocky and then there are so many people, that you land up in a human traffic jam with no place to stand among the rocks. Anyways, let me make an effort to try and make this review informative for people who still want to brave this place.- It is advisable to visit Marble Mountains on the way from Da Nang to Hoi An as it falls on the way.- If it is sunny, then this can be quite a sweaty and tiring activity. So, ideally should avoid if it is hot and sunny. Or, plan accordingly.- Yes, to go up you need to use the lift. Because the steps here are big in size and if you are a family, then it is very tough to climb the stairs. I would say while coming down also, use the lift.
