We took the stairs down, and it was tiring. You have to pay each way to use the lift. I think it is VND 10k per person per trip (up or down). The price is very reasonable. But, must say that this concept of taking a lift to go up the mountain is quite fancy. I have never come across a lift for a mountain. The lift has a glass cover so you can see the view outside. But, it is barely a 1 minute lift ride. Basically it covers 2-3 stories I think- Once you go up by the lift, you reach an area which has some nice garden where you can click pictures. There is a nice group of statues which shows the Buddha praying and many animals, humans are attending. - Once you go up, the steps don't end after that. To go anywhere else you either have to climb up or down. And the problem is that the stairs are all made of stones and they are pretty high (at least or little more than 1 foot). So, this is mostly for elderly people or for kids below 8-9 years of age might have problems.- Then there are these caves. Me and my wife went to one of those. Again the climb up is through very big steps first and then you enter a cave with a Buddha statue which is pretty ordinary. And then behind the Buddha statue is a rocky and narrow path up which is steep. You get into some sense of adventure. And then you will see all kinds of people going up. So, you also join the gang. And it is not easy. Once you climb up, you reach a flat area inside a cave with opening on top where natural sunlight is coming in. But trust me there is nothing else there. It is just an ordinary cave. That's what is wrong with this place. It has the incredible ability to let you down:). We saw that there is a path even further up, but we finally got the idea that it is worthless to carry on.- I would strongly advise not to go for the viewing points. They are useless. Period.- There is a pagoda with a happy fat Chinese Buddha statue outside the same. It has a big stomach which you can touch and rotate your hands on it and wish for something. Besides this I could not see anything of significance here.- All the stairs have railings which are helpful to climb up or down.- If you are going up the lift and coming down the stairs, then your entry and exit point will be different. Why I am mentioning this is that before going up the lift, we saw a particular souvenir shop which we thought we will come back and visit. But, when we came down the stairs, the exit was some distance away from our entry point.- There are many many souvenir shops below the Marble Mountains. And most of these shops sell marble statues. But most look quite fake. I read many TA reviews where people got cheated.- Yes, I remember one specific viewpoint which matched with many TA reviews. The view from this viewpoint is blocked by a newly constructed building. Really it is a disaster. Think about the tourism department sleeping. Blocking is not the word. It really blocks the whole ocean view. What a pity!- All over the Marble Mountains, they play instrumental music through speakers almost hidden among the greenery. It is like when you are slogging it out either climbing up or down the stone steps, there is this music going on in the background. It is quite out of place I felt.- The complex is huge. We just covered very small part of it. There are many pagodas, etc. I will be posting a map of the complex. You can have a look at the same. So, please make judgement accordingly.Marble Mountains was part of our itinerary of Rubicon Tour (please see my separate review of the same). We were on a day long tour for Da Nang and Hoi An. In Da Nang we covered the following places - Lady Buddha, Dragon Bridge, Con Market, Marble Mountains and then finally landed up at Hoi An to spend the late afternoon and evening there. As part of tour we had lunch at Family Indian Restaurant (Da Nang) and then dinner at Namaste Omar's Indian Restaurant (Hoi An). Please see my separate reviews of each of these places.Our Vietnam trip involved 3 places - Hanoi, Da Nang and Ho Chi Minh City. We spent 7 days in Vietnam. Out of all the places we covered, we really liked Halong Bay Cruise (from Hanoi) (please see my separate review of Unicharm Cruise). Then we really liked Da Nang. At Da Nang we liked 2 activities - visit to Hoi An and then visit to Ba Na Hills (both these places take about 45 minutes by road from Da Nang). Ho Chi Minh City was nothing great (we did cover Chu Chi Tunnels and War Remnant Museum there).

Although the view is not as spectacular as you would think, we enjoyed the afternoon on this hill. I don’t see it as a mountain as it is not that high. We took a grab taxi from Da Nang Han Market that took us to here in approx. 20 minutes for 135K VND.You can enter the mountain by stairs or you can take the elevator. We took the elevator option for 4 people and paid 235K VND.The place is bigger than you would think. Once you enter the mountain area you have to use the stairs to go up and down to different viewpoints, caves of temples. Nice to spend an afternoon.
only go if you are probably more keen on Buddhism. Otherwise, it is only an okay visit. I dont really enjoy it

Plan your trip. The caves is worth visiting. The mapping system on site is not that great, bring your own map. The view points are not that great. If visiting during high season expect it to be crowded.
I was here as part of a day tour. It was pricey to get in. I believe it’s roughly 170 steps on the way up. The cave in pretty impressive and it wasn’t overcrowded when I visited (9am).

Went to the Marble Mountain as there are no other beautiful recommendation to go to. The place was not crowded when we visited. There’s not much to see that interest me. It requires a little bit of hiking though. We chose to the elevator going up and down for a fee. Overall, it’s just a treat of fresh air to breathe and some view of the ocean.

Do this if you have spare time otherwise I would skip it. The stairs are really steep which would have been worth it if the view at the top was spectacular which I didn’t think it was. The caves are ok. We booked a taxi through a travel agent in Hoi An town to take us to marble mountain and BaNa hills in one day. It was significantly cheaper than the price the hotel quoted us.
Can explore the place if you have a day to spare. The direction on the sign board is not very clear but you still can locate yourself finally.

It is a mountain even the young and old can visit as they have a lift available. Just pay for the usage and the entrance fees to the mountain . There are steep steps where one can miss to go into the cave. I waited outside the cave with 2 year old . It can be slippery so have to wear good shoes and walk with care. We tracked down the mountain in steps with my 2 year old in a wrong direction move . Surprisingly  he is so accommodating as he loves steps. It took us quite a while.

The uneven steps could be a problem for some, especially if wet.  The caves were not as interesting as I was anticipating.  This could possibly be due to our tour guide who had little history or information to share with us.

Take the elevator up & down (instead of taking the high & uneven steps down as it can be quite dangerous). There is a big statue of Buddha & temple. The caves are slippery, dark, cooling & to our group, a bit eerie going into the caves. After getting into the main cave, we decided not to proceed with the next as we didn't like the feeling or looks of it. Don't stop to buy any marble carvings as most are fake (not real marble) & overly expensive. Our friend was scanned with a colourful pinkish marble stone (colour faded lighter when washed) & 2 so-called marble carving statues that were made of "compressed powder maybe"(?) Friend got them accessed in a proper shop upon his return home
Go in here only if you have time and nothing else to do.Otherwise just passing by this place is good enough.

At the Marble mountains you can visit several pagodas and small and larger caves that contain Buddha statues and worship places. It’s nice to look at, especially the largest of the caves with the gigantic Buddha statue, but not so special and full of tourists. Walking around the mountains between large loud Chinese tourist groups is not as relaxing as the place might have been some time ago. The steps are ok but a little too tall to walk comfortably, so I would suggest investing the small amount of 15k D for the elevator (one way, way down can be purchased separately). Even after arriving by elevator, there are enough stairs and I climb.In conclusion interesting to visit if it’s on your way e.g. from Hoi An to Hue, but not so revolutionary that you have to take a special detour.

If you are staying in Da Nang, it's definitely worth taking some time to walk through some of the caves.  It was interesting but not the highpoint of our trip.  I'd spend a few hours to see it and then venture elsewhere.  Check the local shops for sculptures but be sure to bargain down at least 30% of original asking price and look around for something you really want.
It is not far from the beach hotels. It would be good exercise to walk up to the caves in the morning. There are some photo points. Overall it is okay.

The mountain maybe more appropriate to call it as hill, not that tall. Took the payable lift to top and visited the Linh Un pagoda and the cave temple. Area is not that big, probably 60 to 90 minutes visit is enough. On the foot of the hill, lots of marble seller with various product. According to local, those roadside stall usually used marble debris and powder to make their product instead of homogenous marble, hence is lower in price compare to the shop.

There is nothing about this place that would mesmerize an india. Because marble structures and monumnets are something that is visible in India across the lengths and breadths of the country. If you have seen the creativity from marbles then you could probably go here.We went in and out in approx 2 hours flat and dint like this place 1 bit and surely something that can be missed.The price for using the lift is 55000 vnd ( 22.5 usd ) and if you walk towards the monuments its about 40000 vnd ( 20 usd ).

Not a very high mountains as mentioned and we took the elevator up (fee VND110k for 2 pax one-way). Relatively hot up there but inside some caves you can feel the coolness a little. Not much to see amid some climbing up n down of steps necessary. We went all the way up to Heaven Gate which both slippery and small steps, not advisable for elderly and young kids. Really nothing much up and weather so hot. Give it a miss if you have other places to visit.

The mountain is certainly interesting, but the steps are hard to navigate,due to the missing hand hold rings and if it is raining, it would be treacherous. Some urgent work is needed to fix the problem, l thought the entry cost was supposed to cover maintenance (which seems to be nonexistent) but it was very poor.

Large site with statues of buddhas and temples everywhere.  Some interesting exhibits with neon lights and weird scene and without a tour guide, I wasn't sure what that meant.  Go next door to An Phu Caves, that's more impressive!

We stopped on our way from Danang to       Hoi An. We went in the “hell” side and didn’t bother to go up the “ heaven “ side. It was 20k per person or 50k for both sides. We went around 1230 pm on a Thursday and it wasn’t very busy. If it was busy this place would get a 1 star. It is really warm inside the cave area. We were sweating within minutes of going in. The stairs up to the lookout are extremely steep, narrow and slippery. If you’re are afraid of heights don’t bother. Only one way up and down. The view was ok, nothing to write home about. There is a little market out front full of the usual tourist souvenirs......  you’re guide will probably stop on the way at a marble shop. If you don’t feel like spending $500  🇨🇦 minimum then up into the thousands. Tell him to keep driving. The second  and picture is the stairs 🤪
Its good to see when youre in da nang. But i wont do iT agian as its too mutch waiting.I dont recommend to stay at a hotel there as its dead after 18:00.Nice views tho.

The venue itself is lovely. The caves inside are truly magical. However, be cautious when arriving. Shop owners will try to direct you to park in front of their shops and then later request you purchase something from them. This is not required. My spouse and I simply parked along the main road and didn't pay anything. When you enter, without asking, they will charge a map to your entrance fee. You can decline this. Try not to buy water or snacks inside because the prices are highly inflated and for some reason, some vendors will try to keep your change.
I fell down one of the stairs and nearly over the handrail, very dangerous. No air flow. Very touristy. Was interesting the first 5 minutes but wanted to leave very quickly. Not much to see

If you’ve seen a temple before, or if you’ve taken a walk in the mountain before, this place may seem boring. It’s a small place with stairs and 2 or so caves. 1 hour is enough.就像你去爬了家附近的小山，山上通常有個廟，這裡比較厲害的點就是洞窟裡面的廟囉，網上看看圖片，不是非來不可的景點。

Pictures from many reviews were beautifully taken. Perhaps, I have went too many kinda similar mountains. Personally I wouldn't find it fantastic. Lots of walking and climbing stairs, maybe not suitable for the elderly. Please do not pin high expectations for this attraction.

We had a guide for our 3 day trip to central Vietnam. One of our first stops was marble mountain. We took the elevator up and down so I am only reviewing beyond the elevator. The view is good the cave areas are nice however many of the statues are no historical they are new additions.the large standing Buddha is made of concrete but it blocks an older reclining Buddha. It is a good stop and has some good photo spots. Just don’t to see much historical things.

There are some beautiful features on this mountain, and it is a marvel from the ground looking up as well when you arrive. The peak lends a nice view (although there is a lot of construction happening, which somewhat mars what will ultimately be a very impressive view). The caves were really atmospheric, and there were a couple of steep climbs up staircases, but there is really no need for hiking boots (although flip flops would be precarious). Rather than shrines dotted around en route, this whole mountain is in essence a huge temple. Don't go if you're expecting a full on, back to nature outdoorsy hike. Do go if you like experiencing religious places and a bit of a walk.

.....that....it was not expensive at all .Rather than going with a tour, I took the bus (no. 1  - DaNang  / Hoi An) which stops about 300 meters from the entrance.It was a rainy day,  so not so crowded (which was good); however the marble stairs were wet and had to be negotiated with care.Some nice pagodas, others less interesting.The view from the top is nothing fancy.Overall, a nice half day trip
My partner and I visited, very crowded and needs better crowd control. Also wear good shoes as the marble is slippery.
A lot of stairs, not such a beauriful view (compared to north Vietnam).A lot of different pagodas so if you're a fan you'll enjoy.Worth taking a tour guide to explain what you see.

Marble mountain is ok really. Nothing special. You have to pay extra for the elevator to the top but I suppose it's worth it instead of climbing steps in the heat. At the top theres not much really, a few pagodas and a cave or two. The view from the top is nice if it's a clear day! We had an hour to spend here as part of a stop off and after 35 minutes we were pretty much done.

When we got there the sight was amazing, the mountains were looking over you and the marble shops had amazing statues. the fair to get up from the lift was 15,000 VND which is a pretty fair and cheap price but you can also walk up there but it was a boiling day so we decided to take the lift instead. the fair to get up was was 40,000 VND for each person which is also a pretty cheap price. when we were up there they were tourists EVERYWHERE especially Koreans. they were really loud, this place is sacred with all its beautiful natural caves and Buddas but the tourists ruined it with their loud speaking. the steps were also really steep and the marble made it more slippery so defiantly bring some walking or hiking shoes because you will be a lot of walking and climbing to do. when the day went on, more and more tourists came which madethe place more crowded and noisy. but trust me all the walking and climbing pays off because once you get to the main cave it is gorgeous. it is totally worth it for the price and effort. if you get the chance come here!

There is much to see and do whilst in Danang. As far as caves go, they were quite average and the pestering from the sellers at the base of the caves and the self-proclaimed guides is an annoyance. The walk up was the best part but there are a lot of steps and they are different heights and rathered cobbled. Half of the view at the top ha been obstructed by a large building. For those who don't like stairs, you can buy a ticket for the lift up.

I was there with a group of 16 people from my uni in march 2019. As we arrived there with our bus we already bumped into crowds of tourists running wildly around and souvenir shops everywhere. We got our tickets and took the elevator op to the mountain. The temples are impressive, very interesting architecture and art. I was stunned by the temple inside the large cave. You also got from some places amazing views to the surrounding environment. What really bothered me were the masses of people, all the time you bump into people who are loud, don't watch where they walk or simply sit down on stairs and block the way. If you wanna take pictures, you won't get a picture without some tourists ruining it. This amazing cultural place is a mass tourism spot and in my opinion disrespectful towards the culture.
The caves and trails are really nice. Pitty there is litter everywhere, some areas just piles of garbage
Ordinary except some temples and small cave.   If you are not the type who enjoy walking, you can skip this place although they have elevator service to bring you up or down with a fee.

We stopped by the Marble Mountains on our way to Hoi An. We were brought to a marble shop which I guess is pretty standard when you get here. We did purchase because it was a beautiful but expensive piece. Anyway we lid 110000VND to go up the mountain via the elevator. We walked around. It was lovely, climbed through a few caves. Just be careful with your choice of footwear and bring water. The heat was crazy. We walked back down and at the bottom there are lots of sellers trying to get you to buy in their shop. It’s a nice place to visit.
There are lots of steps which can get slippy in the rain . Great caves to explore

I would say this might not be the best venue for seniors. Very uneven stairs hardly any handrails. Some of the pathways inside the caves are treacherous. I see 30-year-olds camping through but all of us seniors might’ve been struggling to finish our excursion. There is an elevator it’ll get you up there but not the time. There’s many claims once you enter the caves or to get to the caves. Be your own judge might just be a warning for people that used to have better, knees, etc..

Marble Mountains is a must-visit destination in Da Nang. Expect stunning mountain ranges, beautiful pagodas, impressive limestone statues, and—unfortunately—quite a lot of mosquitoes.If you’re planning to visit, make sure to bring mosquito repellent. There are many mosquitoes around, and it’s very likely you’ll leave with a few bites if you’re not prepared (I left with seven!).Be ready for a physical workout as well. There are steep climbs and uneven stone stairs throughout the area. If you have injuries, balance issues, or mobility concerns, this place may be challenging and not highly recommended. However, the entrance tickets are affordable, and since it’s located near the beachfront of Da Nang, getting there by Grab is inexpensive—especially if you’re sharing the ride with a group of four.You’ll find plenty of natural scenery and Instagram-worthy spots for photos. Dress in sporty, comfortable clothing because you’ll definitely get sweaty while climbing. Wearing proper shoes is highly recommended to safely and efficiently navigate the stone steps.Overall, it’s a great place to explore. Plan to spend around 2–3 hours climbing, sightseeing, and taking photos.

Watch out for the pushy vendors as you walk in. Get ready to repeat yourself a hundred times….. no ty, no ty, no ty and keep walking to the ticket office. So glad there’s a lift to take you up some of the way up the mountain, I don’t think I could have done all the stairs personally. Best 40p I’ve ever spent tbh. Up top there’s nice walks around the different old temples and look out spots looking over Da Nang. Some of the caves weren’t worth the effect getting there to see them but the last big cave we saw was very impressive and I’m really glad we saw it.

It would be easy to skip this site whilst on a tour. However, the site is well worth the small diversion. We took the lift to the top and walked down the steps. Make sure that you visit all the various highlights. It would be easy to miss the caves but don't!

Enjoyed our visit but wish we had gone with a guide (or at least done more reading beforehand). The complex is confusing. There are different ticket counters for different entrances. It was fun but we didn't have time to explore everything and for all I know we missed the best parts.

You might expect that the elevator would take you to the TOP of Marble Mountain - WRONG!! It takes you past the first approx 100 steps. But - be warned - that’s only the first stage of your visit to Marble Mountain. After you come out of the lift, you do come to a pretty pagoda at that level. But if you want to explore the whole of Marble Mountain, you be will be climbing up maybe another 300 steps or more. There are lots of ups and downs, all steps. In wet weather, take care on the slippery steps. If you want to do a full explore of Marble Mountain, the elevator doesn’t really save you much. Certainly not worth it if the queue for the lift happens to be long.

Easy pitstop and worth a quick sticky beak.Cheap costs with VND40k entry plus 15k if you want to take the lift up.Missed the cave and it was a bit crowded but otherwise peaceful with lovely views.Steep steps to head down, so take your time.Sadly the touts and pushy sellers at the bottom out to rip off tourists can ruin the experience for some.If you can ignore the sellers and aren’t after bead bracelets or marble buddhas you can walk straight past.Can be done in 30-60 minutes depending on lines.Cash only.

We were surprised at how much we enjoyed Marble Mountain. There are so many beautiful marble sculptures and pagodas!Unfortunately it was very crowded and we didn't get to see cave #5 because people didn't realize it was one way in and one way out, causing a huge backup from people struggling to go back down the way they came in. There was a very long line for the elevator when we arrived but it was only a 10 minute wait. This location has a ton of stairs everywhere, so I wouldn't suggest it for anyone with mobility issues.
涼しいうちにという考えで朝7:30ごろから全体を歩きました。この時間でもかなり暑かったです。見学すべきスポットに番号がつけられていて標識もあるので効率よく回ることができました。ベトナムの仏教寺院を見れて良かったです。

I took a Grab over to the Marble Mountain and spent about 1.5 hours here.From the main parking lot I veered right to go the ticket booth to buy my admission ticket (40k) and pay for the elevator ride up (15k one way).  The ticket booth by the parking lot was just for the Am Phu Cave (separate 20k).The elevator ride up had a short line and I was able to join the second elevator of visitors.  At the top there was a long line of people waiting to take the elevator back down (15k).  However, I walked down and it was quick and easy. There were 15 total places to visit at the Marble Mountain and this was how I tackled my visit:Huyen Kong Cave was towards the back and my primary target.  This cave had the most impressive ambiance with sunlight filtering in through three holes above.  It was also the wettest with some standing water in part of the cave after all the rains.Dinh Thuong Thai was a viewpoint that had the best views of Danang and was the highest point in the Marble Mountains.  The staircase up was steep, slippery and uneven so take your time climbing.Tang Chon Cave was located behind Linh Ung Pagoda and contained multiple stone Buddha statues as well as a unique one that depicted two seated statues playing chess.  At least one Buddha was carved in place from what I over heard.  Xa Loi Tower was a quick stop right before Linh Ung.Linh Ung Pagoda was saved for the end of the visit as it was right next to the exit stairs down.  The long dragon statue was quite impressive being integrated with the wall and trees.I subtracted one circle for the condition of some of the pathways and stairs.  Glad I was able to stop by during a break in the downpours in Central Cietnam.
Было очень интересно. Красивые пещеры и вид на город, фигуры Будды. Много зелени. Заехали рано утром перед Хояйаном и  прогулялись без толпы туристов. Единственный минус комары(((

Buying tickets for the entrance and lift at the main kiosk was quick and easy, with shops and an ATM nearby if you need drinks, snacks, or cash. The climb was sweaty in the midday heat but rewarded us with stunning views from the top, and the caves (a small extra fee) were definitely worth it. Some steps are very narrow and smooth, so I’d recommend visiting in dry weather as they’d be slippery when wet. We even found umbrellas useful – not just for shade but also for steadying ourselves on the steps. A tiring but brilliant experience!
Hidden caves, spiral staircases inside mountains, and surprise pagodas made it feel like a treasure hunt. It’s a bit of a climb, but the views from the top? Worth every step.

It was a pleasant day getting away from the heat into an envied cooler cave with many different path to chose.Ensure good food ware and be aware of steep climbs once within. Overall a truly exciting experience, despite being heavily overcrowded by tourists on that day.- Guess it speaks for itself ✨
Lots of uphill walking so you have to be in fairly good shape. Well worth the trip. Lots of caves, pagodas and sites to see.
Exploring the hidden caves, discovering ancient pagodas, and finally reaching the summit for that breathtaking panoramic view was an incredible bonding experience.

Beautiful location and stunning scenery, two separate experience's and prices. One for the cave and another on a lift to take you to the top of the hill and  to some breathtaking views.  Great experience. It gets very hot here and the steps are wet. Wear appropriate footwear . One suggestion to the operators of the ticket collection at this site. Smile , he is a very angry guy for done reason. Doesn't like being asked questions.

I had heard that the marble mountains in the wet can become quite precarious with the steps turning into waterfalls , and yes , it does happen. We went to the marble mountains start of June , and we started in drizzling rain , only for torrential downpours , and steps turned into gushing streams and waterfalls. It can become a tad dangerous , but we did persist  (with poncho's) and journeyed to the very top - lift and lots of steps walked. The caves were quite spectacular , the pagoda's gorgeous , and I can see how on a clear day the views would have been lovely. The first cave at the bottom is a bit of a surprise , as the representations of Buddhist hell are a bit scary and might scare young kids. Totally worth the experience though. Allow up to 2 hours as there is alot to explore at the top .

We did enjoy our visit but it was quite a steep climb and very hot. One of the caves not easy to climb into (especially in the dark with an 8 year old). It was interesting to go into some of the caves and see the statues and shrines. It's fairly touristy especially at the bottom (as you enter and leave). I certainly wouldn't class th trip as a 'must see'. Might be better to go earlier in the day when a bit cooler. Wear trainers and prepare to get very sweaty!
大理石で出来た岩山です。最初はエレベーターで上がれます。その先は結構ハードなルートが待ってます。まあそれに見合う体験は出来ますが。頂上には沢山の洞窟や寺院がありますよう

Um local bonito e muito próximo de Da Nang, com escadarias íngremes que exigem esforço, mas são recompensadas por vistas incríveis. Existem vários pontos de interesse para visitar ao longo do percurso. Se optar por ir por conta própria — o que eu recomendo —, fique atento ao local de parada, pois há bastante assédio de vendedores e orientadores, o que pode gerar alguma confusão.

Great views, need to be prepared for a bit of chaos. Make sure you take cash, the only way to pay. Best way to do it is take the lift up and walk back down. You will need to pay for the lift again if you choose to come down that way.

This is a unique place with so many hidden spaces.We paid the 20,000 VND each to enter the internal cave, thinking we were going to the top of Marble Mountain.  We weaved in and out of caves and climbed a mountain of stairs (quite precariously!), only to discover that these stairs led to a platform where you turned around and came back down the same way.It was still fun and at times a bit challenging.Inside this cave were grotesque sculptures depicting all sorts of torture.Then we paid the 55,000 VND each to take the lift up the mountain, only to find we could have walked up.  Albeit, up very steep steps, but honestly, if you are fit enough take the stairs. We wandered around a bit and then went back down via the stairs, not realising that there were many more caves to explore and stairs to take while up there.  We missed a lot.Note to self - do more research!There are heaps of shops around the entrance full of trinkets etc, but we didn’t find the shop owners to be pushy.All in all though, we enjoyed what we saw and if we were to go back to Da Nang, we would go back to explore the areas we missed.
동굴은 정말 멋졌습니다.  마블 마운틴 정상에서의 경치는 장관이었어요!  꼭 방문할 가치가 있습니다.........
Statues and steps, impressive caves and marble statues everywhere. Be aware that in some places the steps are steep and smooth/slippery due to many years wear.

Don't know if I expected serenity but the 20-30 tourist busloads of tourists made it the opposite of that.  That's not the fault of the mountain or cave which were very impressive.  Take your time, your water and your non-slip shoes

My lady & I started w/ guide uphill not knowing how steep or far up the ascent was& didnt know you could take elevator. Pro Tip: Take the elevator! You still have a few hundred feet up but not a few thousand…Great sculptures in the caverns aka caves- well worth the visit. ProTip: Bring water w/ you or buy up there as it gets a bit hot

Went here on a cloudy day and wasn’t expecting much but was pleasantly surprised. Loads of steps so be prepared. Loads of rest stops and benches so entirely manageable. Lots of caves with big Buddha’s. There are parts where people are not supposed to walk/ climb but they do so it’s not the safest place.   I would NOT go to the mountains if it’s been raining, its tricky in the dry will be treacherous in the wet as the marble on the stairs is slippy. Places for water etc up the top and also several shops and places to eat at the base. A worthwhile day out.,

五行山，是峴港旅遊必去景點之一，這裡最大的特色是5座聳立的石灰岩山脈，分別象徵金、木、水、火、土，每座都有寺廟與山洞，其中【水山】是最多人會去的觀光景點，可以搭電梯上山，欣賞其他四座山的風景，這裡是峴港的佛教聖地。水山是五行山最大也是最主要觀光景點，門票15,000越南盾，搭乘電梯上下山，單趟也是15,000越南盾，所以如果全程搭電梯加門票，總共是45,000越南盾，折合新台幣約65塊，十分便宜。在水山上面設有兩個瞭望台，一個是望向東邊的望海台，可眺望遠處綿延的海灘，另一個則是西邊的望江台，可以看到漢江的景色還有金木火土的四座山，水山的中心點則是三台寺，四周皆有道路可從三台寺前往附近的洞穴景點，其中玄空洞是洞穴裡面規模最大、最美的景點之一。
Suggestivo complesso di 5 lunghi di culto scavati all'interno della pietra con diversi punti panoramici raggiungibili a volte tramite un percorso decisamente impervio.

Visite incontournable lorsqu'on séjourne à Danang ou alentours.  Au sommet d'une formation montagneuse, une succession de montées et  descentes pour la découverte successive des grottes ("caves") dédiées, d'un intérêt réel pour  la plus grande d'entre elles aux dimensions spectaculaires, ou d'une autre dont on s'extrait par un "trou de souris" qui fera renoncer les claustrophobes.  Au départ et pour un supplément modique nous avons choisi l'ascenseur afin d'éviter une ascension pénible. Mais au retour nous avons emprunté l'escalier pour redescendre en moins de 10 mn. Pas mal de monde (bus et mini bus qui débarquent sans cesse leur groupe) donc un peu d'attente au niveau des ascenseurs (2 au total)

Great views over the entirety of da nang. Much more impressive than we thought, beautiful structures and caves. Be warned there are many steps and some are not very easy to climb up, however there are guard rails sometimes which can be used for assistance. Good small adventure and some exercise

It had been raining on and off when we visited. We met a local from one of the souvenir stalls who assisted by showing us where to access the best gate (less people) and what were the best landmarks to see while in there. We originally brought tickets from the front gate, walked inside, there was not a huge amount to see. It was packed with people. We thought that was it, we purchased elevator tickets to go to the top, however after waiting in line, were told we needed to repurchase gate tickets again. That's when one of the locals assisted. We spent about an hour to hour an a half walking around inside. It was beautiful. Mammoth effort for our 2 boys, especially our 6 year old. The steps at times were steep and narrow and slippery from the recent rain. They have free public restrooms inside, which was helpful. We stopped by the locals souvenir stall and spent a small fortune. Definitely worth the trip. Recommendations; take it slow walking around, there is plenty of rest stops, take drinking water with you. Don't waste your money on the elevator, it costs to go up and come back down.

ホテル近くにあったので軽い気持ちで訪問。なんかこじんまりした洞窟と寺院があるだろうというイメージだったんですが行ってみたら大間違い。洞窟はひたすら巨大で寺院もかなり巨大なものがいくつもあります。まず入口近くのDing Am Phuという洞窟兼寺院が目に入るのでここに入ります。横にあるエレベーターはこの洞窟のてっぺんに登るものだろうと思ったら全然別物。ここも含めてどこの洞窟も足場が悪く急な滑る道を進んでいく必要があり、かつ泥だらけになる可能性があるので汚れてもいい服装で行くことをお勧めします。またエレベーター乗って山頂に行ってからも階段また階段という感じですから足は鍛えておきましょう。ただ苦労したとしてもなかなか見応えはあると思います。

Marble statues, pagodas etc. beautifulPlace. Definitely worth a visit. It take around 2-3 hours to properly see this. The steps can be quite steep. Also more caution is needed during rains. Must visit

When we went to purchase tickets to marble mountain and the elevator up ( worth the money) the ticket lady was a little rude and charged us for 5 people instead of 4. the elevator ride is quick and worth it. The caves are beautiful but the stairs are very uneven and very slippery when they are wet. to see everything you have to climb a lot of stairs. My kids were over it after about 20 min.
位於峴港附近的群山，又叫五行山，共有五座，分別是金山、木山、水山、火山及土山。行了水山，大約需時四十分鐘，途中有不同的石洞可以觀賞。
En plats med mycket historia! Både mer nutida liksom äldre. Grottor och pagoder är intressanta.  Ganska jobbiga klättringar både uppåt och nedåt. Vissa ställen var lite hala. En bra upplevelse.

This is certainly not an easy afternoon stroll. However, it is enjoyed.Tips,Wear good shoes or runners with support and good grip. The many sets of even and slippery steps will try you.We didn't use a guide and managed fine.So start by getting the elevator at gate 2, believe me the 40k dong is money very well spent and will at least eliminate one set of steep steps.Cave 10 is the most impressive and the easiest access with great photo opportunities, just wait for it to empty a litte, it will eventually.Another of the caves has a small opening to the rear, which will eventually bring you to a high-level viewing point.We accessed said viewing point via the arduous steps and rock climbs, which would challenge a mountain goat, and we returned via the back of the afore mentioned cave. Whatever way you do it will involve a lot of sweat and a bit of squeezing though tight spaces ! That said, I got my overweight ageing body through it, so it is absolutely manageable, and there is a certain level of achievement on completion.Everyone is struggling just do it at your own pace and enjoy it, don't be afraid to use your arse like us in places to slide down.Another cave involves a much lesser but still arduous and nattow climb from one level to another and may take a little bit of traffic control ! We had to rather strongly suggest that the people below stopped to let a few of us from the quickly filling upper cave climb down.Don't be put off by my review as I really enjoyed it, just skip the coat, you won't want it even if it's raining, who cares how long it takes you or how much sweat is dripping from your body.I wish you luck.
多くの観光客で賑わう場所で、特に韓国からの人が多い印象を行けました。エレベーターで山の上に登ると七重の塔が建っていたり洞窟もあり、見学する場所も多く点在しています。特に孫悟空とのかかわりの多い場所との事ですので、歴史を感じながらの見学となりました。
Amazing views and exercise spot. There are many statues to look at along the way. There are also spots to refill on water, so don't worry if you forget your water bottle.

We've seen numerous Buddha statues and caves. The good thing about this hike was not just the panoramic views but the vendors selling fresh coconuts, water etc to cool you down after a long walk/hike. We used the elevator on the way up but missed it going down, so we had to endure the steps going down the mountain. It was a good experience.
Interesting and easy to reach. Compination of religious and cultural site. It is a geological wonder as well if interested in caves.

Very nice. But confusing tickets. 1 for entry, 1 for elevator up , 1 for elevator down. So you must find where to purchase each ticket... they should just sell one ticket at one price. Many stress out and angry people trying to figure out how the system worked.
Molto bella la location con varie grotte e tempietti carini. Il costo di accesso è irrisorio. Peccato che le scale in pietra siano con gradini molto alti e scivolosissimi.
It is a fascinating place but the narrow steps that are wet would not be idea for frail or elderly people. It is really amazing to look at, but just be very careful on the slippery stairs and floor

We visited this place in the day. The mountains host 5 caves with Buddhist worship inside. The trek into and out of the caves posed some challenges. The trek up to the highest peak is even more challenging since it didn't have much shade. The view and experience inside the caves are amazing. The view from the highest peak is great.We had good refreshments after the trek.

This place consists of five marble mountains representing gold, wood, water, fire, and soil. Visitors can see numerous Buddha statues and other gods here. We took the elevator up the mountain, but it wasn't very high, so it was easy to climb. The view from the top was amazing because we could see the coastal area.

I went to Marble Mountain on a tour. The place was very crowded with tour groups which made taking photos very difficult. As least we were guided through the caves and ghettos. I'm pleased I did the tour as I never visited on my previous visit 24 years ago!!

The marble mountains were a very nice place to visit.Went at 9 am, still very humid.. 37 deg outside. Bit enjoyed.. Whether also  shop Sam'sJust down the road..For morning tea must be recommended. Lovely  banana fritter..Yum.
It was very hot the day we visited the Marble Mountains but we had a good time there. We didn't see any monkeys, maybe next time 😄

Yes it is interesting to walk around the mountain and see the temples and inside the caves and it is worth a visit but FYI its a very busy place, typically thriving off all the tourists that come by - you probably need around 1.5 hrs to see it all

Pemandangan yang Indah !!Gunung dengan gua-gua yang cukup menarik dan menantang.Namun kurang perawatan, kurang tanda petunjuk dan kurang petugas yang berjaga di lokasi-lokasi yang cukup berbahaya. Masih banyak turis asing/lokal yang membuang sampah sembarangan, dan melakukan vandalisme di dinding gua.

Good views but you have to work to get them. Plenty of stairs and uneven smooth marble rocks to climb over on some tracks, would be really dangerous if wet. Wear good footwear! Interesting place, but once would be enough
The Marble Mountains itself are a nice excursion from Da Nang. But be aware that the paths are steep and not well maintained, especially at the top.

Assolutamente da vedere se andate a Da nang. Vi consiglio di andare prestissimo al mattino così da evitare il caldo e troppe persone. Alcune parti del percorso non sono adatte a tutti e occorre fare attenzione. Troverete dove acquistare qualcosa da bere  e ne avrete bisogno per il caldo. Ci sono due entrate di cui una provvista di ascensore. Il prezzo è di 40 mila dong (1.50. Euro) per visitare tutta la parte alta della montagna. Potete anche visitare la grotta che troverete sotto vicino ai parcheggi allo stesso prezzo circa.

We just returned from our holiday to Vietnam and we visited this place. We very much enjoyed our visit, helped by our guide who was very knowledgeable.There's lots of steps so take this into account.The history was enjoyable and  touching on the Vietnam war and some of the damage from that time was interesting.

The Marble Mountains (Die Marmorberge) sind eine Ansammlung von fünf Marmor- und Kalksteinhügeln südlich von Da Nang. Der vietnamesische Name ist: Ngũ Hành Sơn, was die "5 Elemente-Berge" (Metal, Wasser, Holz, Feuer, Erde) heißen soll.Es gibt 2 Ebenen mit einer Ansammlung von Höhlen, Pagoden und Tempelanlagen. Auf die erste Ebene kann man entweder zu Fuß (Stufen) oder mit dem Fahrstuhl hochfahren (15000 VND p. Fahrt). Die zweite Ebene erreicht man nur zu Fuß. Dort befindet sich auch die Höhlen. Als störend erfand ich die Standsverkäufer auf der Straßenebene, die auch hartnäckig werden können. Ignorieren Sie sie. Die Marmorberge sind ein sehr touristisches Ziel aber gleichzeitig ein schönes Ausflugsziel in Da Nang.
Climbing the Marble Mountains was a fun mix of exploring caves, visiting temples, and enjoying the panoramic views of Da Nang.

Our tour guide brought us here and it was worth a visit. Lots of architecture like pagodas to see on the mountain and the big cavern was worth a visit. The light streaming in made for great photos. This is a nice outing and doesn't take too long.One warning--skip the marble shops if your guide suggests them! Just another tourist trap to get you to spend money. The sales woman was pretty aggressive and got irritated with us when we didn't want to buy a statue for our house in the US! :)

Very beautiful view and beautiful temple no need to worry about hiking lift is available but if you do want to hike there is still some very long staris to see but it was beautiful and one monk was so kind to me I was very happy but do not wear shorts there if possible long dresses like maxi and jeans will be fine for both gender they do provide some skirts to wear on above the outfit so no need to worry

Due to its  distance from downtown Danang, I hired a driver to reach this attraction. Learned about 2 separate attractions here, so separate entrance fees.Shrines and Temples: I visited them first, took the elevator and walked a short distance to visit several temples and shrines here. There are areas with so many steps going down or going up. I opted to skip these attractions.Caves: There are no elevators to enter these caves. There are several statues, etc. along the way plus of course narrow steps to climb up or down.Having visited the Lady Buddha areas before this attraction, I noted this attraction less appealing and more physically challenging due to the number of steps to climb.
Many tours are available to visit the Marble mountain , but you can just go there by taxi and buy tickets. Make sure you get the right ticket.
Impressionante as várias cavernas na montanha. Sem dúvida um local a visitar. Para conhecimento a montanha tem a forma de uma mulher gravida deitada.

Located just 5 kms away from the My Khe beach of Danang, this is a magnificent natural edifice. A unique cave completely formed of marble rocks with enormous caverns and crevices as commonly found in the carbonates. The main chamber is large and well ventilated. This is adorned with huge statues and artefacts. One can relate with the Batu caves in Gombak, Kuala Lumpur. From the main chamber there are stairs going down into the narrow passage ways where you need to take care so as to avoid bumping your head with the hanging ledges. Also the rocky walls are slippery due to continuous seeping of waters. Advice to elderly visitors and people with knee problems to use the elevator just adjacent to the cave. The stairs are very steep and even if one manages to climb up, it is rather difficult to climb down.A well recommended site to visit when you are on Danang.

We stayed close by and walked to the Marble Mountains in 20 minutes or so. We arrived early which we were super happy about as it gets very busy with buses full of tour groups. We took the stairs up there rather than the elevator because there were literally no people at the stairs yet the elevator queue was very long! We actually really liked taking the stairs which are a little uneven but not that bad in general - if you struggle to take stairs (approx. 200 and then a few more once you’re at the top) you can get there early and take the elevator.  There are lots of little shops around the area which sell souvenirs and some food and drink places too. Overall, we had a very nice experience and it’s certainly something to consider for half a day while you’re in the area.

Il biglietto del sito costa 55.000 Dong a testa. Abbiamo visitato varie grotte e templi molto particolari. Queste montagne sono venerate da secoli dai vietnamiti. Le Marble mountain si trovano a 25 km da Danang; sono cinque promontori che si trovano in una zona ricchissima di marmi (ora non è più possibile estrarlo in quanto protetta dal governo). Ogni montagna ha un nome derivante da elementi naturali, terra, acqua, fuoco, metallo e legno. Si può prendere un ascensore per salire a Thuy Son, visitare la pagoda Tam Thai e poi si passa in una stretta gola che è a mio parere la più bella, Huyem Khong. Durante la guerra i vietcong la utilizzarono come ospedale da campo. Nel 1972 fu dislocata una batteria antiaerea vietcong governata da una pattuglia tutta al femminile che riuscì a abbattere 19 aerei americani. A febbraio c'era una discreta umidità e si esce sudati dalla visita.

We throughly enjoyed going here and seeing the monuments. It’s a unique, beautiful place only a short distance from downtown Da Nang. A few notes before you go:Stairs - There are 100s of them in all shapes and sizes and they can get slippery. I agree with others you should not visit if you have trouble with them. Crowds - It can get VERY busy. Patience (especially in the stairs) can be required at times. Time of Day - To maximize the experience, know when the sun will approach or be over the cave to create the light rays (seen in most ads).

Easy 2-3 hour excursion from Danang. Can do it yourself by booking a Grab so no need for an organised package. It’s enroute from Danang to Hoi An , so would recommend Marble Mountains & then head to Hoi A for the evening lanterns on the river.  Marble Mountains has some nice caves , pagodas & small temples. Best bits are the caves. Avoid the elevator , long queue for it & only takes you up a small section which we walked in 10 mins. Some of the steps on Marble Mountain higher up are quite steep but doable.

Vistas preciosas desde el complejo, diferentes lugares para visitar, la gran cueva,pagodas,la puerta del cielo..No apto para todas las edades ni personas con movilidad reducida, requiere un considerable esfuerzo físico,y los peldaños son muy incómodos.

Посетили Мраморные горы в Дананге и остались с неоднозначными впечатлениями. С одной стороны, это уникальное природное явление и место с богатой историей, которое обязательно стоит посетить. С другой стороны, есть некоторые моменты, которые могут испортить впечатления.Начну с положительных сторон. Мраморные горы действительно очень красивые. Они состоят из пяти вершин, каждая из которых имеет свое название и значение. Самая высокая и популярная вершина - это гора Воды, на которой расположены многочисленные пещеры и пагоды.Пещеры на горах также заслуживают внимания. Особенно впечатляют пещера Linh Nham, знаменитая своими сталактитами и сталагмитами, и пещера Thien Cung, славящаяся своими красивыми водопадами.Кроме того, на Мраморных горах есть несколько исторических сооружений и памятников, которые также стоит посетить.Теперь о минусах. Первое, что бросается в глаза, - это большое количество людей. В высокий сезон здесь бывает очень много туристов, и это может создавать определенные неудобства.Второй минус - это зазывалы, которые пытаются продать вам что-то на каждом шагу. Если вы не хотите тратить деньги, нужно быть внимательным и не поддаваться на их уговоры.И, наконец, третий минус - это билеты. Их продают в нескольких местах, и цены могут отличаться. Будьте внимательны, чтобы не переплатить.В целом, Мраморные горы - это интересное место, которое стоит посетить. Однако, если вы хотите избежать толпы и не попасться на удочку зазывал, лучше приезжать сюда рано утром.Не забудьте взять с собой воду и удобную одежду, так как место предполагает активную ходьбу и подъемы по лестницам на вершины.

Took a taxi from Hoi An where we were staying (probably closer from Da Nang). Ended up spending quite a while here. We walked up the mountain and walked down. Did not use the elevator. It was quite a physical walk up and down but enjoyable and the kids saw it as a big adventure. Beautiful views and great photos to be taken. The way the marble has been crafted is amazing and the caves are fun to explore. Note if it’s a rain day the steps can be slippery.

The marble mountains are only 10 min by Grab taxi from Da Nang beachfront and lies on the way to Hou An. One can see Marble mountains enroute to Hou An . The caves are beautiful with Marble sculptures carved into the rocks . There are two routes apart from the vcemteal grand cave with Buddha statues. The one on top is very narrow but it opens up to an open space where one can see DaNang city from the top of the hill. We could not visit the other  route. The ticket counter offers a map for purchase but it is useless unless one wants to keep as souvenirs. Overall a quick visit from Da Nang and worth the visit. You can do the entire round trip in one hour spending 30  40 minutes inside the caves.

パワースポットと聞き、午前中にハイキングに行きました。パワーを感じられる場所でした。思ったより、ハイキングというより山登りでした。階段が大理石でできていました。滑るので気をつけてください。穴から抜けて登る道は急斜面で怖くて迂回しました。洞窟の中は圧巻です。山頂は達成感がありました。五行の山々がみられます。ツアーではなく、個人旅行でいったので、自分のペースで休めて良かったです。エレベーターで登るコースは全部みるのに二時間かかりました。入口のコースは一時間くらいかかります。見学できるところが違うので別料金です。町に降りる石材店がたくさんあり、面白いです。

Long Queue at the foot of the mountain as you want to enter the mountain. Overall quite a good experience. Just some improvements where the floors and grounds are rather muddy. Not sure if it’s supposed to be like this. Some of the workers left u finished cleaning stuffs at a corner. Not so glam however quite dark so not so noticeable. Steep steps and many straps to take to go up to the peak. So those with heart problems and back problems please note. Wear very good grip shoes and we brought an umbrella as well due to weather. Somehow it helped us to walk better. Dec is wet month. Do bring along poncho and keep yourself warm.
Merece la pena la visita a lasarbles Mountains. Nosotros estábamos alojados en Hoi An y alquilamos una moto en el hotel. De vuelta nos paramos en la playa a tomar algo.

We were staying at Hoi An so hired a car & driver for 4 hours to take us to the marble Mountains and the Dragon Bridge. Can't remember how much but not expensive) We arrived at Marble Mountain at 8-25 and there were very few people there. We didn't bother with a guide or tour just walked around by ourselves. Little bit difficult to know from the map they give you which cave you were in but we managed to see everything including the view from the top (worth the steps) in just under 2 hrs. When we left at 10-25 the place was heaving and the car park full - so go early. Driver then took us to Dragon Bridge and the love bridge, couldn't get there at night but wanted to see it. Worth the visit as we were close by but prob not worth it  otherwise. The whole thing took under 4 hours
Nice place. Great views of the Han river and Ocean. Highlight are the views. Lots of stairs but they do have an elevator. It wasn’t to crowded. Would recommend if looking for some exercise.

Eigentlich fand ich die Marble Mountain echt cool mit vielen Möglichkeiten, Höhlen oder via Treppen schweisstreibend zu oberst rauf mit traumhafter Aussicht. Das Ganze ist etwas überlaufend, aber war halb so schlimm, man muss sich einfach seinen Weg suchen. Am Fusse des Berges halt das obligate mit den Touristenständen und dem ständigen Ansprechen, ob man was kaufen möchte. Aber auch daran gewöhnt man sich.

very interesting place, impressive the caves inside and the work done by locals. The usual tourist busy spot but worth the visit. Lots of walking and high steps going up. Definitely start your adventure by taking the elevator.

ホイアンからのダナンへの帰りのタクシーで寄り道してもらいお金を払って待機してもらいました。タクシーの運転手が2時間くらいで回れるとのことだったので観光しました。　昼くらいに行きましたがエレベーターで1時間近く並びました。。五行山の頂上 (The Highest Peak) から眺め、フエンホン洞窟 (Huyen Khong Cave) は特に良かったです。頂上はなかなかの段差の階段が続くのであまり人は行かないようです行き帰りとも誰ともすれ違わなかったです。フエンホン洞窟は地上から差し込む光がとても幻想的で良かったです。時間帯によって差し込む光の角度や量が変わりそうなので色々な時間で楽しめそうです。
Thiên nhiên, cảnh vật đẹp. ban quản lý giữ được vệ sinh rất sạch sẽ, các di tích cũng có vẻ được trùng tu nên còn mới. Tuy nhiên hiện tượng bán hàng rong vẫn còn, nếu dẹp được vấn đề này sẽ tốt hơn.
Magnificent views from the top of the mountains, but don’t be taken in by the souvenir shops at the coffee stop at the top.

Là một trong những danh thắng nổi tiếng của TP Đà Nẵng. Ngũ Hành Sơn là 1 cụm các ngọn núi, được theo Ngũ hành: Kim - Mộc - Thuỷ - Hoả - Thổ. Mình tham quan ngọn Thuỷ Sơn, bên trong có 1 hang động, có 1 cột chính giữa động, xung quanh có các trụ xoay, có chạm khắc chữ Phạn theo kinh Phật. Có 1 dòng suối thiêng, mà người ta tin rằng dòng suối này tinh khiết, chữa lành tâm hồn, cầu sức khoẻ - bình an. Điểm trừ: Đang xây các hạng mục nên còn 1 số chỗ bừa bộn. Người lớn tuổi sẽ khó di chuyển xuống hang động do các bậc thang đi xuống khá nguy hiểm và trơn.

Interesting place. Stairs are tricky as they are quite uneven but once you get to the main caves, thoroughly enjoyable. There are many little cave areas to explore. There are opportunities to buy water or other drinks during the walk up as it was hot! Highly Recommended.

There’re several marble mountains around but tourists usually access only one of them. The areas consist from 2 parts, the one to go inside and the other is outside where you can access by lift. The one inside is a little bit dark and you proceed your way and come across to Buddhism status. Some are big. You can imagine this was the place for prayers. You can go up to the top but stairs are steep and  tight so watch your steps and others. The view from the top is great to see other mountains but can’t stay too long since many tourists waiting.The outside area is accessible by lift. You buy tickets near the lift gate. They sell one way or round. We bought one way. We came down without ticket because nobody checked it on the way back lift. They gave us post cards with tickets. Don’t know if this comes along or we bought the service with tickets by mistake but honestly we don’t need this service but wish for lowering the entrance fee instead.The area is like a small park. The pagoda very close to the lift is great. There’s a map for the whole area near the lift. But map is not kind enough to give you each spot’s characteristics. You set the destination and walk around.We targeted to go to a spot called xxxx heaven but the way to the spot was continuous big rocks and we didn’t see heaven. I wish more maintenance and care to this wonderful world heritage by Vietnamese National tourism agency for more safe and efficient visits.
The marbles are nice and the entrance is cheap but for me not a must see if you don’t have enough time. The viewpoints are great.
Took elevator to go up instead of climbing even then there are many steps to climb if you are keen to view Budha statue & Caves

Wir sind mit dem Moped hingefahren. Unterwegs hat uns eine Dame angesprochen, ob wir zu den Marble Mountains wollen. Sie hat uns in ihrem Shop parken lassen. Im Gegenzug sollten wir dann natürlich etwas kaufen. Am Besten auf den offiziellen Parkplatz gehen, da zahlt mann dann unter dem Strich weniger. Die Marble Mountains an sich sind auf jeden Fall sehenswert. Der Eintritt ist 40.000 Dong. Nur die letzte Grotte ist nicht inbegriffen. Für diese soll man noch mal extra 20.000 Dong bezahlen. Ob diese so sehenswert ist weiß ich nicht, da wir uns dann entschieden haben nicht hinein zu gehen.
Nice caves and pretty cool rock formations. Very hot to climb all those stairs! Worth while getting up the high viewing point for an awesome view. Rip of for parking scooter guaranteed 😉

Wonderful views, caves, temples but be careful as the steps are steep and slippery. Followed a path into a cave and the only way out was to climb knee high steps and pull yourself out. A warning would have been good. Recommend the escalator...saves your energy for the rest of the site.

Our friend, Jack has showed us this place many times since  he came back from Vietnam. So we decided to visit these mountains by a simple way.Always fun to explore caves. We did the discovery tour; it was very commercialized, but a nice tour overall.The caves 're nice and cool especially compared to how hot it was today. It was absolutely beautiful.I have a small kid so it was challenging, but overall, I would totally go there again. Amazing place!

There are two sites at the Marble Mountains and consideration should be given as to your level of fitness when embarking on going there. Each has separate admission costs which should not be a deterrence as they relatively minor costs. The lower larger cave is the more accessible for people who might have more mobility issues.  Most parts of this cave are interesting and provide a good experience of what can be seen in the caves and grottoes of the other site that can be accessed by elevator (or steep steps if you are keen). The second site has numerous steep steps and as a person with health issues I struggled to reach some points and due to electrical faults some caves were not lit.  While not advocating that you should not visit the area accessed by the elevator I would say people with mobility issues will be limited in exploring the site to the full extent of what is present.What I saw of the second site, almost at the level of the elevator exit, was interesting.  There were Buddhist monks in prayer when we visited and several accessible grottoes and landscaped spaces located at this level.  The remaining areas of the site required steep climbs on steps that might provide issues for some visitors.  If you are prepared to climb the steps I think you will enjoy the added attractions that I could not reach.  Given the low cost of entry you should not be put off going to the elevator accessed area as what I managed to see was well worth the visit.
We enjoyed our visit to Marble Mountain, which we did as a private trip. The mountain is very interesting. Note: The steps inside are steep and good shoes need to be worn.

Its good for something to pop out to late afternoon or early morning to avoid the heat.  Cave cabins are large and amazing how they got that way, however not preserved that well. Its more on how they integrate their faith into these caves is what you will see.

Hills come out of nowhere. Site allows you to go to the top and have a look around. There are many caves with temples inside and also several religious buildings is outside. I liked it there.However some marble manufacturers are really annoying trying to convince you to buy some of their "products".

Our Guide told us the Marble Mountains consist of 5 hills each named after different element : fire, wood, metal ,water and earth.  The entire area seems a little unkept , felt really stressed by the locals pressing us to take our pictures, however when we took the lift to the top , the view at the top  is beautiful.  We walked into the caves , they are interesting with amazing statutes.

Well worth a visit but lots of well worn stone steps even if you use the lift. The main cave is very impressive. Be warned you will get your patience tested with the street traders and coach tour guests

Nice to visit, the main cave is probably the main attraction, the rest being temples that are not too different from other temples you can see in Vietnam, and there's also a smaller cave that's good but not amazing. Easily reachable by motorbike / scooter from Da Nang or Hoi An.You can take a lift to the first level but if you don't, it's not too hard or too long to walk up, it's just that the stairs are a bit steep so if you have knee issues, better get the lift. There are stairs after anyway, the main cave is only accessible after going up another set of stairs (much more doable than the one that takes you to the first level).You can visit the entire place in about 1h. Everyone says 2h minimum but it can be done quicker.
Great trip with natural view, cave and temple in mountain. Nice handicraft marble stone village.Contact Mr Eric with Whatsapp number : + 84 944337018

Steep and high steps. Multiple steps, required ascension and descession on many uneven and some slippery, Not recommended for elderly and chidren. Fitness level is required to climb the mountains with hiking shoes.

Very interesting site.  Be prepared for climbing; steps and rocky terrain.  Beautiful views at the top. The cave is quite tight,  so this is not for those with claustrophobia. There is an elevator but that only takes you half way

Everything is great here. I docked 1 point off the rating only because I think visitors should just prepare for being followed and asked to buy things. The people are friendly and kind but do follow you around if you speak English. It doesn’t overly bother me but I can see it being overwhelming for some. Wear non slip shoes as the steps are obviously very old and crumbling so you want non stick. The elevator up is very hot but only takes about 30 seconds to get up or down. There’s lots of places to buy water and souvenirs. You have to buy a ticket for entrance as well as a ticket to use the elevator up. There are steps to climb up however it can be challenging for anyone who isn’t sure footed. You can always take the elevator down. Ticket in per person is 40,000 dong and elevator is 15,000 dong up and buy another ticket to go down (total 30,000).
If u like to visit temple or pagoda, you might want to try to visit this pagoda. Nothing special inside the temple.

Beautiful area with beautiful temples and caves. Very nice when you get up to the mountain. Before you can enter there are loads of stalls trying to sell you everything. It also can get pretty crowded.

Awesome caves with many religious statues, natural and man made steps.  Steps and passage ways are not difficult to manage but can be steep, narrow and uneven especially the climb to the top.  Proper walking shoes is recommended.  There are 2 sections to the caves/mountains. 1st with 40K entrance fee, 2nd 20K entrance fee.  It's the 2nd section/cave that has slippery steps due to natural dripping water.  Be early or will have to deal with many large tour groups.

Nice way to spend an afternoon in DA Nang - you can walk up one of the mountains. There are caves, pagodas and viewpoints. It’s not worth the cost of a guided tour - take the local bus (or hire a motorbike  if you’re feeling confident then you also have the freedom to go elsewhere when you wish - the roads in da nang are a bit better than some other cities with one way streets and traffic lights but still very busy)  55 vnd entry including the lift up but not down. Lots of shops at the bottom wanting to sell you things.

We would highly recommend a visit to Marble Mountains. As many have said, the steps are steep and can be very slippery.. if you go slow, you are fine. The various altars throughout the mountain are amazing. We did not find the sellers at the entrance any more aggressive than any market area. MAKE sure to count you change when buying tickets. Plus they included a map and postcards for an extra 10k without asking.  And did it all so quick we only caught the short change when we turned around. We went back and the woman said she made a mistake - only 100k - but it is the principle! Definitely did not take away from our visit. Love the road of  statues before the mountain- every religion is covered!

A great visit allthewhile pretty tourist-y. If you are looking for a more authentic visit I’d go towards Hoi An. The spots I’d recommend the most are the caves as they are varied, large & fun to visit! (Wear sneakers or good shoes, I did the caves in flip flops and it was not fun) :)

The marble mountain was awesome. It was raining when I went but still very beautiful.The commercial nonsense at the bottom of the mountain was terrible. I went to the mountain with a driver. I was pounced on by a very loud, unpleasant woman in a yellow raincoat with good English, who insisted I come and look at her family’s shop on my return from the mountain. I came down a different way than she expected but she still found me on my return. She then tried to tell me everything in her shop was made by her father.I bought one object although she could not understand why I would not by two. I subsequently found on that this one thing cost 4 times what it would cost in Hanoi and clearly was not made by her father!!!!The mountain was great, shame about the desecration of the importance of the site by such terrible commercialism.
Nothing new and special about this place . Go by lift and up there are Buddhist temple . Come down by foot or take lift ticket and come back . There are lots of marble shops to buy

Interesting and scenic. There's some steep climbing involved, which suited me as I lake to hike, and I was walking off a big earlier meal. Not crowded. Reviewed by Miles Archer of Boca Raton, Florida.

The temple itself is wonderful, the way the authorities thought of installing some elevators without disturbing the monument in any way, seems perfect to me. The problem is with souvenir sellers, which you find when climbing or descending, who are very annoying, you really need to buy something of them. Otherwise, if more cleaning were done in the area it would be an oasis of heaven

It requires some strength to see it completely but if you show courage & complete all the marked area, passing through caves, you'll feel amazed. There are lifts available to take you up & down with separate tickets. If you come by your own vehicle make sure to park your vehicle in a safe area, The shopkeeper would ask you to pay for a parking fee if you don't buy anything from them.

This place was well worth a visit. We went with a local guide and it was worth it because he showed us a lot that standard tours don’t see. The temples are beautiful and the caves truely amazing!  It’s a strenuous walk to the top but if you have good fitness you’ll be fine,  the view of the area is nice.  There is an elevator to part of the mountain. What we could not cope with was that rubbish is left on the tracks. Surely for a tourist destination that charges an entry fee someone could send some blokes in with a rubbish bag to clear up the plastic bottles, wrappers, and everything else, that are all over the place and put some signs up that litterers will be prosecuted. It’s really not cool. Everyone - tourists included-  need to do better when it comes to rubbish. Vietnam can’t afford to lose tourists! It seems people turn a blind eye but it is absolutely putting me off this part of the world. Ban the plastics.

It is a good place to visit and see amazing rocks with a lot of Buddha statues .Can see lower level caves .But lot of climinlmg , even after list . Not good for a person who can't take high steps . Lots of climbing . So careful .

Quite a cool experience. Fun walking around and looking at the caves and statues Fair few steps to get to highest point and some elderly might find it hard to reach - view from the top isn’t too amazing either to be honest
This is a great exercise with lots to discover - temples, caves and natural beauty with good views in all directions . Loved my few hours here

A nice and interesting  place to go with 3 small caves and one amazing big cave with Buddha placements and other religious things to look for. Go up with the elevator make the course and go down to gate 1. If you are in a good physical condition you can use the way up from Gate 1. and go down gate 2.

Definitely not for those who have difficulty with steps. Not only there are many steps, there are also a bit of climbing. Of course, you can choose to take the elevator, (with fee payment) or choose to detour or simply just miss out some of the caves. But the trek is worth it when you reached the top, the view is nice.
Steep steps and lots of them. Climb with care. I wondered why I decided to go for it as I was climbing up, but at the end of it, I quite enjoyed the whole experience. If the weather is good
We enjoyed the visit. A marble mountain out of nothing. We took the elevator to the plateau with several impressive caves and Buddha statues.Nice trip!

This mountain is not for the faint heart! It can be quite challenging and slippery, especially after a downpour. However, the sights in the caves are worth the time and effort in going up the mountain!

We visited on 29 Sept 2022. The marble mountains area is slightly affected by the storm and is experiencing some power outages. The elevator is not operating today but would likely be resumed soon. There are local buses that stop nearby. We took a Grab from the bridges to gate A. Gate A/ gate 1 entrance is closed today as they are clearing the fallen tree. Gate B is open and available for entry. Most sites are accessible and the locals are still clearing up the debris in some areas. The place is free to visit and 40k for elevator (optional). There are donation boxes at most sites. Do contribute if you have spare change as they are run and maintained by locals and especially now where they are picking up after the storm. The nearby stalls and locals do marble carving and craft and sell many ornaments. Do try and support them especially if you are interested in home decor. Some of the stall owners speak decent English. There are some drink stalls around and within the areas and free toilets available. The steps are pretty steep and undulating and can be slippery. Walk with care and wear shoes with good grip or high ankle boots if you are prone to slip or tripping.

Beautiful pagodas and Buddha statues set among mountain forest and caves. We really enjoyed the couple of hours we spent exploring here. We took the elevator up and walked back down where there were cafes for us to grab fresh cold drinks.Worth the visit but you’ll need to be fit and be wearing sneakers because there’s A LOT of stairs and parts that are tricky to navigate (steep climbs, narrow footholds). It was rainy season when we visited - cloudy and 30C degrees. I couldn’t imagine doing this in the heat of Vietnamese summer! Also, be prepared for the hard sell on marble statues and ornaments- the streets at the foot of the mountains are lined with stores selling these.
Walking and marvelling nature mMany interesting sights natural and man made.Sign posting needs much improvement.
it was an experience , very nice mountains , make sure you are fit there is few steps you need to walk up and down but lovely caves

It was a perfect day for us to visit Marble mountain. Cool and breezy. Our guide explained that the mountains are cluster of five marble and limestone hills. And they are appropriately named after the five elements: Kim (metal), Thuy (water), Moc (wood), Hoa (fire) and Tho (earth).We explored  some caves and several Buddhist sanctuaries.

It was a lovely few hours walking round and seeing the caves and pagadas, we didn't see them all as I suspect that would have taken a number of hours and it was too hot. There is a lot of stairs and I would advise shoes with a grip as the floor is not and slippery in places. The heaven vantage point involved a lot more steep stairs and then climbing up over large marble boulder type things of which did not feel at all safe, apparently it was worth it for the view but I safely waited just below which was as comfortable as I felt climbing too.

A must visit place, you just pay a very small entrance fee and you can really enjoy an amazing experience.  I have spend here almost 4 hours. Many nice caves and views. Just one thing, it's not worth taking the lift at all! Long waiting times and the stairs to where the lift will take you are very ok.
Mit der combo Tour unbedingt buchen! Super Gaudi auf dem Lake und night market.
Мраморные горы - это комплекс из пяти холмов. На вершину можно подняться по ступенькам или на фуникулере. Там находится гроты/пещеры и множество пагод.

This was an unexpected pleasure. The trick is to skip the elevator and go in through gate 1. Skip the touristy shops below and take a photo of the map before going up. It’s beautiful views, old pagodas and amazing limestone temple caves. When we first went up we thought it was just an old temple but the paths to the caves are behind it. You could spend a couple of hours exploring all of it, or an hour to see a handful and get the feel. It was confusing down below but some helpful ladies pointed us towards the gate 1 steps. The elevator puts you too far over - the gate one steps puts you right in the middle of most of the stuff. I think the bad reviews must come from people who didn’t discover all there is to see. We loved it.

Climb the 300+ steps (or take the elevator) and be prepared for an incredible array of caves, many of which contain shrines. Some of these, spectacular on their own merit. It also affords visitors a variety of opportunities of views of the surrounding district.

Saya sangat kagum dengan keindahan pemandangan di Ngũ Hành Sơn atau Marble Mountains. Kawasannya sangat unik kerana terdapat tokong dan pagoda yang dibina di atas serta di dalam gua-gua gunung yang misteri. Suasana di sini terasa sangat suci dan tenang, terutamanya apabila melihat seni bina lama yang sebati dengan alam semula jadi. Selain mendaki, kawasan ini juga terkenal dengan perkampungan ukiran batu tradisional yang sangat mahsyur di tengah Vietnam. Ia merupakan destinasi yang sangat menarik untuk dikunjungi bersama keluarga dan anak-anak. Namun, pastikan anak-anak sudah boleh berjalan sendiri kerana mendaki tangga batu agak mencabar jika perlu mendukung bayi. Pengalaman melihat hasil kraf tangan batu marmar yang halus di sini memang luar biasa. Secara keseluruhan, ia adalah gabungan sejarah, budaya, dan alam yang sangat mantap!

Another must-see when visiting Da Nang or even as a day trip from Hoi An. You can take the elevator most of the way up, which saves you from the toughest climb. We chose the stairs instead — doable, but definitely not for the faint of heart. Once at the top, you’ll find an interesting mix of caves, pagodas, and statues scattered around the mountain, with some great viewpoints along the way. It’s easy to spend more time here than expected, so don’t forget to bring water, especially in the heat.

This was not part of our tour but we included this in the end and I am happy we did it. It was worth a visit it is a mountain which holds beautiful temples in some caves as will.There is a bit of walking but with it.Highly recommend

An out of the world experience which includes lots of leg exercises - climbing up and down steps. Highly recommended to (1) stop at gate 2 (or gate b according to Grab) to take the elevator to save some energy, (2) wear comfortable walking shoes, dress in airy clothes that allows you at times to climb rocky steps without railings, (3) bring a towel (as there will be quite a lot of sweating), (4) water, (5) snacks. There are a couple of snack shops at the marble mountains for drinks and simple snacks though.

Мраморные горы - одна из главных достопримечательностей Дананга, и посетить их точно стоит. Добиралась туда на такси, дорога недолгая. Входной билет стоит 40 000 донгов, и при покупке дают набор открыток. На территории расположен комплекс пещер с красивыми панорамами, несколько храмов, сувенирные лавки и закусочные. Место большое, интересное и насыщенное. Для прогулки лучше надеть удобную обувь - предстоит много подниматься по лестницам и проходить узкие участки в пещерах. Есть лифт, который поднимает наверх за 15 000 донгов, но дальше всё равно нужно идти по ступеням. Лестницы местами выглядят небезопасно, местами встречаются крутые подъёмы - лучше быть внимательным. В пещерах душно, людей много, но виды компенсируют неудобства. После подъёма на лифте можно увидеть множество красивых храмов - оттуда тоже открываются отличные виды. Поэтому, если не хочется лазать в пещеры, можно ограничиться этой частью маршрута - впечатлений всё равно будет достаточно. Территория ухоженная, приятно гулять, много зелени. Чувствуется спокойная, умиротворяющая атмосфера. Отличное место, чтобы провести пару часов и увидеть город с другой стороны.

We chose the stairs instead — doable, but definitely not for the faint of heart. Once at the top, you’ll find an interesting mix of caves, pagodas, and statues scattered around the mountain, with some great viewpoints along the way. It’s easy to spend more time here than expected, so don’t forget to bring water, especially in the heat.

This was not part of our tour but we included this in the end and I am happy we did it. It was worth a visit it is a mountain which holds beautiful temples in some caves as will.There is a bit of walking but with it.Highly recommend

There are a couple of snack shops at the marble mountains for drinks and simple snacks though.
